content.text: Let me read the primary target files in parallel.

content.content:
1	# Fleet daily maintenance routine (the single scheduled entry point)
2	
3	A portable, **project-agnostic** spec for the **one** daily routine that maintains **every repo the owner has opted into Claudinite** — scheduled once, from the canon repo, and reaching all the others. It carries no maintenance logic of its own. It is a thin **orchestrator** that does two things: **(1)** run the **planner** logic — a pack-agnostic walk over the repos it was handed that decides *what work each repo needs* in code and emits the plan — then **(2)** dispatch each planned unit as an isolated worker **at the tier its `smarts` names**. Every unit is independent — they run concurrently, nothing waits on anything else. The point is twofold: the owner registers **a single schedule** for **all** their repos, and every unit is **guaranteed to run** because each is its own isolated subagent (or code action) — one failing, stalling, or exiting early cannot stop the others. The "should I run" decision is **code** (the planner's gates), never this routine's judgment.
4	
5	Two deterministic **migration passes** bracket that pack work: **before** any pack runs, the migration **apply pass** brings every covered member to the current canonical shape; **after** every unit settles, the migration **retire pass** finalizes (retires) any migration the fleet has fully applied and left quiet for a cycle. Both are pure decision logic (`smarts: none`) the orchestrator runs directly: the orchestrator **passes them this session's scoped repos** and the MCP-backed `gh` reader/writer, and they act only on those repos. The migrations flow owns them ([../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs](../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs), [../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs](../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs)); the routine only sequences them around the plan, adding no reasoning of its own. Retirement targets the **canon repo the migrations live in** — the same place they are loaded from, not a separately-discovered "home".
6	
7	This routine **replaces** scheduling anything individually **and** any per-repo maintenance schedule: schedule *this* one, in the canon repo, and nothing else. Do **not** also schedule the planner, a pack's task, or a per-repo routine. The planner and each step's spec stay exactly as written — this routine only dispatches the plan the planner emits, concurrently, in isolation.
8	
9	## Conventions used in this doc
10	
11	- **Canon repo.** This routine is scheduled from, keeps its tracking issue in, and ships its migrations from a single fixed **canon repo** — the repo where you vendor and schedule this doc (for the reference deployment, that's Claudinite itself). "Canon repo" below means that repo; every *other* repo it maintains is a **member repo**. It is a known constant the orchestrator names — nothing is discovered from a CI env var.
12	- **Default branch.** Each member repo's own default branch is whatever *that* repo uses; the plan and workers substitute it per repo, so this routine never assumes `main` for a member repo.
13	- **GitHub access is MCP-native — no REST client, no token.** This routine, its `smarts: none` code steps, and its workers all reach GitHub **only** through the session's **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`). The orchestrator knows the repos it manages from **this session's granted scope** — it does not enumerate GitHub — and backs the code steps' injected I/O with those MCP tools. There is no `GITHUB_TOKEN`, no `gh`/`curl`, and no raw `fetch` to `api.github.com` anywhere in this process: in a scheduled web session the shell has no GitHub REST credential, and cross-repo `git` checkout is impossible, so MCP is the whole surface. Two injected shapes, both the orchestrator's to fulfill over MCP:
14	  - The **planner** reads through a read-only `gh(path) -> { status, json }` — each path a `list_*` / `get_file_contents` call.
15	  - The **migration passes** take a semantic **`io`** object whose methods map **1:1 to MCP write/read tools**, so no Git-Data-API shapes leak into the code: `io.getDefaultBranch` (get repo), `io.read` (`get_file_contents`), `io.ensureBranch` (`create_branch`), `io.commit` (`push_files`), `io.remove` (`delete_file`), `io.hasOpenPr` (`list_pull_requests`), `io.openPr` (`create_pull_request`), `io.openIssue` (`issue_write`), and the optional `io.updateBranchFromBase` (`update_pull_request_branch` on the branch's open PR; throws on conflict — the pr delivery lane uses it to refresh the maintenance branch from base each night, #332). Writes land as one `push_files` commit; a rare file-move's delete follows via `delete_file` (MCP has no atomic multi-file+delete — no active migration produces a delete).
16	
17	## What the planner decides, what this routine dispatches
18	
19	The **planner** ([fleet/DESIGN.md](fleet/DESIGN.md), [fleet/plan.mjs](fleet/plan.mjs)) is **pack-agnostic decision logic**: it goes over the repos it was handed — every covered member, plus the **canon repo itself, planned last** with the fleet aggregate its home-only packs' gates need — and for each assembles the maintenance actions its declared packs contribute (their `run_daily` tasks), runs each action's **"should I run" gate** (pure code), and emits the day's **work plan** — a flat list of `(repo, task, worker, targets, reason, order, smarts)` **units**, one per `(repo, task)` a gate marked live. A repo with nothing to do yields no units, at an MCP-read's cost. **The planner never dispatches or depends on any one pack's workflow** — every unit stands alone, so an un-set-up or failing pack surfaces as one isolated unit (a baselining fix), never a reason the plan can't be built. This routine runs the planner over the repos it was handed, reads its units, and dispatches them; it decides *nothing* itself.
20	
21	The units span every kind of maintenance, each deferring wholly to its own doc:
22	
23	- **Growth** (the growth pack owns the lifecycle narrative) — the member-side tasks (`growth-extract-new-instructions`, `growth-dedup-local-instructions`, `growth-discover-packs`, `conversation-extract`) on every repo declaring the growth pack, and the central `growth-promote-to-claudinite` on the canon repo via its home-only curation pack. **All ordinary, independent planned units** — no phases, no barriers, no bespoke central step: each stage reads only what's already merged, so extract→promote→dedup propagates across successive nightly runs (and promote's PR approval was always the dominant latency). `conversation-extract` is the conversation-side sibling of `growth-extract-new-instructions` — same access model (local git on the repo's working tree, MCP only for the issue API), mining the repo's captured `conversation-logs` branch instead of its issues/PRs/commits.
24	- **The repo-tidying pack** — the single `repo-tidy` task (assess branches/PRs read-only, act on issues, reconcile the standing tracker, in one pass) on every repo declaring the pack, and against the canon repo.
25	- **`baselining`** (the baseline pack's task — the member re-run of the idempotent bootstrap + check-alignment); a handed repo that isn't covered yet gets its **first baseline** (adoption) from the same worker.
26	- **Any pack task** — e.g. `chrome-store-release`. New maintenance is a new pack `(gate, worker)` pair; the plan picks it up automatically — **no edit here**.
27	
28	## Step 0 — apply migrations across the fleet (before any pack work)
29	
30	Before the planner runs, bring every managed repo to the current canonical shape: run the migration **apply pass** ([../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs](../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs))'s `applyToRepos(io, repos, migrations)` over this session's scoped repos, with the MCP-backed `io` object and the migration set (loaded via `migrations/registry.mjs`). It computes each member's pending migration writes — the declared file renames, template materializations, and ref rewrites — and lands them as **one commit** through `io.commit` (`push_files`), honoring the member's `push`/`pr` delivery. It is **pure decision logic (`smarts: none`** — no subagent) and idempotent: a member already on the canonical shape gets no commit. Running migrations *first* means the pack tasks (baselining's check-alignment included) evaluate the already-migrated shape. It **returns the ids it applied this cycle** — hold that set in hand and pass it to **Step 3** as its quiescence evidence (no intermediate file).
31	
32	## Step 1 — run the planner over the handed repos
33	
34	The orchestrator already knows the repos it manages — **this session's granted scope** — so run the **planner** ([fleet/plan.mjs](fleet/plan.mjs))'s `planGivenRepos(gh, canonRepo, repos)` over them with the MCP-backed `gh`, and read the returned plan. For each repo it assembles the declared packs' `run_daily` tasks ([fleet/registry.mjs](fleet/registry.mjs)) and runs each task's **gate** ([fleet/gates.mjs](fleet/gates.mjs) — pure "should I run" code) over the repo's signals ([fleet/signals.mjs](fleet/signals.mjs)). The result is the unit list — `buildWorkPlan` *is* exactly this loop over the repos you hand it. **The worklist is decided by code, not by this routine's reasoning.**
35	
36	**Local-pack tasks are planned by default.** A member's canon-pack tasks are on disk in the canon checkout; its **own local packs** (`.claudinite/local_packs/`) contribute `run_daily` tasks the same way, read from the member repo by `readLocalTasks(gh, fullName)` ([fleet/local-tasks.mjs](fleet/local-tasks.mjs)) — the planner's **default** `localTasksFor`, so `planGivenRepos(gh, canonRepo, repos)` plans them with no extra wiring. A local task is gated by the member's own declaration exactly like a canon task, and the canon home's curation tasks ride this path nightly. The reader's header states the single-owner-trust contract it runs under; a task shadowing a canon pack id is never planned (canon wins).
37	
38	If the planner logic itself **fails** (the gate code errors across the board), or its `errors` show probes that couldn't cover repos you *can* reach, you **cannot guarantee coverage** — log it (see Tracking) and dispatch whatever plan you have. But a **broken or un-set-up pack is not that failure**: it surfaces as an isolated adoption/baselining unit for that one repo (which its own worker fixes), never as a missing plan. Only a genuine planner-code failure is a no-op day, logged.
39	
40	## Step 2 — dispatch the units, at their tier
41	
42	Run each unit's **worker** at the tier its `smarts` names: a subagent on the matching capability tier for `high` / `medium` / `low`, or, for **`none`**, a **direct code / tool execution** with no subagent. Dispatch subagents with the **Agent/Task tool, never the multi-agent-orchestration Workflow tool** — its mandatory interactive opt-in stalls an unattended run on step one, waiting for an "Allow" no one is there to click. Each worker runs **exactly per its own doc**, handed the unit's `targets`; this routine adds **no** behavior. Read the worker doc from the right repo: a canon-pack unit's `worker` is a canon-relative path read from the canon checkout (`workerRepo` null); a **local-pack** unit carries `workerRepo` — the member repo — and a member-relative `worker` path, read from the member over MCP.
43	
44	**Pass the plan's gathered enumeration through — don't make the worker re-fetch it.** The planner already enumerated each repo's touched branches / PRs / issues to build the unit (they're in the unit's `targets`, and the worker docs already say "the plan hands you the list — you don't enumerate"). So hand the worker that enumeration and let it start from there; **do not re-run `list_branches` / `list_pull_requests` / `list_issues` in the dispatch, and do not instruct the worker to re-enumerate** what the plan already holds — a worker fetches only the per-item detail it needs to verify (e.g. a branch's landed status against the default branch). Re-gathering the same lists the planner just fetched is pure duplicated cost and adds nothing; the plan is the single source of the worklist.
45	
46	Concurrency — every unit is independent:
47	
48	- **All units run concurrently**, capped to a sane batch. There is **no ordering and no barrier of any kind** — not per-repo, not fleet-wide. The repo tidy-up is a single `repo-tidy` unit whose worker does assess-branches → assess-PRs → triage-issues → reconcile-the-tracker in one pass, so that sequencing lives inside the one worker, never in the plan; nothing else waits on anything.
49	- **Await async downstream** — a unit that triggers a dispatch-only Action (a pack task's) is done only when that Action **completes**, not at the trigger; poll it (report at completion, not the trigger).
50	
51	The subagent boundary delivers the guarantees the owner cares about: **failure isolation** (a unit that errors, stalls, or exits early fails *its own* subagent only), **context isolation**, and **behavior unchanged**. Also run the tidy tasks against the **canon repo** (it doesn't declare the tidy pack — its declaration carries only its home-only packs and the checks it runs on itself — but its PRs/branches/issues still need tending). Cap concurrency if the fleet is large, but **every** unit must be launched **and** waited on — a launched-but-unwaited unit is not a guaranteed unit. Wait for **everything** to settle before finishing.
52	
53	## Step 3 — finalize migrations (retire)
54	
55	Only after **every** unit has settled, run the migration **retire pass** ([../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs](../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs))'s `runRetirement(io, canonRepo, migrations, repos, unknownCount, today, appliedThisCycle)` over the **same** scoped repo list — it must be the COMPLETE managed set, since retirement fires only when the whole set is clean, so a missing repo could hide drift. Pass it Step 0's returned `appliedThisCycle` set directly (in memory) and the canon repo — the same repo the migrations were loaded from, where the deletes land. It probes each migration's fleet-wide completion via `io.read`, and for a migration the whole fleet has applied **and** that Step 0 touched on **no** repo this cycle (the **quiescence guard**: if that evidence is absent it retires **nothing**), it **stages the deletions** — the record and any files it relocated into the consumers — onto a stable retire branch and opens **one PR** (`io.ensureBranch` → `io.remove` on that branch → `io.openPr`, idempotent via `io.hasOpenPr`). Retirement is **irreversible**, so it is **never pushed to the canon's default branch**: the PR routes it through the canon's own CI (`barrier`, `reference-integrity`, the test suite), which validates that the deletion strands no inline reference before it can land — a clean retirement is a green, mergeable PR (never auto-merged); a stranding one is a red PR held for cleanup, so `main` never breaks. It runs **last**, on a fleet proven both converged and quiet, and it writes only to the canon repo. It is **pure decision logic (`smarts: none`)**.
56	
57	## What the orchestrator itself must not do
58	
59	The orchestrator is a sequencer, not a maintainer. It **never** decides the worklist by its own reasoning — the "should I run" gate is code (the planner); the orchestrator *runs* that code and reads its units, it doesn't reason its way to a plan. And it **never** runs a *worker's* logic, merges, commits docs by its own judgment, edits the canon, or writes to a step's tracking issue — every such action belongs to the worker (or the deterministic pass) that owns it. Running the plan's **code steps** — the planner and the migration apply/retire passes (all `smarts: none`, performing their reads/writes to members and the canon repo through the MCP `gh`) — is not the orchestrator reasoning; its *only* judgment-driven write is the failure log on its **own** tracking issue in the canon repo (next section). Keep it that thin.
60	
61	## Tracking: log only failures, on the canon repo's own issue
62	
63	This routine keeps its **own** standing tracking issue **in the canon repo**, separate from every step's — found **by title**, never a hard-coded number. Open it if it doesn't exist; reopen it if it was closed while a run still needs logging.
64	
65	It logs **failures only** — the job is to guarantee every planned unit *ran* where it should, so the one thing worth surfacing is a run that **didn't** complete:
66	
67	- **The planner run failed / the plan couldn't cover the fleet**, or **any worker failed, stalled, or exited without completing** (name the repo and task) → post a **dated comment** naming what was affected and the symptom.
68	- **Every planned unit completed where it should** (whether it changed anything or correctly did nothing) → **log nothing.** Workers already log their own *changes* to their own issues; a fleet-wide "all green" roll-up here would just be noise.
69	
70	So on a normal day this routine writes nothing at all, and the only entries that accumulate are the failures you actually need to see.
71	
72	## The launcher (Claude Code routine)
73	
74	Keep the routine's config a **thin pointer** to this doc, not an inlined copy. Vendor this file (and the [fleet/](fleet/DESIGN.md) specs) in your canon repo, then schedule **this one routine** daily, pasting a prompt like the following and substituting the path where you vendored these docs:
75	
76	> Run the fleet daily maintenance routine exactly as specified in `<path/to/routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md>`. Work **entirely through the GitHub MCP tools** — there is no `GITHUB_TOKEN` and no REST client. Back the code steps' injected I/O with those MCP tools: a read-only `gh(path)` for the planner (each path a `list_*`/`get_file_contents` call), and a semantic `io` object for the migration passes whose methods map 1:1 to the MCP tools (`io.read`→`get_file_contents`, `io.commit`→`push_files`, `io.remove`→`delete_file`, `io.ensureBranch`→`create_branch`, `io.hasOpenPr`→`list_pull_requests`, `io.openPr`→`create_pull_request`, `io.openIssue`→`issue_write`, `io.getDefaultBranch`→get repo, `io.updateBranchFromBase`→`update_pull_request_branch` on the branch's open PR). **The repos you manage are this session's granted scope** — you already know them, so do not enumerate GitHub. Name the **canon repo** (a known constant — the repo this doc and the migrations ship from). **Apply migrations**: run the apply pass's `applyToRepos(io, repos, migrations)` (migrations loaded via `migrations/registry.mjs`) to bring every managed repo to the canonical shape before any pack work — it lands each repo's pending writes as one commit honoring `push`/`pr` delivery, and **returns the ids it applied this cycle**; keep that set in hand for the retire step. **Run the planner**: run `planGivenRepos(gh, canonRepo, repos)` — it decides every unit in code (pack-agnostically; a broken pack is one isolated unit, not a missing plan); you do not decide the worklist by your own reasoning. Local-pack daily tasks are planned by default — the planner reads each member's own `.claudinite/local_packs/` descriptors over `gh` (Step 1) — so pass nothing extra. **Then dispatch each unit** as an isolated **plain subagent (never the Workflow orchestration tool, whose interactive opt-in an unattended routine can't answer)** at the tier its `smarts` names — or, for `smarts: none`, as a direct code/tool action, no subagent — running its `worker` doc (canon-relative, from the canon checkout) against its repo with the unit's `targets`. Every unit is independent: run them concurrently (capped to a sane batch) — there is no ordering and no barrier of any kind (the repo tidy-up is a single `repo-tidy` unit that sequences its own pass internally). Run the tidy task against the canon repo too. Await any dispatched Action to completion, not to its trigger. Once every unit has settled, **finalize migrations**: run `runRetirement(io, canonRepo, migrations, repos, unknownCount, today, appliedThisCycle)` with the same repo list and the applied-ids set from the apply step, to retire any migration the fleet has fully applied AND left untouched this cycle (it retires nothing if that evidence is absent). Retirement is **never pushed to the canon's default branch** — the pass stages its irreversible deletes onto a retire branch and opens one CI-gated PR (never auto-merged), so the canon's own checks validate reference-safety before it lands. Wait for everything to settle. You are a sequencer only: never decide the worklist by your own reasoning, never run a worker's logic, commit docs, edit the canon, or write to any step's tracking issue. Log **only failures** — the planner code failing, a plan that couldn't cover the reachable fleet, or a unit that didn't complete — to this routine's own standing tracking issue in the canon repo (found **by title**), naming the repo and task affected; on a clean day, log nothing.
77	
78	Schedule it daily as a **Claude Code routine** (the Routines UI, or any scheduler that launches a Claude Code session — the steps need the session's MCP GitHub tools), from the canon repo. Every pack-task Action stays `workflow_dispatch`-only; this routine is the one schedule ([fleet/scheduling.md](fleet/scheduling.md)).
79	
80	## Run on a capable model
81	
82	The workers make **judgment calls** — each unit's `smarts` names the tier its worker needs. This routine's own step (run the planner, read its units, fan out at each unit's tier) is mechanical, but it drives those judgment-heavy workers, so run this routine — and therefore its subagents — on a capable model.
83	
84	## What this routine must never do
85	
86	- **Never decide the worklist by the orchestrator's own reasoning** — run the planner (the gate code) over the repos it was handed and read its units; the "should I run" gate is code, not a judgment call.
87	- **Never run a worker's logic itself** — it dispatches units into subagents (or runs the `none`-tier code action); it does not merge, commit, edit docs, or open any worker's output except its own failure log.
88	- **Never fan out through the Workflow tool** — dispatch runs as plain subagents (Agent/Task). Its mandatory interactive opt-in has no one to answer it in an unattended routine, so it stalls the whole run on step one.
89	- **Never let one unit's failure block another** — each is its own isolated subagent (or code action), and every unit is launched and waited on regardless of how the others fare.
90	- **Never artificially sequence units** — every unit is independent and runs concurrently; there is no ordering or barrier to impose.
91	- **Never also schedule the planner, a pack's task, or a per-repo maintenance routine** — this routine is their single schedule across the whole fleet.
92	- **Never log on a clean day** — it logs only failures to its own canon-repo tracking issue.
93	- **Never inline this spec, a worker's spec, or the plan into the launcher** — the launcher stays a thin pointer here.
94

input.prompt:
I'm auditing the Claudinite corpus (a repo at /home/user/Claudinite) to find EVERY place where its instructions cause an agent to write to a git repository — specifically anything that pushes to `main`, opens a PR, merges a PR, force-pushes, commits directly, or creates/pushes branches.

Focus ONLY on these files (the daily-task workers and the growth lifecycle):
- /home/user/Claudinite/packs/basics/run_daily/baselining.worker.md
- /home/user/Claudinite/packs/product-wiki/run_daily/wiki-growth.worker.md
- /home/user/Claudinite/packs/tidy-repo/run_daily/repo-tidy.worker.md
- /home/user/Claudinite/packs/chrome-extension-release/run_daily/store-release.worker.md
- All files under /home/user/Claudinite/packs/grow_with_claudinite/ (README.md, extract.md, dedup.md, conversation-extract.md, discover-packs.md, extracting-lessons.md, and any .mjs scripts like capture-log.mjs — read the .mjs for what branch it pushes to)

Read them fully. For each distinct repo-mutating operation you find, report:
1. A short name for the operation
2. The file + line reference where it's described
3. What it writes (files, config, extracted rules, conversation logs, version bumps, etc.)
4. WHERE it lands: directly on `main`? on a feature branch? on an orphan branch (e.g. conversation-logs)? via an open PR waiting for human approval? via a PR that gets auto-merged? Quote the exact phrasing that establishes this.
5. Whether it runs interactively (a human is present) or unattended (scheduled/automated fleet run).

Be exhaustive and precise — quote the exact sentences that describe git push/commit/merge/PR behavior. I need to know the CURRENT behavior verbatim so I can decide what to change. Do NOT edit anything. Report findings as a structured list.

attachment.content:
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use only when the user explicitly asks to run, see, or set up their morning brief, or if they invoke /morning by name. A question about their day, schedule, or calendar is not by itself a request for the brief; answer it directly instead.
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- session-start-hook: Creating and developing startup hooks for Claude Code on the web. Use when the user wants to set up a repository for Claude Code on the web, create a SessionStart hook to ensure their project can run tests and linters during web sessions.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- adopt-claudinite: Bootstrap Claudinite into a consuming repo — mount, hooks, checks, skills. Use when asked to bootstrap, adopt, or set up Claudinite, or to baseline a repo to pick up updates.
- authoring-agent-docs: How to write instruction files coding agents follow reliably. Use before writing or editing any Claude instruction doc — a project CLAUDE.md, a convention doc, a routine spec.
- bug-investigation: Method for investigating a bug and pinning down its root cause. Use when investigating a bug report, when a fix didn't hold or a bug recurs, or when a report doesn't reproduce against main.
- bump-version: Raise the project's version — a minor bump by default. Use when the owner says "bump version".
- engineering-practices: General software-engineering practices — naming by scope, single-source-of-truth with drift guards, GENERATED-file discipline, earning each dependency, verifying real platform behavior. Use before writing or editing code.
- file-placement: Where a file should live — the reference-distance metric, the high-reach code smell, and the mandated-location and test-location exemptions. Use before placing, moving, or renaming a file, or when reviewing where one lives.
- generate-project-instructions: Decompose a project into its pack facets (working-style class, technology, aspect, domain) and extract its working instructions into reusable canon packs plus the project's own local packs (.claudinite/local_packs/). Use when a project has no established working style yet, or it exhibits a facet no pack covers — during bootstrap of a fresh/empty repo, or on an uplevel request.
- git-github-advanced: Git/GitHub procedures beyond the baseline lifecycle. Use for commit layering, recovering a branch after a squash-merge, CI-trigger rules, GitHub Actions gotchas, or merge-relocation traps.
- merge-to-main: Merge the change in front of the owner into main. Use when the owner says "LGTM" or asks to merge/land the current branch or PR into main.
- prose-to-checks: Mine the corpus's existing prose (pack RULES.md, skill SKILL.md) for always-testable rules that were never converted to checks, and convert the strongest ones. Use when auditing the corpus for convertible rules, or when the nightly growth routine runs its prose-to-checks sweep.
- repo-text-sweeps: Mechanics for grep/sed sweeps, renames, and path relocations across a repo. Use before a bulk find-replace, a rename, or moving files — and after one, to catch silently broken references.
- unattended-agents: Architecture and practices for unattended, automation-invoked agents and recurring routines. Use when building, structuring, or running an AI agent, a scheduled routine, or a multi-stage agent pipeline.
- writing-claudinite-skills: Authoring or changing a skill in the Claudinite corpus — SKILL.md conventions, where a skill's checks and tests live, catalog and pack wiring. Use when creating or editing skills/<name>/ in the canon; a consumer project's own agent docs are authoring-agent-docs' turf.
- writing-tests: Practices for writing tests you can trust. Use before writing or changing any test — see-it-fail discipline, snapshot/golden rules, CI-only and heavy-browser tests, fuzzy-metric gating.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# How scheduled work runs in the fleet — one scheduler
2	
3	The **fleet daily maintenance routine** ([../auto-all-repos-maintenance.md](../auto-all-repos-maintenance.md))
4	is the **only** schedule. Everything else that runs on a cadence is a **`workflow_dispatch`-only
5	executor** the routine triggers and awaits — never a thing with a cron of its own.
6	
7	This is an agent-practices rule, stated once here as the fleet's home for it:
8	
9	> Give an executor a cron and it silently becomes a **second orchestrator with a competing trigger**.
10	> One schedule, owned by the orchestrating routine; executors run only when dispatched.
11	> — the agent-practices skill
12	
13	So a **future daily / pack-based routine does not self-schedule.** In particular:
14	
15	- The **fleet bootstrap sweep** — sequenced by the routine, never scheduled.
16	- **Every pack task's supporting GitHub Action** — e.g. `chrome-store-release`'s `Release to Chrome
17	  Store` workflow. A pack task that needs an Action declares it `workflow_dispatch`-only; the task's
18	  worker triggers it and awaits its completion (report at completion, not at the trigger — the
19	  async-completion rule).
20	
21	The fleet routine itself is scheduled **externally** (the Claude Code Routines UI or a cron on a home
22	repo), so no workflow *in* a repo — canon or consumer — carries a `schedule:` trigger for Claudinite
23	executor work. A pack-task or fleet-executor workflow that grows a `schedule:` trigger is a
24	conformance violation (enforced by a workflow-lint pack check), not just a review note.
25

1	# routines/fleet/ — the code planner for the daily maintenance routine
2	
3	> **Status: Stages 1 and 2 implemented** (issue #241). Stage 1 — the engine (`registry.mjs`,
4	> `gates.mjs`, `signals.mjs`, `schedule.mjs`, `tasks/`), the plan wired into the census walk, the
5	> seed-migration mechanism that backfills a default-on pack into the existing fleet, an example pack task,
6	> `scheduling.md` + its enforcing check, and the plan-driven orchestrator. Stage 2 — single-object
7	> worker skills and the single per-repo `repo-tidy` unit (assess branches/PRs, act on issues, reconcile
8	> the tracker in one pass), with the standalone tidy routine retired into the pack that owns it. All
9	> built and tested.
10	
11	The fleet daily maintenance routine ([../auto-all-repos-maintenance.md](../auto-all-repos-maintenance.md))
12	today fans an agent out to **every task × every member repo** every night, and decides *what to do*
13	in-session. Most repos have nothing to do for most tasks on most nights, so the expensive tier — a
14	model turn — is entered to discover "nothing to do." This engine moves that discovery into
15	**deterministic code** and lets agents run only the work a gate already proved is live.
16	
17	## The one boundary: code decides, agents act
18	
19	Every maintenance task is split into two halves that never mix:
20	
21	- **"should I run" — the gate.** Code. Runs in the planner, over the run's own token.
22	  Given a repo + the signal bundle, returns `{ run, targets, reason }`. Costs an API read, never a
23	  token.
24	- **"what to do" — the worker.** A doc/skill a subagent runs, handed the gate's `targets`. Costs a
25	  model turn — spent only on `(repo, task)` units a gate marked live.
26	
27	The planner is the aggregate of every gate; the orchestrator dispatches every worker. **The entire
28	worklist is computed by code that runs to completion and emits a plan, before a single worker agent
29	is spawned.** That boundary is the frugality lever.
30	
31	## Process map — the orchestrator IS the routine session
32	
33	```
34	[Routine session]  (agent, capable model, home repo)          ← orchestrator, one run
35	   ├─ run the core planner over the environment's repos ─► units  (plan.mjs — pure gate code, no pack)
36	   └─ per unit (all independent) ─►  [worker subagent] × N    ← children of the session
37	```
38	
39	The planner is **pack-agnostic core code** (`plan.mjs`): the orchestrator hands it the environment's
40	repos — for each, assemble its declared packs' `run_daily` gates, run them, emit units. It never
41	enumerates GitHub, and it depends on **no** pack. An enforcer pack's coverage census (the account-spanning
42	audit that needs the fleet PAT) is a **separate, isolated** concern with its own dispatch — it never
43	gates the plan, so a missing or broken enforcer pack can't stop the day's work from being planned over
44	the repos already reachable. One supervisor owns the run (and the failure log).
45	
46	## The task registry
47	
48	A task descriptor is a plain object:
49	
50	```js
51	{
52	  id:     'repo-tidy',                                   // stable task name
53	  scope:  'pack:<name>',                                 // 'fleet' | 'pack:<name>' (implicit for pack tasks)
54	  worker: 'packs/<name>/maintenance/<task>.worker.md',   // the "what to do" doc, canon-relative
55	  full_sweep_supported: true,                            // has a distinct weekly/full mode? (see below)
56	  smarts: 'medium',                                      // judgment the WORKER needs: 'high'|'medium'|'low'|'none' (see below)
57	  gate:   async (repo, signals, gh) => ({ run, targets, reason }),  // the "should I run" predicate
58	}
59	```
60	
61	**The `full_sweep_supported` capability — declared, so the engine knows whether a weekly pass is even
62	meaningful.** Only some tasks have a *distinct* full mode: `repo-tidy` in full re-examines **all** open
63	branches / PRs / issues (not just the touched ones), `baselining` in full re-runs the bootstrap + the
64	whole check suite to catch member-side drift the canon diff can't see. Others have none — a task that either fires or
65	doesn't has nothing "fuller" to do. So each descriptor **declares `full_sweep_supported`**: on a
66	repo's `fullSweep` night the engine runs the full pass **only** of the tasks that declare
67	`full_sweep_supported: true` (it invokes their gate in full mode — the exhaustive candidate set); a
68	task with it omitted/false just runs its normal incremental gate, and `fullSweep` is a no-op for it.
69	This keeps the weekly sweep from *attempting* a full action a task doesn't have.
70	
71	**The `smarts` level — declare the judgment the worker needs, and drive it down.** The gate is *always*
72	code (deciding "should I run" costs no model); `smarts` governs only the **worker** ("what to do"):
73	
74	- `high` / `medium` / `low` → the worker subagent runs on a **descending capability tier**. The
75	  descriptor names the *need*, not a model — the engine resolves level → tier at dispatch, so the
76	  model mapping lives in **one** place and can change without touching a single task. (The current
77	  mapping is three tiers, most-capable → least; the tier names stay out of the descriptors on purpose.)
78	- `none` → **no agent at all.** The engine runs the worker as a direct **code / tool execution**, no
79	  subagent spawned. A `none` task is code end-to-end — gate *and* worker.
80	
81	**Lowering a task's `smarts` is the standing goal** — push mechanical judgment out of the worker and
82	into code, tier by tier, exactly as the planner already did for the "should I run" half
83	(the agent-practices rule: "match the agent model to the judgment it must make," and "hard-code the
84	deterministic parts … push that boundary continually"). **`none` — code only — is the best version of
85	any task**; every tier down is a win, in cost and in reliability (a cheaper deterministic path can't
86	hallucinate the judgment a model tier might fumble). So `smarts` is not a fixed property but a *current
87	high-water mark* each task is meant to descend.
88	
89	- **Every task is a pack task** — there is no fleet-core category (`registry.mjs`): a task is active
90	  exactly where its pack is declared. "Fleet-universal" is achieved by riding a universally-declared
91	  pack: **`baselining`** rides the baseline pack — restore the member to the current
92	    canonical baseline — re-run the idempotent bootstrap to refresh the mount + wiring, and evaluate the
93	    member against its declared packs' *current* checks (the same engine its Stop hook and CI run),
94	    applying each failing check's own `fix` and opening a member-side issue for any finding needing
95	    judgment. Incrementally gated by `canonChanged` (the canon shipped new checks/wiring →
96	    propagate them). Its **full** mode baselines regardless, catching member-side drift the canon diff
97	    can't see. (The census/adoption half of the sweep stays in the census executor, not this task.)
98	    Its gate self-skips the home repo — the canon doesn't mount itself.
99	  The growth lifecycle's member-side tasks ride the growth pack; its central promote stage
100	  rides a home-only curation pack (see the home-repo paragraph under
101	  [Where it lives](#where-it-lives--the-planner-is-core-the-census-is-the-enforcer-packs)) — an
102	  ordinary planned unit, **not** a bespoke orchestrator step.
103	- **Pack tasks** register exactly like a pack's checks and skills — the **`run_daily: [...]`**
104	  field on `pack.mjs`, listing descriptor modules (parallel to `rules`, `skills`, `env`). A task
105	  declared by a pack applies to a repo **iff** the
106	  repo declares that pack in `.claudinite-checks.json`. Adding a task is dropping a `(gate, worker)`
107	  pair — the engine discovers it, no orchestrator edit.
108	- **A member's own local packs contribute tasks the same way — planned by default.** A repo's
109	  `.claudinite/local_packs/` packs carry `run_daily` descriptors exactly like a canon pack, but they
110	  live in the *member* tree, not the canon checkout the planner reads. The planner's `localTasksFor`
111	  seam defaults to the real reader ([local-tasks.mjs](local-tasks.mjs)): it fetches each member's
112	  local-pack descriptors over the same MCP `gh`, imports each self-contained descriptor, and tags it
113	  with its pack and `workerRepo` so the unit's worker is read from the member. A local task is gated
114	  by the member's declaration exactly like a canon task (a descriptor shadowing a canon pack id is
115	  never planned — canon wins), and the canon home's own curation tasks ride this path nightly.
116	  Running the member's own gate code centrally is safe under the single-owner fleet model — the same
117	  trust the routine already extends to every member-authored worker. The seam stays injectable for
118	  tests, and `localTasksFor: null` disables it.
119	
120	`gates.mjs` assembles, per covered repo, `applicable = (active canon packs' tasks) ∪ (the member's
121	active local packs' tasks)`, runs each gate, and collects the units that returned `run: true`.
122	
123	## The signal bundle
124	
125	`signals.mjs` builds one bundle per covered repo (plus one global signal), from a small, bounded set
126	of cheap reads. Gates lean on it and/or do a targeted probe of their own via `gh`.
127	
128	| Signal | Source | Feeds |
129	|---|---|---|
130	| `fullSweep` | `hash(full_name) % 7 === weekdayUtc` | the full pass of every task that declares `full_sweep_supported: true` (others just run their normal gate) |
131	| `pushedAt` | repo object (already in hand from enumeration) | short-circuits the code-side probes when idle |
132	| `mainMoved` | commits on the default branch `since` the window | `projectChanged` |
133	| `projectChanged` | the default branch advanced (any commit, merges included) | the fleet aggregate |
134	| `substantiveChange` | a **non-housekeeping** default-branch commit in the window — excludes bot bumps, `[skip ci]`, and the nightly baselining/seed commits | **growth-extract-new-instructions** and **conversation-extract** (a real merge is when a fresh conversation log lands; the `[skip ci]` capture commit is excluded, so this tracks the merge not the capture); widens the **repo-tidy** landed/implemented candidate set |
135	| `prsTouched[]` | open PRs `updated_at` in window (∪ all if `substantiveChange`/`fullSweep`) | **repo-tidy** |
136	| `issuesTouched[]` | open issues `updated_at` in window (∪ all if `substantiveChange`/`fullSweep`), **excluding the routine's own standing trackers** (whose nightly self-update would otherwise re-fire tidy forever) | **repo-tidy** |
137	| `branchesTouched[]` | all open branches, only when `substantiveChange`/`fullSweep` (else empty) | **repo-tidy** |
138	| `activePacks[]` | member's `.claudinite-checks.json` | which pack tasks apply |
139	| `hasLocalPacks` | member tracks ≥1 `.claudinite/local_packs/<pack>/` subdir | gates **growth-dedup-local-instructions**; and the central **promote**'s weekly full sweep (re-promote over all with local packs) |
140	| `localPacksChanged` | a default-branch commit in the window touched `.claudinite/local_packs/` (per-commit scan, only run when `hasLocalPacks`) | the central **promote**'s daily trigger — target only members whose local packs *actually changed*, not merely have them |
141	| `canonChanged` *(global, coarse)* | home-repo commits in window touching member-affecting paths (`packs/`, `checks/`, `skills/`, `migrations/`, bootstrap wiring) — **excluding** `plan.json`, trackers, and orchestration docs | **baselining** |
142	| `relevantCanonChanged` *(per repo)* | a canon **pack this repo declares** moved, or a cross-cutting area (checks/skills/migrations/mount/bootstrap) moved | **growth-dedup-local-instructions** |
143	| `isHome`, `fleetMembers[]` *(home bundle only)* | stamped by the planner, which plans the home repo **last**: every successfully-probed member's `{ repo, activePacks, packConfigs, projectChanged, substantiveChange, hasLocalPacks, localPacksChanged }` | home-only packs' gates (e.g. the central promote gate targets members that declare the growth pack, whose **local packs changed** in the window, and whose growth entry doesn't set `config.promote: false`) |
144	
145	Three rules the table encodes, because getting them wrong defeats the purpose:
146	
147	- **The landed/implemented tests key off `substantiveChange`, not `mainMoved`.** A branch that hasn't
148	  moved in weeks becomes *superseded* the moment `main` advances past its idea; an untouched issue
149	  becomes *closeable* when a new commit implements it — so when the project genuinely moves, the
150	  candidate set widens to **all** open branches/issues. But a *housekeeping-only* move (a nightly
151	  baseline commit, a bot version bump) lands nothing and implements nothing, so it must **not** widen —
152	  otherwise every maintained-but-quiet repo is re-tidied every night against its whole backlog.
153	- **A member only re-dedups when a pack it actually mounts changed (`relevantCanonChanged`), not on any
154	  canon movement.** A change to a pack the repo doesn't declare can't newly cover its local items; and a
155	  repo with **no** local packs (`!hasLocalPacks`) has nothing to prune at all, so the unit is skipped
156	  before a subagent is ever booted.
157	- **`canonChanged` excludes the plan/tracker/orchestration artifacts.** The planner's own artifact
158	  and the routines docs must not count as canon movement, or the engine self-triggers dedup every
159	  night.
160	
161	### Two guarantees, one stateless
162	
163	The daily loop is **stateless** — a fixed ~25h lookback window (UTC; the Action runs UTC, and
164	`consumer-safe-changes.md` says normalize schedules to UTC at the door). No watermark to persist or
165	corrupt. The **weekly full sweep** (`fullSweep`) is the self-healing safety net: each repo does one
166	guaranteed full re-examination per week — of the `full_sweep_supported` tasks — staggered so ~1/7 of the fleet
167	full-sweeps each night. Anything the daily window misses (a skipped/failed night, a subtle
168	supersession, member-side drift) is caught within ≤7 days by the full pass of the task that owns it;
169	anything inside the window, immediately. `fullSweep` and `canonChanged` override `pushedAt`'s idle
170	short-circuit.
171	
172	## The plan
173	
174	`plan.json`, emitted as a workflow **artifact** (ephemeral — not committed, so no commit noise and
175	nothing for `canonChanged` to trip over) and mirrored to the step summary for humans:
176	
177	```json
178	{
179	  "generatedAt": "2026-07-12T04:00:00Z",
180	  "windowStartUtc": "2026-07-11T03:00:00Z",
181	  "weekdayUtc": 6,
182	  "canonChanged": true,
183	  "units": [
184	    { "repo": "owner/foo", "task": "repo-tidy", "worker": "packs/<tidy-pack>/run_daily/repo-tidy.worker.md",
185	      "targets": { "branches": ["feat-x"], "prs": [7], "issues": [3] }, "reason": "project changed substantively — re-check landed status", "smarts": "medium" },
186	    { "repo": "owner/foo", "task": "growth-extract-new-instructions", "worker": "packs/<growth-pack>/extract.md",
187	      "targets": {}, "reason": "project changed substantively in the window", "smarts": "high" },
188	    { "repo": "owner/foo", "task": "growth-dedup-local-instructions", "worker": "packs/<growth-pack>/dedup.md",
189	      "targets": {}, "reason": "a declared pack changed in canon — local items may now be covered", "smarts": "high" },
190	    { "repo": "owner/bar", "task": "<pack-release-task>",
191	      "worker": "packs/<pack>/maintenance/<task>.worker.md",
192	      "targets": { "unreleasedVersion": "1.4.0" }, "reason": "unreleased manifest bump", "smarts": "none" }
193	  ]
194	}
195	```
196	
197	Each unit fully specifies one dispatch: repo, worker doc, targets, and `smarts` (copied from the task
198	descriptor so the orchestrator picks the tier — or runs code — without re-loading descriptors). The
199	release unit above is `smarts: "none"`: the version comparison is already code, so the whole task runs
200	without an agent.
201	
202	## Orchestration — dispatch from the plan
203	
204	The routine session runs the core planner over the reachable fleet, reads its units, and runs one
205	worker per unit — **at the tier the unit's `smarts` names**: a subagent on the matching capability
206	tier for `high`/`medium`/`low`, or, for
207	`none`, a **direct code / tool execution** with no subagent at all. It adds **no behavior** to any
208	worker — each runs exactly per its own doc, just handed a pre-filtered target set.
209	
210	**Every unit is independent — no ordering, no barriers of any kind.** Units run concurrently, capped to
211	a sane batch. Nothing waits on anything else: the growth stages each read only what's already **merged**
212	(promote picks up tonight's extracts on its *next* run, its input list decided at plan time from the
213	`fleetMembers` aggregate), and the repo tidy-up is a single `repo-tidy` unit that assesses branches/PRs,
214	acts on issues, and reconciles the tracker in one pass — so its own dimensions-then-reconcile sequencing
215	lives inside the one worker, not in the plan. (The old per-repo `tidy-report` mini-barrier — the last
216	ordering the orchestrator honored — is gone with the merge.)
217	
218	**Await async completion, don't report at the trigger** (the async-completion rule; #140). A unit
219	that kicks off an async downstream process whose output matters is done only when that output exists,
220	not when it was triggered — the session **dispatches-and-awaits** the planner Action up front, and
221	any worker that triggers a dispatch-only Action (e.g. a release task → its release Action)
222	polls it to completion on a rolling backoff before the unit counts as finished. Reporting at the
223	trigger point would race a follow-on unit onto stale output.
224	
225	Failure logging is unchanged — the session logs only failures to its own home-repo tracker, per the
226	parent routine.
227	
228	## Scheduling — one scheduler for the whole fleet
229	
230	The fleet daily routine is the **only** schedule. Every other recurring piece — the coverage census,
231	the bootstrap sweep, and **every pack task's supporting GitHub Action** — is a `workflow_dispatch`-only
232	**executor** that the routine triggers and awaits. This is the one-scheduler rule verbatim:
233	*"give [an executor] a cron and it silently becomes a second orchestrator with a competing trigger;
234	one schedule, owned by the orchestrating routine; executors run only when dispatched."* A second cron
235	would double-run the work and race the fleet routine's dispatch.
236	
237	A release task makes this concrete: because the nightly task triggers the release flow, the
238	release Action **must not carry a `schedule:` trigger** — it is dispatch-only, fired by the pack
239	task's worker (which then awaits its completion, per the async-completion rule above).
240	
241	- **The home of the principle** is `scheduling.md` (a Stage 1 deliverable) — a thin doc stating "the fleet
242	  routine is the only schedule; every census/sweep/pack-task Action is `workflow_dispatch`-only,
243	  triggered by it," pointing to the agent-practices skill for the *why*. So a future
244	  pack-based routine has one place that tells it not to self-schedule.
245	- **Enforced, not just stated** (the promotion ladder — a check beats prose): a workflow-lint-pack
246	  check flags any workflow that both carries a `schedule:` trigger **and** is a pack-task /
247	  fleet-executor workflow (everything except the one sanctioned fleet-routine schedule). A
248	  self-scheduling pack task then fails a member's own CI, not just review here.
249	
250	## Where it lives — the planner is core, the census is the enforcer pack's
251	
252	```
253	routines/
254	  fleet/
255	    DESIGN.md                 ← this spec
256	    plan.mjs                  ← the PLANNER: over the repos the routine hands it → assemble packs'
257	                                 run_daily gates → emit units. Pack-agnostic; MCP-native (injected gh).
258	    signals.mjs               ← per-repo signal bundle + global canonChanged
259	    gates.mjs                 ← per-repo gate evaluation → plan units
260	    registry.mjs              ← per-repo assembly of its active packs' run_daily tasks
261	  scheduling.md               ← the single-scheduler contract (see Scheduling, above)
262	packs/
263	  <enforcer-pack>/            ← contributes the coverage/adoption CENSUS (a separate concern: its own
264	                                dispatch, its own account-wide REST client)
265	  <maintenance-pack>/         ← contributes run_daily: [(gate, worker), …] tasks (+ any single-object
266	                                worker skills); the engine discovers them from the active packs, naming none
267	migrations/
268	  active_migrations/          ← the migration records (specs); the mechanism reads beside them
269	    <date>-<pack>-seed.mjs    ← one-time seed of a default-on pack into the existing fleet; the retire pass auto-retires it
270	  registry.mjs                ← discovery + write ops + the retirement guard (quiescence-gated)
271	  fleet-apply.mjs             ← phase-1 APPLY pass (fleet-wide, migrations-owned)
272	  fleet-retire.mjs            ← phase-3 RETIRE pass (fleet-wide, migrations-owned)
273	```
274	
275	**Everything here operates on the repos it's given, over MCP.** The **planner** (`plan.mjs`) and the
276	migration **apply** / **retire** passes (`../../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs`, `../../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs`)
277	all operate over the repos the routine hands them (this session's scoped repos) through a single injected
278	`gh` reader/writer the orchestrator backs with its GitHub MCP tools — they never enumerate GitHub and carry
279	**no REST client or token**. The one Claudinite process that *does* need account-wide REST — enumerating
280	every owned repo to discover coverage gaps — is the enforcer pack's **census** (its own account-wide REST
281	client), a wholly separate concern in a separate repo's Action: it is not scheduled, dispatched, or
282	depended on here. (Earlier the plan was emitted *inside* the census; that coupling is exactly what let a
283	missing census workflow sink the whole plan, so the planner was split back out — held apart now by the
284	core⟂pack barrier declared in `.claudinite-checks.json`.)
285	
286	## Blast radius (it's small)
287	
288	The planner and **every gate run centrally** in the home repo's Action. No consumer executes any new
289	code. A member contributes only its existing `.claudinite-checks.json` (read for `activePacks`, never
290	written). Pack **worker docs** are canon docs the dispatched subagent reads over the API — the same
291	propagation as the growth stage docs today. The only new home-repo write is the ephemeral
292	`plan.json` artifact.
293	
294	A **maintenance pack** rides the ordinary pack channels (`consumer-safe-changes.md`): its `RULES.md`
295	loads at session start where declared and its skills mount — low blast radius, same as any pack prose.
296	Its *maintenance tasks* still execute centrally like every pack task. A default-on pack adds one
297	bootstrap-wiring change — seeded at `--init`, with the deliberate carve-out that baselining does
298	**not** backfill an opt-out — travelling the bootstrap channel to converge from every layout in the wild.
299	
300	## Verifying before the nightly does
301	
302	Per `consumer-safe-changes.md` ("verify against a real consumer before the nightly does"):
303	
304	- `signals.mjs` and `gates.mjs` are unit-tested against a fake `gh` (the census already tests its
305	  pure helpers this way).
306	- The planner runs **standalone**: `node routines/fleet/plan.mjs` emits `plan.json` over the
307	  reachable fleet without the orchestrator consuming it, so the plan can be inspected against the
308	  real fleet before the session is wired to dispatch from it.
309	
310

1	# baselining worker
2	
3	Restore the repo the plan hands you to the current canonical baseline. Works entirely over the session's
4	**GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`) — never a clone, and no shell GitHub access in the fleet run.
5	
6	First read the member's `.claudinite-checks.json` and branch on its mount shape — the `claudinite`
7	stamp is the discriminator ([mount/DESIGN.md](../../../mount/DESIGN.md)):
8	
9	- **Vendored member** (`"claudinite": { "updated": … }` present) — perform the **transactional
10	  refresh**. First **verify the checkout**: the canon checkout this session runs in must be at
11	  the canon's **remote** default-branch head (one MCP read of the head sha vs `git rev-parse
12	  HEAD`) — a lagging checkout would silently rewind the member's whole corpus, so it is this
13	  unit's failure, never a tree to converge from (#328). Then:
14	  1. **Apply the pending migration notes** — every `migrations/active_migrations/` record in the
15	     canon dated **on or after the day** of the member's `updated` stamp, oldest first
16	     (mechanical ops, plus following a note's agentic instructions where it carries them).
17	     Same-day **inclusive**, because the stamp's day can't order against a day-dated note — a
18	     note landing later on the stamp's day must still apply (#330); notes are idempotent
19	     (mechanical ops by construction, agentic instructions preconditions-first), so the
20	     re-application this admits is safe.
21	  2. **Converge `.claudinite/shared/`** to the canon head snapshot — the member's vendor set per
22	     [mount/vendor.mjs](../../../mount/vendor.mjs) (engine roots minus tests and root docs, the
23	     packs/skills machinery, the declared packs with their `requires` closure, their skills
24	     union, the corpus index), written copy-if-different **and** deleting files in `shared/` the
25	     set no longer contains. Unconditional: a member-side edit to a vendored file reverts here,
26	     visibly in the diff. Never touch `.claudinite/local_packs/` or anything outside `shared/`
27	     except what a note names.
28	  3. **Advance the stamp** — `{ "updated": "<full ISO datetime>", "ref": "<verified canon head sha>" }`
29	     — **in the same commit as steps 1–2's writes**: the stamp gates which notes apply, so it
30	     must never advance in a commit that lacks any pending note's ops (#329).
31	  If any part fails **before that commit, write nothing** — the member keeps running its old
32	  snapshot coherently, tonight's failure goes to the routine's failure log, and the next night
33	  retries from the same stamp. Also keep the fresh-path wiring converged per [bootstrap.md](../../../bootstrap.md)
34	  (hook registrations, the `@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md` import, the CI stub copy) — additive
35	  and in-place fixes only, never clobbering the member's own entries.
36	- **Pre-flip member** (no stamp) — first consult the live **flip note**
37	  (`migrations/active_migrations/*vendored-mount-flip*`): if its `flip.repos` names this member
38	  (or is `'fleet'`), perform the **conversion** by following the note's `steps` verbatim — one
39	  delivery-aware commit, transactional (any failure writes nothing). Otherwise apply **only**
40	  the legacy maintenance in bootstrap.md's
41	  [transition appendix](../../../bootstrap.md#appendix--pre-flip-members-transition-window-retiring):
42	  refresh the tracked sync hook from the canon, keep the legacy wiring/gitignore/declaration
43	  backfills converged. **Never convert a member the note doesn't name** — the pilot gate is the
44	  point; widening it is a reviewed one-line canon change, not this worker's call.
45	- **Adopt a queued repo** (a repo with an open `Adopt <owner>/<repo>` issue — the census applied
46	  every eligibility rule) — run the fresh-path bootstrap (which vendors the mount); it's a
47	  **first** adoption, so **do open** its enrollment issue, noting the owner-in-the-loop parts
48	  skipped unattended (adoption interview, project classification, cloud env setup) and anything
49	  the API couldn't do. A queued repo you can't reach stays queued — the issue is the owner's cue
50	  to grant access; log it and move on.
51	
52	Then, for a covered member (either shape):
53	
54	- **Declaration normalization** — a local pack's canonical declaration token is namespaced:
55	  `local_packs/<name>` ([packs/registry.mjs](../../registry.mjs) `declTokenFor`). Rewrite any **bare**
56	  local-pack declaration in the member's `.claudinite-checks.json` to that form: a declared id (string
57	  entry, or an entry object's `id`) without the `local_packs/` prefix whose pack lives in the member's
58	  own `.claudinite/local_packs/<id>/` gets the prefix; everything else on the entry stays verbatim, and
59	  a bare id with no such local pack is a canon declaration — leave it alone. Idempotent, and tracked by
60	  the `local-pack-namespace` baseline migration until the fleet converges.
61	- **Align** — evaluate the repo against its declared packs' *current* checks (the same engine its Stop
62	  hook and CI run). Apply a failing check's own `fix` remedy, **never more**; a finding needing
63	  judgment becomes an issue in the member repo, not an edit.
64	- **Enrollment issue** — being reached proves enrollment: close a still-open one (title
65	  `Enroll <PROJECT_NAME> in Claudinite fleet maintenance`, found by title) as `completed`.
66	
67	**Delivery** — member-side changes go per `maintenance.delivery` in the member's `.claudinite-checks.json`
68	(always explicit; a missing key is drift — materialize `{ "maintenance": { "delivery": "push" } }`):
69	`push` commits to the default branch; `pr` amends the stable `claudinite/maintenance` branch and its
70	one open PR (never merged); an unrecognized value commits nothing and opens an issue there naming it.
71	Adoption necessarily precedes the flag — a first bootstrap lands as a direct commit. A vendored
72	member's refresh lands as **one `push_files` commit** regardless of delivery mode — notes + converge
73	writes + stamp never split. The one thing that can't ride it: file **deletions** (convergence pruning
74	a file the set dropped, or a note's delete) — MCP has no multi-file+delete, so they follow immediately
75	as their own `delete_file` commits, **after** the stamped content commit (#329). Trailing deletes are
76	safe to interrupt: the next night's unconditional convergence re-deletes any straggler, whereas note
77	ops are stamp-gated and so must always land with the stamp.
78	
79	Never touch the home (sheepdog) repo; never adopt without an open adoption issue; never flip a
80	pre-flip member; never let alignment edit beyond a failing check's own remedy; never merge a
81	delivery PR or guess a delivery preference. `smarts: medium` — the bootstrap and alignment merge
82	into existing `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` without clobbering, which is judgment.
83

1	# wiki-growth worker
2	
3	One compile-once/refine-in-place research pass over the repo's product wikis (the
4	Karpathy LLM-wiki pattern): read what the wiki already knows, research only what
5	its own backlog flags, write it back cited. **Most runs find little or nothing —
6	no new citable material means no branch, no PR, and that is the documented good
7	outcome.** A padded or fabricated update is worse than none.
8	
9	Works entirely over the session's **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`) — never
10	`gh`/`curl`/a clone; the fleet run has no consumer shell or npm. "Default branch"
11	below means the repo's actual default branch. The wiki set is derived
12	**structurally**: every `README.md` at depth ≥ 2 under `product-wiki/`, excluding the
13	`product-wiki/product-requirements/` and `product-wiki/sample-data/` subtrees.
14	
15	## Preflight
16	
17	Search the repo for an open PR whose head branch matches `claude/product-wiki-growth/*`.
18	If one is open, do **not** stack a second round of unreviewed research on it —
19	add one dated nudge comment on that PR and stop.
20	
21	## Method
22	
23	1. **Read every wiki page end to end** (`get_file_contents`), the `## Open
24	   questions` and `## Growth log` sections first-class. Never re-derive a claim
25	   that is already cited and current.
26	2. **Pick the one or two open questions** across the set most worth this run;
27	   also spot-check one or two existing citations for staleness (a dead link, a
28	   superseded stat).
29	3. **Research.** *Web mode* (WebSearch/WebFetch available): the open web.
30	   *Repo-derived mode* (web tools absent — do not fail, do not fake): citable
31	   repo-native signal only — new feature-request/extractor-request issues,
32	   merged PRs, issue/PR discussion carrying user-side signal — each citable to
33	   its own GitHub URL.
34	4. **Write into the relevant wiki page(s) only**, per the pack's RULES.md: cite
35	   every claim in that page's `## Sources`; correct a wrong or superseded claim
36	   with a note of why, never a silent deletion; touch `product-wiki/sample-data/`
37	   only when a new claim needs an illustrative example; **never write
38	   `product-wiki/product-requirements/`** — a finding that should move a requirement
39	   gets a growth-log note (and a repo issue) and waits for a human.
40	5. **One dated `## Growth log` entry per touched page** (`- **YYYY-MM-DD** —
41	   what changed`).
42	6. **Update `## Open questions` both directions** — remove what this run
43	   answered, add what its research surfaced.
44	
45	## Stop condition
46	
47	Neither mode yields citable material → stop. No commit, no log entry, no PR.
48	
49	## Delivery
50	
51	This worker's own delivery policy (deliberately stricter than the member's
52	`maintenance.delivery`, and documented here as that sanctioned deviation):
53	branch `claude/product-wiki-growth/<date>` (unique branch per run), commits
54	touching only `product-wiki/**` minus `product-requirements/`, one **PR — never
55	merged, never pushed to the default branch directly**. Web-researched claims
56	entering a committed knowledge base need the review gate; mechanical
57	drift-correction doesn't, but this isn't that. PR body: the question(s)
58	researched, what changed where, the citations added, and the open questions
59	left for the next run.
60	
61	## Tracking
62	
63	Standing issue found by exact title **"Claudinite tracker: Product Wiki
64	Growth"** in the member repo — found by title, never by number, never
65	opened/closed as state (open it if missing). One dated comment when a pass
66	**grew** (`grew (PR #N): <one line>`) or was **blocked** (`blocked: <why>` —
67	the human-visible convergence for a failed unattended run). Clean no-ops stay
68	silent — the pack's freshness advisory is the prolonged-silence observer.
69	
70	Run on a capable model (`smarts: high`): open-web research plus curation is
71	judgment-heavy, and the PR review gate is the last catch for fabrication.
72	
73	## What this worker must never do
74	
75	Edit `product-wiki/product-requirements/`; edit anything outside `product-wiki/`; create
76	new wiki folders (automation refines existing pages — a human creates a wiki
77	deliberately); write an uncited claim; delete a claim without a superseded
78	note; pad a growth log or a wiki page to look productive; merge or approve its
79	own PR; schedule anything (no cron, no `schedule:` workflow); touch the shared
80	canon.
81
