The fleet run has no shell GitHub access — the shell reaches only a git-over-HTTPS proxy scoped to one repo, with no REST credential — so never reach for `gh`/`curl` or a cross-repo clone.
11	- **The mounted canon.** The exact canon revision your project currently consumes — compare against *that*, not a live fetch. Under session-start sync it's the latest `main` (so a promotion is visible only once its PR is merged, not the moment promote opens it); under a pinned submodule it's the pin (so the item lands here only once the pointer is bumped). Either way you prune only against what the project actually mounts.
12	- **The project's local packs.** The set identified in [this pack's README](README.md#identifying-a-projects-capture-surface-its-local-packs) — everything under `.claudinite/local_packs/`. That's the corpus this routine prunes within; the read-only mounted canon elsewhere under `.claudinite/` is never a prune target, only the yardstick you prune *against*.
13	
14	## What it does: prune / rephrase local packs the canon now covers
15	
16	When the canon has **absorbed** a practice a local doc still carries — most often an item the central promote stage lifted up and the canon now owns — the local copy is redundant. This routine:
17	
18	- **Removes** the now-duplicated local item, since the canon is the single source of truth for portable rules.
19	- **Rephrases** a local procedure when the canon's wording of the same idea has changed, so the local packs stay consistent with the canon they point at.
20	
21	**Keep a local item only if it says *more* than the canon — not merely says it more specifically.** Every local item is more specific than the canon, so specificity alone is never the test. Distinguish two cases:
22	
23	- **The general rule in local dress** — it makes the canon's point but leans on this project's classes, files, or names to make it. Once the canon covers the point, those names were only illustration: prune it.
24	- **A stronger point about a narrower case** — it asserts something the canon's general rule leaves out: a tighter constraint, a sharper claim that holds for this project's narrower situation. Keep it.
25	
26	So ask not "is it specific" (it always is) but "does it only lean on specific names to make the general point, or does it make a point the canon doesn't?" Prune the first; keep the second.
27	
28	## A canon check covers an item too — and more strongly than prose
29	
30	The canon carries rules as **conformance checks**, not only prose. A local item is covered when a canon check *enforces* it — stronger coverage than a stated line, since the rule runs on every session and CI pass. Consult the machine-readable rule catalog (`node .claudinite/checks/run.mjs --list`: id, severity, description, doc pointer — it lists the active local packs' own checks too) alongside the prose corpus, and when a check covers the item, **quote the rule id** where you'd otherwise quote a canon line. This cuts both ways: a **local pack's own check** is redundant once a canon check enforces the same rule — prune the local `.mjs` (and drop it from `pack.mjs` + its fixture) exactly as you'd prune a duplicated prose line. The keep-test below is unchanged: a local item that says *more* than the check detects (a stronger point about a narrower case) stays.
31	
32	## Discipline
33	
34	- **Only remove a local item you can show the mounted canon genuinely covers — quote the canon line (or the covering check's rule id).** When unsure, leave it; a wrongful prune deletes a real local lesson.
35	- **Open a single PR against `main`** from a per-run-unique branch (see [the git-github-advanced skill](../../skills/git-github-advanced/SKILL.md)) — one PR for the whole run's prunes, not one per item — never a direct push. This is an unattended routine, on a capable model, and a **wrongful prune deletes a real local lesson**, so — unlike [extract](extract.md), which commits its additive edits straight to `main` — this routine keeps a human approval gate. **Put the issue reference in the commit message** — `Refs #<n>` for this routine's tracking issue (below), in the commit itself, not only the PR body. The member's `basics` `task-lifecycle` check gates a PR on its commits referencing an issue, so a prune commit that cites none reds the member's CI and blocks the merge.
36	- If an edit touches something a test reads, run the project's offline test suite and keep it green before pushing.
37	
38	## Tracking
39	
40	The routine's standing log is the issue titled exactly, in this member repo:
41	
42	> **Claudinite tracker: Growth Dedup**
43	
44	Find it **by that exact title, never a fuzzy match or a hard-coded number**. This tracker never had a fixed title before now, so there is no reliable prior title to migrate from — a run that finds no issue under the exact new title just creates one (closed); it does not need to search for an old name. **Never open, close, or reopen it** afterward — its state carries no meaning, only the log does. Log each run that changed a doc as a **dated comment** — naming what was pruned and the canon line that now covers it. A run that prunes nothing logs nothing.
45	
46	## Run on a capable model
47	
48	Proving the mounted canon genuinely covers a local item before pruning it — and telling "the canon now owns this" from "the canon states this too generally, keep the local cut" — is a **judgment call**. A downgraded model prunes a real lesson; the review PR is a backstop, but a wrongful prune is easy to wave through in review, so don't lean on it. Run this routine on a capable model.
49	
50	## What this routine must never do
51	
52	- **Never edit the read-only canon** — it only prunes the project's *local packs* against it.
53	- **Never prune a local item without quoting the mounted-canon line (or covering check rule id) that covers it** — when unsure, leave it.
54	- **Never prune a local item that makes a stronger point about a narrower case** than the canon — that isn't redundancy. (A local item that only restates the canon in project-specific names *is* prunable once the canon covers the point.)
55

1	# Chrome extension — release & Chrome Web Store publication standard
2	
3	Every Chrome-extension repo of ours ships the **same** release pipeline: same workflows, same
4	Chrome Web Store API usage, same secrets, same versioning and artifact rules, same README install
5	sections. This doc is that contract, the setup steps for a new extension repo, and the manual
6	Chrome Web Store actions the automation can't do. The workflow **logic** is authored once, in this
7	pack's [`stubs/`](stubs/) — the [orchestrator](stubs/workflows/chrome-extension-release.yml), the
8	four `workflow_call`-only **reusable workflows**, and the
9	[report-failure](stubs/actions/report-failure/action.yml),
10	[read-release-config](stubs/actions/read-release-config/action.yml) and
11	[bump-extension-patch](stubs/actions/bump-extension-patch/action.yml) composite actions — and
12	**vendored into each extension repo's own `.github/`**, where the whole pipeline runs with no
13	cross-repo dependency. GitHub only resolves a reusable workflow or composite action from a repo's
14	own `.github/`, so "the logic lives in the pack" means the pack holds the templates and each repo
15	hosts a *managed* copy: the pack keeps every copy in sync — baselining re-materializes on drift via
16	the `chrome-release-vendoring` migration — so a repo **hosts** the pipeline without **owning** it.
17	Treat the vendored `.github/workflows/` + `.github/actions/` set as generated: edit the pack, not
18	the copy. A repo adds only its **required** `.github/release.config` (five explicit keys, **no
19	defaults**). A merged canon change reaches every extension repo through the nightly baselining, not
20	a live `@main` reference. Everything a repo used to carry as workflow values — the version files, the
21	zip location, the test gate, the shipping set, and the previously per-repo `bump`/`filter` scripts
22	— is either pack logic (the bump/filter, and `npm run build`) or an explicit `release.config` key,
23	so no repo ships a bump or filter script any more. Reference implementation:
24	`missingbulb/GoogleCalendarEventCreator`; also adopted by `TLDR` and `CrosswordChat`.
25	
26	Naming in the flat `.github/workflows/` namespace: Chrome-specific logic carries the
27	`chrome-extension-` prefix (future publish standards — other stores — will live beside it);
28	genuinely platform-agnostic pieces (`deploy-privacy-page.yml`, the `report-failure` action) keep
29	general names so other standards reuse them as-is. When in doubt, prefix.
30	
31	## The contract
32	
33	**Versioning**
34	
35	- The extension manifest's `version` (`X.Y.Z`) is the single source of truth. The extension's
36	  `package.json` `version` must equal it, enforced by a CI-run guard (a unit test or an inline
37	  workflow check — the repo picks the mechanism, the invariant is fixed).
38	- Minor/major bumps are deliberate and human: **"bump version"** = edit the manifest and
39	  `package.json` (plus any repo version-sync constant) together on a branch — default the next
40	  **minor** — and land on `main` via a normal PR. Merging the bump *is* cutting the release.
41	- Patch bumps belong to the daily auto-release. No workflow other than the daily bump ever
42	  changes the version; "Release: Create Package" never does.
43	
44	**Artifact**
45	
46	- `npm run build` produces the zip at the **forced-uniform** standard location
47	  `dist/<kebab-cased repo name>.zip` (e.g. `dist/google-calendar-event-creator.zip`, `dist/tldr.zip`,
48	  `dist/crossword-chat.zip`) — the place/name is derived, not a config choice; a repo's build must
49	  write there. Stable, never version-stamped, so
50	  `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/releases/latest/download/<zip>` is a permanent
51	  newest-build URL. `manifest.json` sits at the zip's top level.
52	- The zip's contents come from a single shipping-set source of truth in `dev/build/release/`,
53	  drift-guarded by a test — nothing dev/test-only ships, and a renamed runtime file fails the
54	  build rather than silently dropping out of the package.
55	- A release is GitHub Release **`vX.Y.Z`**, tagged at the released commit, with auto-generated notes
56	  and **two** assets built from the one tested tree, differing only in the injected `API_BASE_URL`:
57	  the headline **`<zip>`** (the permanent `…/releases/latest/download/<zip>` URL) is built with
58	  `API_BASE_URL` **cleared**, so it keeps the committed **dev** default — the downloadable build is
59	  dev, never prod; **`<zip>-prod.zip`** carries the injected prod `API_BASE_URL` and is the store's
60	  source of truth (the publish job downloads it). So **only the store submission is prod-pointed**;
61	  the signing key + client id are injected into both. (A repo with no `API_BASE_URL` — no backend —
62	  produces two byte-identical zips; the split is a no-op there.)
63	
64	**Workflow** — **one** orchestrator per repo, [`chrome-extension-release.yml`](stubs/workflows/chrome-extension-release.yml), named exactly
65	`Release to Chrome Store`. It owns only the triggers; its three `if:`-guarded jobs each call a **local**
66	reusable workflow (`./.github/workflows/…`, all vendored alongside it). The failure reporter keys
67	tracking issues on the **per-operation** names baked into the reusable workflows (`Release: Create
68	Package`, `Release: Publish to Chrome Web Store`, `Release: Daily Auto-Release`) — not on the
69	orchestrator's `name:` — so collapsing to one entry point loses no per-operation triage:
70	
71	| job (trigger) | reusable workflow it calls — what it does |
72	|---|---|
73	| `create-package` (push to `main`; dispatch `mode: package`) | `chrome-extension-create-package.yml` — version guard (a clean no-op unless the manifest version was bumped, so ordinary pushes don't cut a release) → full test gate → build the dev headline `<zip>` + the prod `<zip>-prod.zip` → GitHub Release |
74	| `publish` (dispatch `mode: publish` — the default — with `tag`, `auto_publish`) | `chrome-extension-publish-store.yml` — download the prod `<zip>-prod.zip` (falls back to `<zip>` for pre-split releases) → upload via the store API (publish to users unless `auto_publish: false` → dashboard draft) → refresh the `/privacy/` page (via the `deploy-privacy-page.yml` reusable) |
75	| `daily` (schedule `30 0 * * *`; dispatch `mode: daily`) | `chrome-extension-daily-release.yml` — shipped-file diff vs the latest release tag → patch bump pushed to `main` → calls the two reusable workflows above |
76	
77	The `create-package` job triggers on **every** push to `main` (no per-repo manifest path in the
78	trigger) and relies on the version guard to no-op unless a bump landed. The privacy page has **no
79	job of its own** and no dedicated option: it redeploys as part of **every** publish, from the same
80	`PRIVACY.md` the listing points at. The publish workflow runs that deploy leg even when the store
81	upload fails (so the `/privacy/` URL goes live before the first publication too — see below). The
82	platform-agnostic `deploy-privacy-page.yml` reusable workflow is vendored alongside the others and
83	called by the publish reusable — a repo never dispatches it directly.
84	
85	- Repo-specific values do **not** travel as `with:` inputs — the reusable workflows read them from
86	  the repo itself via the `read-release-config` action, so the orchestrator is copy-verbatim. They live in a
87	  **required, fully explicit** `.github/release.config` (dotenv) — **five keys, no defaults**, because
88	  a default that "happens to match" a repo's layout silently ships the wrong thing when the layout or
89	  the default later changes:
90	  `manifest_path`, `package_json_path`, `setup_command` (`""` = no install, stated),
91	  `test_command`, `ship_paths`. Two things are **not** keys because they are **forced-uniform
92	  structure**, not a per-repo choice: the **build** is always `npm run build`, and the **zip** lives
93	  at `dist/<kebab repo name>.zip` (both `zip_path` and the asset name are derived).
94	  `cer/release-config` fails the review on a missing file, a missing required key, or an unknown
95	  (typo'd) key.
96	- The one value that stays in the **orchestrator** (not the config file) is `build_env` — KEY=VALUE lines a
97	  repo whose build bakes in release config passes on the `create-package` and `daily` jobs, because
98	  it must reference `${{ vars.* }}` statically. Every listed key must be non-empty or the run fails,
99	  so a repo can't ship a placeholder-configured zip. These are the genuine per-**environment**
100	  values that must not be committed; they live in repository **variables** (Settings → Secrets and
101	  variables → Actions → Variables). Store secrets travel via `secrets: inherit`.
102	- Every unattended workflow (all of the above; not PR CI) reports failures through the
103	  `report-failure` composite action baked into the reusable workflows — a red run must reach a
104	  human, never sit unseen in the Actions list. Each failure opens a **fresh** `workflow-failure`
105	  issue, and any earlier open failure issues for the **same** workflow are closed as duplicates of
106	  it, so the newest failure is always the single open bug to triage. Repos no longer carry a
107	  `report-failure.yml`; a repo's own non-standard unattended workflows use the vendored action
108	  directly (`uses: ./.github/actions/report-failure`).
109	- Daily auto-release semantics: the baseline is the **latest release tag**, not a 24-hour window
110	  (self-healing after a failed day); "deployable" = a change under one of the repo's explicit
111	  `ship_paths` roots; the patch bump is pushed straight to `main` (`[skip ci]`)
112	  because the store rejects a version that isn't strictly higher; release + publish are invoked via
113	  `workflow_call` because a `GITHUB_TOKEN` push triggers no workflows. Days with no shipped-file
114	  change are a clean no-op.
115	- The patch bump and the shipped-file filter are **pack logic**, not per-repo scripts: the bump is
116	  the `bump-extension-patch` composite action (token-replaces the version in `manifest_path` +
117	  `package_json_path`), and the filter is a `ship_paths` prefix match in the daily workflow. Both,
118	  and the `read-release-config` action, are dependency-free and run on a bare runner (no `npm ci`).
119	  A repo whose shipping set is a curated subset (not a whole directory) lists it in `ship_paths` and
120	  keeps its build's own ship-set test honest against that list.
121	- Until the four store secrets exist, the publish leg fails **loudly** (a fail-early step lists
122	  the missing names, and the tracking issue nags) — that is the designed state for a repo that
123	  hasn't finished its first manual publication; releases and zips still work.
124	
125	**Chrome Web Store API** — uploads go through `chrome-webstore-upload-cli@3`
126	(`npx --yes chrome-webstore-upload-cli@3 upload --source dist/<zip> [--auto-publish]`), which
127	reads env `EXTENSION_ID` / `CLIENT_ID` / `CLIENT_SECRET` / `REFRESH_TOKEN` — set from the four
128	repository **secrets**, same names in every repo:
129	`CHROME_EXTENSION_ID`, `CHROME_CLIENT_ID`, `CHROME_CLIENT_SECRET`, `CHROME_REFRESH_TOKEN`.
130	
131	**Privacy page** — the policy source is `dev/build/release/store_artifacts/PRIVACY.md`; a Jekyll
132	`permalink: /privacy/` pins the public URL `https://<owner>.github.io/<repo>/privacy/`
133	independent of the file's location. The store listing's Privacy-tab URL points **there**, never
134	at a `blob/main` link. The page redeploys on every store publish — the publish workflow's
135	privacy-deploy leg runs regardless of whether the upload leg succeeds, so it also brings the URL up
136	for the very first publication.
137	One-time: repo Settings → Pages → Source = "GitHub Actions".
138	
139	**Store listing & permission justifications live in the dashboard, not the repo** — the listing
140	copy (name, summary, detailed description, category), the graphic-asset choices, and the
141	Privacy-practices answers (single-purpose statement, a justification for **every** permission the
142	manifest requests — `permissions`, `host_permissions`, and `optional_*` alike — the remote-code
143	answer, data-usage declarations, reviewer notes) are all Chrome Web Store dashboard state. We keep
144	**no** repo copy of them: a mirror only drifts from the dashboard it duplicates. They are authored
145	once, at the first publication (tracked by a one-time issue — see setup below), and edited in the
146	dashboard thereafter. What the repo *does* hold and enforce is the privacy policy and its
147	agreement with the manifest:
148	
149	- `dev/build/release/store_artifacts/PRIVACY.md` is the privacy policy, deployed verbatim as the
150	  public `/privacy/` page the listing points at.
151	- `cer/privacy-permission-alignment` (test the world) blocks whenever a permission the manifest
152	  requests isn't disclosed in `PRIVACY.md` — the one alignment that must hold at all times.
153	- `cer/permission-added-store-issue` (test the work) fires when a change *adds* a permission,
154	  prompting the one step that can't be automated: open a tracking issue to add that permission's
155	  justification on the dashboard's Privacy-practices tab before the next publish.
156	
157	**Store assets & icons** — required inventory: a 128 px store icon; the manifest icons the
158	extension ships (16/32/48/128), living inside the extension source where the manifest points;
159	at least one 1280×800 (or 640×400) screenshot; optionally 440×280 small and 1400×560 marquee
160	promo tiles. Listing-only images live in `store_artifacts/`; shipped icons live in the extension
161	source. Every asset comes from a **committed, deterministic generator script** — regenerate and
162	commit, never hand-edit a generated PNG. The generator's tech is the repo's choice (the
163	reference repo draws with stdlib-only Python; CrosswordChat rasterizes SVG/HTML with headless
164	Chromium); what's fixed is that every asset is reproducible from the repo.
165	
166	**Layout** — release machinery lives in `dev/build/release/`: the zip builder + shipping-set
167	module (with its test), and `store_artifacts/` (`PRIVACY.md`, listing screenshots, icon/asset
168	generators). The patch-bumper and shipped-paths filter that used to live here are **gone** — they
169	are pack logic now (vendored). No per-repo release doc: the concrete names and paths live in
170	`manifest.json`/`package.json` and the required `.github/release.config`, and the shared procedure
171	lives once, in this guide — a repo copy would only drift from it.
172	
173	## README template
174	
175	Every extension repo's README carries these two sections, same wording, repo values filled in:
176	
177	```markdown
178	## Install
179	
180	**[Install from the Chrome Web Store →](<store listing URL>)**
181	
182	Or load the latest development build:
183	
184	1. Download [the latest release zip](https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/releases/latest/download/<zip>)
185	   and extract it — it unpacks to a folder with `manifest.json` at its top.
186	2. Open `chrome://extensions`, enable **Developer mode** (top right), click
187	   **Load unpacked**, and select that folder.
188	
189	## Releasing
190	
191	The version users see is [`<manifest path>`](<manifest path>)'s `version`. Merging a version
192	bump to `main` cuts GitHub Release `vX.Y.Z` with `<zip>` attached, and the daily auto-release
193	ships shipped-file changes to the Chrome Web Store on its own (patch-bumping as needed).
194	```
195	
196	Until the extension's first store publication, replace the store line with:
197	
198	```markdown
199	*Not yet on the Chrome Web Store — the listing goes live after the first manual publish (tracked
200	in the repo's first-publication issue).*
201	```
202	
203	## Setting up a new extension repo
204	
205	1. Declare the pack in `.claudinite-checks.json`; baselining then **vendors the release set** — the
206	   [orchestrator](stubs/workflows/chrome-extension-release.yml) plus the reusable workflows under
207	   [`stubs/workflows/`](stubs/workflows/) and the composite actions under
208	   [`stubs/actions/`](stubs/actions/) — into this repo's own `.github/`. (Setting up before the next
209	   nightly pass? Copy those two trees yourself; there are no tokens to replace.) Then write the
210	   **required** `.github/release.config` (dotenv) with **all five** keys, explicitly — no defaults:
211	   `manifest_path`, `package_json_path`, `setup_command` (`""` = no install), `test_command`,
212	   `ship_paths`. (The build is always `npm run build`, and the zip lives at the derived
213	   `dist/<kebab repo name>.zip`, so neither is a key.) If the build bakes in per-environment config,
214	   uncomment the `build_env` block on the `create-package` and `daily` jobs of the orchestrator (the
215	   only vendored file a repo edits) and set the matching repository **variables** (see step 5).
216	2. Create `dev/build/release/` — zip builder + shipping-set module (with its test) and
217	   `store_artifacts/` with `PRIVACY.md` and the icon/asset generators — adapting from the reference
218	   repo's `dev/build/release/`. **No** per-repo patch-bumper or shipped-paths filter (those are
219	   vendored pack logic now); a curated shipping set is expressed as `ship_paths` in `release.config`, kept honest
220	   against the build by the repo's own ship-set test. No `releasing.md` and no `STORE-LISTING.md`:
221	   the release procedure lives in this guide, and the listing copy / permission justifications are
222	   dashboard state (above).
223	3. Wire `npm run build` to produce the zip at the standard `dist/<kebab repo name>.zip` (a monorepo
224	   sets a root `build` that delegates, e.g. `npm --prefix client run build`) and set `test_command`
225	   to the repo's full release gate. Add the two README sections above.
226	4. One-time GitHub setup: Pages → Source = "GitHub Actions". The four `CHROME_*` secrets come
227	   later, after the first manual publish. (No cross-repo Actions access to configure — the whole
228	   pipeline is vendored, so it never calls another repo's reusable workflows.)
229	5. Open the **first-publication tracking issue** (idempotent — search the tracker first, skip if
230	   one already exists, open or closed) so the one-time manual setup below is tracked as state, not
231	   carried as a repo file. Its checklist:
232	   - Set the four `CHROME_*` **secrets** (minted below) — Settings → Secrets and variables →
233	     Actions → Secrets.
234	   - **Only if the orchestrator uses `build_env`**: set the repository **variables** it references. These
235	     are automatable where API access exists — the issue carries a ready-to-run script, e.g.
236	     `gh variable set API_BASE_URL --repo <owner>/<repo> --body "https://…"` (repeat per key) —
237	     rather than a click-through, so the one manual part is minting the store credentials, not
238	     copying config into Settings.
239	   - Do the "First publication" steps below, then close the issue. From then on the daily pipeline
240	     owns routine shipping.
241	
242	> A repo's **own** first-publication runbook — a go-live checklist, an account/credentials setup
243	> guide — is the same do-then-close artifact by another name: first-publication *guidance*, not
244	> standing reference. Once the extension is live it's spent; fold anything durable into the
245	> standing architecture/README docs it already defers to, then remove the file. (That a cleanup
246	> makes you fix references *into* such a doc is the cue to ask whether the doc itself is in scope
247	> for removal — not to polish it.)
248	
249	## Manual actions — publishing to the Chrome Web Store
250	
251	The steps the automation cannot do, distilled from the path actually run for the reference
252	extension; the upstream reference is
253	[Using the Chrome Web Store Publish API](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/webstore/using-api).
254	
255	### First publication (once per extension)
256	
257	1. Register a developer account at the
258	   [developer dashboard](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/devconsole) (one-time $5 fee).
259	2. **Add new item** → upload the release zip. If the extension pins its ID with a manifest
260	   `key` (needed when OAuth redirect URIs depend on a stable ID — see
261	   [the chrome-extension pack RULES](../chrome-extension/RULES.md)), the **first** upload must NOT contain the
262	   `key`: the store assigns the ID at first upload, and you copy the dashboard's Package-tab
263	   public key back into the build afterwards. Record the 32-char item ID → the
264	   `CHROME_EXTENSION_ID` secret.
265	3. Complete the listing — 128px store icon, name, summary, detailed description, category,
266	   ≥ 1280×800 screenshot — authoring the copy **directly in the dashboard** (this is where it
267	   lives; the repo keeps no copy). Screenshots and icons come from `store_artifacts/`.
268	4. Privacy tab: write the single-purpose statement, a justification for **every** permission the
269	   manifest requests, and the data-usage declarations, directly in the dashboard; set the
270	   **Privacy policy** field (bottom of the tab) to the `/privacy/` Pages URL — the same policy
271	   `PRIVACY.md` deploys. **Before submitting**: bring the privacy page up by running the **Release**
272	   workflow once with **mode: publish** — its privacy-deploy leg runs even though the store-upload leg
273	   fails at this stage (no store item or secrets yet — the designed pre-publication state), so the
274	   `/privacy/` page goes live. Load the URL in a browser to confirm it's live, and paste that exact
275	   permalink — never a guessed path. Google re-fetches this URL on **every** publish, and an
276	   unreachable link fails the publish (see [When a store publish fails](#when-a-store-publish-fails)).
277	5. Submit for review — approval takes hours to a few days (`ITEM_PENDING_REVIEW` = success).
278	   While the item is **pending review the API rejects uploads** — hold the pipeline dry run
279	   until the first review completes. Every subsequent upload must carry a **strictly higher**
280	   version, which is why the pipeline always bumps before it ships; a "version must be
281	   greater" rejection on a dry run is a **pass** — it proves the credential wiring works
282	   end to end.
283	
284	### Minting the API credentials (once per extension)
285	
286	Browser-only — no local tooling needed. Two standing rules for every step below:
287	
288	- Every "sign in with Google" must use the **same Google account that owns the store listing**,
289	  and will hit a **"Google hasn't verified this app"** interstitial — click **Advanced** →
290	  **"Go to \<app\> (unsafe)"** → **Allow**. That's fine and permanent: you are this OAuth app's
291	  only user, so **ignore every verification prompt and never start verification** — an
292	  unverified app's tokens work indefinitely.
293	- Before acting on any Cloud-console page, confirm the **top-bar project picker** shows the
294	  project created in step 1.
295	
296	**Google Cloud setup:**
297	
298	1. **Create a project**: <https://console.cloud.google.com/projectcreate> — any name, keep
299	   "No organization" → **Create**. Confirm the top-bar project picker switched to it.
300	2. **Enable the Chrome Web Store API**:
301	   <https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/library/chromewebstore.googleapis.com> → **Enable**.
302	3. **Configure the OAuth consent screen** (the console now brands this "Google Auth
303	   Platform"): <https://console.cloud.google.com/auth/overview> → **Get started** → app name +
304	   support email → Audience: **External** → contact email → agree → **Create**.
305	4. **Publish the app to production**: <https://console.cloud.google.com/auth/audience> →
306	   **Publish app** → **Confirm**. Left in "Testing", refresh tokens silently expire after
307	   7 days — the unattended daily release dies a week later.
308	5. **Create the OAuth client**: <https://console.cloud.google.com/auth/clients> → **Create
309	   client** → type **Web application** (NOT Desktop — Desktop clients can't take custom
310	   redirect URIs; the client type makes no difference to `chrome-webstore-upload-cli`, the
311	   refresh-token grant is identical) → add authorized redirect URI exactly
312	   `https://developers.google.com/oauthplayground` → **Create**. The client ID and secret are
313	   `CHROME_CLIENT_ID` / `CHROME_CLIENT_SECRET`.
314	
315	**Mint the refresh token in the OAuth 2.0 Playground:**
316	
317	1. Open <https://developers.google.com/oauthplayground> → gear icon (top right) → check
318	   **Use your own OAuth credentials** → paste the client ID + secret.
319	2. In Step 1, ignore the API list: type `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chromewebstore` into
320	   **Input your own scopes** → **Authorize APIs** → sign in as the listing owner → past the
321	   unverified interstitial → **Allow**.
322	3. Step 2 → **Exchange authorization code for tokens** → copy the **Refresh token** (starts
323	   `1//`). Ignore the access token — it expires hourly; the CLI mints its own.
324	4. **Empty Refresh-token field?** Consent wasn't force-prompted: in the gear panel set
325	   **Force prompt: Consent** and redo the authorize step.
326	
327	Add all four values as repository secrets at
328	`https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/settings/secrets/actions/new`:
329	`CHROME_EXTENSION_ID`, `CHROME_CLIENT_ID`, `CHROME_CLIENT_SECRET`, `CHROME_REFRESH_TOKEN`.
330	
331	**Alternative — local Node**: `npx chrome-webstore-upload-keys` walks the same flow from a
332	terminal (it needs the client to be a **Desktop app**, whose `http://localhost` redirect it
333	uses). Use it only when a Node-equipped machine is at hand; the Playground route above is the
334	default because it assumes nothing about the operator's machine.
335	
336	### Routine shipping
337	
338	- Nothing to do for accumulated work: the daily auto-release ships any day whose merges touched
339	  shipped files, on its own patch bump.
340	- A deliberate release now: **"bump version"** (default minor) → merge the PR (that cuts the
341	  GitHub Release) → run the **Release** workflow with **mode: publish** from its dispatch page,
342	  `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/actions/workflows/chrome-extension-release.yml` (blank tag = latest release).
343	- Once the store approves, Chrome auto-pushes the update to installed users within hours — no
344	  reinstall.
345	
346	### When a store publish fails
347	
348	- **HTTP 400 `Publish condition not met: Privacy policy link is not reachable.`** — Google
349	  fetches the listing's privacy-policy URL at publish time, and re-checks it on **every**
350	  publish. Fix it on the item's **Privacy** tab in the
351	  [developer dashboard](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/devconsole)
352	  (`https://chrome.google.com/webstore/devconsole/<publisher-id>/<item-id>/edit/privacy`,
353	  bottom field, "Privacy policy"): set it to the exact live `/privacy/` permalink → **Save
354	  draft** → re-run the publish.
355	- **Upload rejected while `ITEM_PENDING_REVIEW`** — the API can't upload while a review is in
356	  flight; wait for the review to complete, then re-run.
357	- **"version must be greater" than the live one** — expected whenever the zip's version isn't
358	  strictly higher; on a credentials dry run this is success, otherwise let the daily bump (or
359	  "bump version") raise it first.
360	
361	### When a change touches the extension's permissions
362	
363	Any PR that changes the manifest's `permissions`, `host_permissions`, or `optional_*`:
364	
365	1. Disclose the permission in `PRIVACY.md` in the **same PR** — the deployed privacy policy must
366	   reflect what the extension can access. `cer/privacy-permission-alignment` blocks the change
367	   until it does.
368	2. Adding a permission trips `cer/permission-added-store-issue` (advisory): open a tracking issue
369	   for the manual dashboard step — the Privacy-practices tab must carry a written justification
370	   for the new permission, and the store blocks publishing the new version until it does, so the
371	   next store publish (daily or manual) stalls on it. (If the daily pipeline hits it first, the
372	   failed publish lands on its `workflow-failure` tracking issue; the proactive issue beats the
373	   reactive one.) After updating the dashboard, re-run the publish.
374	3. Expect deeper store review than a plain code update — permission changes re-open scrutiny.
375	4. A new **required** permission that carries an install-time warning disables the extension
376	   for existing users until each one re-approves it — prefer `optional_permissions` /
377	   `optional_host_permissions` requested at runtime (`chrome.permissions.request`) when the
378	   feature allows.
379

1	# Conversation extract — the growth pack's conversation-side daily task
2	
3	The conversation-side sibling of [extract.md](extract.md): mine a repo's captured conversation
4	logs for durable lessons, post the dialogue behind each extracted rule on the issue it was worked
5	under, and prune logs past retention. It runs with the same access model as `growth-extract` —
6	the repo's own content is plain **local git** in the checkout; only the GitHub **issue API** goes
7	through MCP. (Capture, which needs the live session transcript, is the separate in-session step at
8	merge; this task consumes what capture already pushed.)
9	
10	The **method** — the friction signals, the measured efficiency analysis, the lesson bar — stays
11	canonical in [extracting-lessons.md](extracting-lessons.md) and is not restated here. The captured
12	log carries what that method needs: per-entry timestamps, per-message token usage, and the
13	tool_use/tool_result pairs behind wall-time numbers.
14	
15	## Conventions used in this doc
16	
17	- **Default branch.** `main` stands for the repo's default branch — substitute whatever it uses.
18	- **Access split — local git for the repo, MCP for the issue API.** The `conversation-logs` branch
19	  and its files, the `.claudinite/local_packs/` commit, and the retention prune are all **plain git
20	  in this repo's checkout** (`git fetch`/`show`/`rm`/`push`) — the branch is *in this repo*, so no
21	  MCP and no cross-repo access is involved, exactly as `growth-extract` reads `git log` and commits
22	  to `main`. Only posting the dialogue and the tracking-issue log go through the session's GitHub
23	  MCP tools (`mcp__github__add_issue_comment`) — the issue API isn't a git operation.
24	- **The project's local packs.** Everything under `.claudinite/local_packs/` — the project's own
25	  packs (the canon home repo's own is `.claudinite/local_packs/claudinite/`); never the read-only
26	  mounted canon elsewhere under `.claudinite/`.
27	
28	## The run
29	
30	1. **Fetch the queue.** `git fetch origin conversation-logs`; list its files
31	   (`git ls-tree --name-only origin/conversation-logs`). No branch, or no `*.jsonl` → stop; a quiet
32	   repo is the common, valid outcome.
33	2. **Read retention.** From this repo's `.claudinite-checks.json`, the grow_with_claudinite entry's
34	   `config.retention_days`. Unset → the prune in step 5 is skipped entirely (capture-only adoption).
35	3. **Fresh pass.** For each log captured in the recent window (its filename carries the capture
36	   stamp; corpus dedup makes an overlapping re-read harmless, so err toward re-reading the last
37	   several days): read it (`git show origin/conversation-logs:<file>`) and run the
38	   extracting-lessons method over it — including the wall-time/efficiency analysis, measured from
39	   the log's own timestamps, never hand-waved.
40	4. **Route, commit, and post.** Fold each keeper into the repo's **own local packs**
41	   (`.claudinite/local_packs/`) at the local promotion ladder's strongest mechanism, exactly as
42	   extracting-lessons.md prescribes — one commit to `main` for the whole run, direct (no per-run
43	   PR, same rationale as [extract.md](extract.md)). For each rule that actually landed, render its
44	   source log's dialogue — `node packs/grow_with_claudinite/render-dialogue.mjs
45	   <(git show origin/conversation-logs:<file>) --max-chars 60000` — and post it via
46	   `add_issue_comment` on the issue named in the log's filename (`--issue-<n>--`), one comment per
47	   chunk, opening with one provenance line (the rule added, the capture date, the session id). A
48	   log that yields nothing gets **no** comment — extraction is the only path to permanence. Finding
49	   nothing at all is fine and common.
50	5. **Retention prune.** When `retention_days` is set: for each log whose filename stamp is older
51	   than `retention_days` days, give it a **final hindsight pass** — one last read against the
52	   now-current corpus (steps 3–4 apply to anything it still yields) — then `git rm` it on the
53	   `conversation-logs` branch and push (commit message ending `[skip ci]`). The branch is never
54	   merged and its history is never rewritten — plain add/remove commits only.
55	
56	## Tracking: log each run under the routine's own issue
57	
58	The standing log is the issue titled exactly **`Claudinite tracker: Conversation Extract`** in this
59	repo — found by that exact title, never a number; created **closed** if missing; its state never
60	changed afterward. When a run lands a lesson, posts dialogue, or prunes logs, log it as one dated
61	comment (via `add_issue_comment`) naming what changed; a run that changed nothing logs nothing.
62	
63	## Run on a capable model
64	
65	Whether a lesson clears the bar — and whether the hindsight pass should overturn a fresh-pass
66	call — is the judgment this task exists for, and its commits land without a review gate. Its
67	`smarts: high` names the tier; run it there.
68	
69	## What this task must never do
70	
71	- **Never merge `conversation-logs`** anywhere, and never rewrite its history — plain add and
72	  remove commits only.
73	- **Never post raw JSONL to an issue** — only the rendered dialogue, chunked under the comment
74	  size cap.
75	- **Never delete a log younger than retention, and never delete anything while `retention_days` is
76	  unset** — deletion is the ack that both passes happened.
77	- **Never touch the shared canon** — writes go to the repo's `.claudinite/local_packs/`, its
78	  `main`, its issues, and its logs branch; lifting portable lessons is the central promote stage's job.
79

1	# firebase-release pack
2	
3	The opt-in release standard for Firebase-backed apps: two fully separate projects with the
4	committed default pinned to dev, prod config injected only by the release pipeline, and — the part
5	provenance alone can't give — **App Check attestation** so only store-installed builds reach the
6	prod backend. Declared when a project approaches shipping, like
7	[chrome-extension-release](../chrome-extension-release/README.md).
8	
9	> **Status: standard decided ahead of first exercise.** Distilled from missingbulb/TLDR's worked
10	> AWS split (account/stack/config/CI layers, its provenance-only gap explicitly noted) and decided
11	> for Firebase in missingbulb/ShoutsAndWhispers `docs/ENVIRONMENTS.md`; no project has run a
12	> release through it yet. Expect refinement — and conformance checks, mirroring
13	> chrome-extension-release's — when the first release exercises it.
14	
15	## Prose (`RULES.md`) — by section
16	
17	| Section (≤5 words) | How enforced |
18	|---|---|
19	| Committed default is dev, always | prose (+ guard tests at release) |
20	| Prod config is pipeline-injected | prose (+ release-workflow gate) |
21	| Attestation beats provenance | prose (App Check enforcement) |
22	| Promotion is deliberate, dev automatic | prose |
23

1	# Growth — discover packs (a run_daily task, executed centrally)
2	
3	The pack-discovery pipeline, run as an ordinary **`run_daily` task** — the planner picks it up per
4	member on that member's **weekly full sweep**, and the orchestrator dispatches it like any other unit
5	(member-scoped, where the central promote stage is home-only). For the member it's
6	handed, it runs the whole pipeline end to end — **manifest → suggest → populate → open PR** — as one
7	process. It is Claudinite-internal; consuming repos don't vendor or `@import` it.
8	
9	**Per member, executed centrally.** Like every worker, it runs in the home-repo routine session with
10	the fleet token, and reads the member and writes the **canon** over the API. So "central" means *where
11	it runs and what it can write* (it opens PRs against the canon, which a member-sandboxed session can't),
12	while *scheduling* is the regular per-member weekly stagger — no special-casing. Over a week the whole
13	fleet is covered, ~1/7 per night; **first sight wins** — the first member whose run hits an unhomed
14	technology authors its pack, and the rest see it on the shelf and skip.
15	
16	**The steps are distinct, not conflated.** Cataloguing the member's stack (step 1) and judging whether
17	the canon needs a pack (step 2) are different levels of analysis — step 1 decides nothing about packs.
18	They are sequential steps of one task, not one merged judgment.
19	
20	## The governing principle — distilled from worked examples, never from imagination
21	
22	A pack this task authors is *distilled from worked examples, not written from imagination* — the
23	constraint that makes automated authoring safe. **The member actually using the technology is the
24	worked example.** Every rule must trace to how it really uses the technology — a real build config, a
25	real packaging/signing step, a gotcha already in its docs — and be cited. **Never pad** with
26	speculative best-practice rules the evidence doesn't demonstrate; a rule you can't ground, you don't
27	write. A smaller honest pack beats a padded one. And **never open an empty stub to fill later** — the
28	point is to populate it now (step 3).
29	
30	## Run on a capable model
31	
32	The task's `smarts` is `high`: past step 1 every step is heavy judgment — is a technology genuinely
33	unhomed, which usage is a portable rule vs. this member's one-off, does a rule mechanize into a check,
34	what fingerprint reliably detects the technology. Per [the unattended-agents skill](../../skills/unattended-agents/SKILL.md)
35	("match the agent model to the judgment it must make"), the worker runs on a capable tier.
36	
37	## Conventions used in this doc
38	
39	- **The member.** The one repo this unit was dispatched against (`repo` in the plan unit). Read it over
40	  the API — you run centrally, not inside it.
41	- **The canon.** The Claudinite home repo, where authored packs land. You open PRs here, with the fleet
42	  token.
43	- **GitHub access is MCP-native.** Reading the member and opening the canon PR go through the session's
44	  **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`); the fleet run has no shell GitHub access and no cross-repo
45	  checkout, so never reach for `gh`/`curl`.
46	
47	## The pipeline
48	
49	### Step 1 — manifest the member's stack
50	
51	Produce a manifest of what the member is built on and how it ships, by reading its files over the API.
52	This step **only catalogues** — it does not look at the pack shelf or judge whether anything deserves a
53	pack. Run it with this instruction:
54	
55	> Produce a **manifest** of what this project is built on and how it ships — a comprehensive,
56	> evidence-grounded inventory. You are **only** observing and cataloguing. You are **not** deciding
57	> anything about tooling, packs, standards, or what should change; you make no recommendations, and you
58	> do not compare this repo against anything outside it.
59	>
60	> **Ground every entry in the repo's files** — dependency and build manifests, lockfiles, toolchain and
61	> config files, CI and release workflows, packaging and signing scripts, the source structure, and the
62	> docs. For each entry, cite the concrete evidence — the file (and the line/section or step) that proves
63	> it. Never infer from "projects like this usually…"; if the repo doesn't show it, it is not in the
64	> manifest. If something is present but appears vestigial or aspirational (declared but unused), include
65	> it and say so.
66	>
67	> Catalogue across **three axes**. Put each item under the single axis that fits best; when it genuinely
68	> spans two, place it under the primary and cross-note the other.
69	>
70	> 1. **Technologies** — languages and their versions, runtimes, frameworks, build systems, and the major
71	>    libraries that shape how you write and build here (the load-bearing ones, not every transitive
72	>    dependency). Evidence: manifests, lockfiles, toolchain/config.
73	> 2. **APIs & external services** — every third-party service, cloud API, SDK, auth provider, datastore,
74	>    message bus, or external integration the code actually talks to. Evidence: client SDK dependencies,
75	>    config/env keys, call sites.
76	> 3. **Deployments & distribution** — how and where this ships: packaging format(s), distribution
77	>    channel(s), the runtime/host it targets, signing/notarization, and the release mechanism. Evidence:
78	>    release workflows, packaging and signing scripts, deploy config.
79	>
80	> For **each** item report: **name**; **axis**; **evidence** (the file(s), and what they show); **what
81	> it is in this repo** (one line); **prominence** — one of `core` (the project is built on it),
82	> `supporting` (used but peripheral), `vestigial` (present but apparently unused); and a **`?` flag** if
83	> you are uncertain the item is real or correctly characterised. Prominence is a factual read of how
84	> central the item is *in this repo* — **not** a judgment about whether it deserves any downstream
85	> treatment.
86	>
87	> Be **comprehensive over concise**: a later step filters and decides, so a true item you omit is lost,
88	> while an over-included one is cheaply dropped. When unsure whether something rises to an entry, include
89	> it with the `?` flag. Do **not** deduplicate against, reference, or even consider any pack, tool, or
90	> catalogue outside this repository.
91	>
92	> Output the manifest as Markdown grouped under the three axis headings, one bullet per item with the
93	> fields labelled.
94	
95	### Step 2 — suggest new packs
96	
97	Now check the manifest against the canon's pack shelf (`packs/`). A technology is **homed** — drop it —
98	when any pack owns it, **a stub pack included**. Also drop anything with an **open pack-authoring PR**
99	on the canon (another member's earlier run this week may have authored it — this is the first-sight
100	dedup), and anything the manifest marked **`vestigial`** (declared-but-unused is not a real sighting).
101	What remains is this member's candidate set — **suggest a pack for each, on first sight** (don't wait
102	for the technology to recur).
103	
104	### Step 3 — populate the pack
105	
106	For each candidate, **author a populated pack** by distilling from how the member actually uses the
107	technology — its build/toolchain config, CI and release workflows, packaging/signing scripts, relevant
108	source, and any gotchas already in its docs. Apply [generate-project-instructions](../../skills/generate-project-instructions/SKILL.md)'
109	method (don't re-derive it): strip the origin project, keep what's true for the technology, and descend
110	the promotion ladder ([checks/DESIGN.md](../../checks/DESIGN.md)) —
111	a rule a deterministic check can carry becomes the **check plus a fixture test** (it fires on a
112	violating input, stays quiet on a clean one), a procedure with a nameable trigger becomes a skill the
113	pack requires, and only signature-less judgment lands as `RULES.md` prose. Ground and cite every rule;
114	never pad. Write the four-file pack — `RULES.md`, `pack.mjs` (add the `marker`/`detect` fingerprint when
115	the technology carries a reliable one, so it self-declares on future repos; `detect: null` otherwise),
116	`README.md` (rule table + a **provenance line** naming the member it was distilled from), and the index
117	rows ([packs/README.md](../README.md), plus [CLAUDE.md](../../CLAUDE.md) for a new pack *kind*).
118	
119	### Step 4 — open one PR per pack
120	
121	Push each authored pack to a per-run-unique branch (see [the git-github-advanced skill](../../skills/git-github-advanced/SKILL.md))
122	and open **its own PR** against the canon's default branch — never a direct push, and never several packs
123	in one PR. A new pack is reviewed differently from a rule addition, so each earns its own review surface.
124	The write surface is bounded to the new `packs/<tech>/` directory (with any check's registration and
125	fixture) plus its index rows. Keep the commit and PR terse, and **put the issue reference in the commit message** — `Refs #<n>` for this task's tracking issue (below), in the commit itself, not only the PR body. The canon's `basics` `task-lifecycle` check gates a PR on its commits referencing an issue, so a commit that cites none reds CI and blocks the merge.
126	
127	## Tracking issue
128	
129	The task's standing log is the issue titled exactly, on the **canon**:
130	
131	> **Claudinite tracker: Pack Discovery**
132	
133	Find it **by that exact title, never a fuzzy match or a hard-coded number**.
134	
135	**One-time rename (drop once the fleet has converged):** if no issue carries this title yet, look for
136	one titled exactly `Auto-Improvements Tracker - Pack Discovery` (the old name). If found, rename it to
137	`Claudinite tracker: Pack Discovery` and close it if it's open — do this once, then use the new title
138	on every later run. If neither title is found, create the new one (closed).
139	
140	**Never open, close, or reopen it** afterward — its state carries no meaning, only the log does. Log
141	each run as a **dated comment**: the member processed, and per authored pack the technology, the rungs
142	its rules landed on (check ids / skills / prose); for a candidate you found nothing groundable for, name
143	it and why.
144	
145	## What this task must never do
146	
147	- **Never conflate steps 1 and 2** — the manifest step catalogues and never consults the pack shelf; the
148	  suggest step is where the shelf and the pack decision come in.
149	- **Never author from imagination or pad** — every rule traces to the member's real usage; an
150	  ungroundable rule is not written. The pack may be small; it may not be invented.
151	- **Never open an empty stub to fill later** — populate it now (step 3).
152	- **Never re-author an existing pack** — a stub counts as a home, and an open pack-authoring PR counts as
153	  in progress. Dedup against both; a pack that exists is the central promote stage's to fill, not this
154	  task's to replace.
155	- **Never author for a `vestigial` technology** — declared-but-unused is not a real sighting.
156	- **Never exceed the bounded write surface** — per pack, only its new `packs/<tech>/` directory (and any
157	  check's registration + fixture) plus the index rows, in its own PR. Never alter the member or an
158	  unrelated pack.
159

1	# Firebase release — environments, provenance, attestation
2	
3	The release standard for Firebase-backed apps. **Decided ahead of first exercise** (from TLDR's
4	worked AWS split + the design in ShoutsAndWhispers `docs/ENVIRONMENTS.md`); refine on the first
5	real release rather than treating this as settled canon.
6	
7	## 1. The committed default is dev, always
8	
9	- **Two fully separate Firebase projects** — dev and prod. Isolation is at the project boundary
10	  (data, auth, quotas, keys), the Firebase analog of TLDR's separate AWS accounts.
11	- **Everything committed points at dev**: `.firebaserc`'s `default` alias, the checked-in client
12	  `firebase_options`, every documented command. Strongest form: **no prod alias committed at
13	  all** until the release milestone — no command run from the repo can reach prod by accident.
14	- **Dev builds coexist with prod installs** (Android `applicationIdSuffix .dev` / iOS bundle-id
15	  suffix) so testing on a real phone never displaces the store app.
16	- **Guard tests pin the contract** once prod exists: the committed client config's projectId must
17	  be the dev project (TLDR pins the equivalent in `client/test/inject-config.test.mjs`), and seed
18	  scripts hard-refuse prod targets.
19	
20	## 2. Prod config is release-pipeline-injected
21	
22	- The store artifact gets its prod `firebase_options` (and any prod-only identifiers) from
23	  **release-workflow variables**, never from the tree. A plain/local/CI build physically lacks
24	  prod coordinates.
25	- **The release workflow fails if any injected variable is unset** — a dev-pointed or
26	  placeholder-configured artifact must be unbuildable, not merely unlikely.
27	- The same artifact that goes to the store is the release download — one prod build, one
28	  provenance trail.
29	
30	## 3. Attestation beats provenance
31	
32	- Provenance (only the pipeline holds prod config) is necessary but not sufficient — anyone can
33	  extract config from a shipped APK/IPA. TLDR documents exactly this gap; Firebase can close it:
34	  **enforce App Check on the prod project** (Firestore, Functions, Storage) with Play Integrity
35	  (Android) / App Attest (iOS), so requests without a store-signed attestation are rejected
36	  server-side.
37	- **Dev keeps the App Check debug provider** so emulators, tests, and local builds stay
38	  friction-free; enforcement is a per-project switch, which is precisely why prod must be a
39	  separate project.
40	- Register the store apps in the Firebase console at first release; until enforcement is ON,
41	  treat prod as provenance-only and say so in the project's environments doc.
42	
43	## 4. Promotion is deliberate; dev deploys are automatic
44	
45	- Dev auto-deploys from `main` (it is the always-current sandbox); **prod deploys are a manual,
46	  explicit promotion** of something already verified in dev — a workflow dispatch or a named
47	  command, never a side effect.
48	- Per-environment deploy credentials: the prod deploy identity is distinct from dev's, so a
49	  compromised or misconfigured dev lane cannot touch prod.
50	- Data hygiene follows the project split: seed/backfill tooling targets dev by name and refuses
51	  the prod project id as a hard guard, not a convention.
52

1	# chrome-extension-release pack
2	
3	The release & Chrome-Web-Store publication standard for our extensions — the reusable workflows' contract, the setup steps, the manual store actions (`RELEASE.md`), the **vendored release set** (`stubs/workflows/` + `stubs/actions/`, materialized into each consumer's own `.github/`), and the conformance checks. **Opt-in**: a project declares it in `.claudinite-checks.json` when it's ready to ship (a `manifest.json` alone does not pull it in). Declaring it is the cue to vendor the release machinery — the migration apply pass materializes the set (the `chrome-release-vendoring` migration), the checks below keep it in shape, and setup opens the one-time first-publication issue. GitHub only resolves a reusable workflow / composite action from a repo's own `.github/`, so the pack holds the templates and each consumer hosts a managed copy — no cross-repo `@main` dependency.
4	
5	Fingerprint: a repo already carrying the standard's `Release to Chrome Store` orchestrator (a workflow with that name — or a legacy pre-rename name like `Release` — that wires the create-package reusable, whether via the vendored local `./.github/workflows/chrome-extension-create-package.yml` or the pre-vendoring canon call `@main`). `--init` uses it to seed the pack into a fresh declaration (including a repo that shipped release before this pack existed); the marker only *suspects* the pack, so it never forces or forbids the declaration afterward.
6	
7	## Checks
8	
9	| Check | Enforces (≤5 words) | Severity |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| `cer/release-workflows` | vendored set present: orchestrator name/schedule + local reusables + actions | blocking |
12	| `cer/template-tokens` | no unreplaced `__TOKEN__` survives | blocking |
13	| `cer/release-config` | `.github/release.config` present with all 5 required keys, no unknowns | blocking |
14	| `cer/version-sync` | manifest and package.json versions agree | blocking |
15	| `cer/release-layout` | privacy policy source present | blocking |
16	| `cer/privacy-permission-alignment` | every permission disclosed in PRIVACY.md (test the world) | blocking |
17	| `cer/permission-added-store-issue` | added permission → open store-dashboard issue (test the work) | advisory |
18	| `cer/readme-sections` | README has Install + Releasing | blocking |
19	
20	`RELEASE.md` is the full contract (setup and the manual store steps); it is read on demand, not loaded every session. Most of its invariants are enforced by the checks above.
21

1	# Extracting lessons from a working session
2	
3	> **Scope: a single agent ↔ user conversation.** This doc is the **method** for mining one
4	> working session's *dialogue and the actions taken in it* for improvements. It is **not** the
5	> growth extract ([extract.md](extract.md)) that mines **issues, PRs, and commits** over repo
6	> history — that routine never sees a conversation. Its two callers: the
7	> [conversation-extract daily task](conversation-extract.md), which applies it to **captured**
8	> conversation logs — where the friction signals below (a clarifying round-trip, a backtrack, a
9	> wait you sat through) survive with their timestamps — and an owner's occasional plain-words
10	> ask for a retrospective over the live session.
11	
12	Portable guidance for mining an agent ↔ user session — a Claude Code web session, a CLI run, a
13	captured conversation log — for durable improvements: read the conversation and convert its
14	friction into changes that make the **next** run faster, clearer, and less dependent on human
15	feedback.
16	
17	Run the reflection on a **capable model.** Extraction is mechanical, but deciding *what clears
18	the bar* is a judgment call a weaker model fails silently — it ships a plausible-but-wrong
19	"lesson" where a capable model correctly bails (see
20	[the unattended-agents skill](../../skills/unattended-agents/SKILL.md), "Match the agent model
21	to the judgment it must make"). The terse rules that *govern* the pass — run it, plus the
22	separate efficiency analysis — also live there; this doc is the how-to.
23	
24	## The goal
25	
26	Every pass optimizes for one outcome: **the next run needs less.** Less clarification, less
27	rework, less waiting, less human steering. Read the session not as "what did we accomplish" but
28	as "where did this cost more than it should have, and what durable change removes that cost
29	next time." If a pass can't name a concrete next-time saving, it found nothing — and that's
30	fine (see [No new lessons](#no-new-lessons-is-a-valid--and-common--result)).
31	
32	## When it runs
33	
34	Routinely, in the [conversation-extract daily task](conversation-extract.md) over each captured
35	log (a fresh pass within a day of capture, a final hindsight pass before retention deletes the
36	log) — the standard path. If an owner asks in plain words for a retrospective over the live
37	session, apply the same method and deliver the capture as a branch + PR for review, never
38	straight to `main` (the nightly commits direct per its own doc; a live pass doesn't). **Never
39	reflect-and-edit unprompted in the middle of a task** — it interrupts the work; the owner
40	decides when a session is done enough to mine.
41	
42	## What to look for — the friction signals
43	
44	Read the conversation end to end and hunt for these signatures. Each marks a place the loop
45	cost more than necessary, and each converts to a specific durable fix.
46	
47	### 1. Misunderstandings between agent and user
48	
49	The signs: a clarifying-question round-trip; the user correcting an assumption ("no, I
50	meant…"); the agent building the wrong thing and reworking it; the user restating the same
51	request; an `AskUserQuestion` whose answer was, in hindsight, the obvious default.
52	
53	**Convert to:** encode the resolved understanding so the next agent never has to ask. Every
54	question the user answered is a candidate **default**; every correction is a candidate
55	**convention or glossary entry**; a recurring wrong assumption is a candidate **confirm-first
56	checkpoint placed earlier**, or a clearer doc the agent reads cold and gets right the first
57	time. The test of a good fix: next time the agent proceeds correctly **without the round-trip**.
58	
59	### 2. Suboptimal agent actions
60	
61	The signs: the agent took an approach then backtracked; did redundant or repeated work; re-ran
62	a command that had already failed for a knowable reason; reached for a heavy tool where a light
63	one fit; reinvented something the codebase already provided; re-read a file it had just written
64	to "verify"; missed an existing convention and had it corrected in review.
65	
66	**Convert to:** a rule pointing at the right approach or tool, a pointer to the existing
67	helper/convention so it's found first, or a **footgun note placed where it'll be read.** Place
68	it by where the trap is hit, not in a central bucket: a gotcha from **misusing a specific API,
69	class, or library goes as a comment right at the usage site** — next to the call itself, where
70	the next editor of that line sees it — never collected into a centralized gotchas list.
71	Co-locate it in a file's own header comment when the trap spans the whole file and you'd only
72	hit it *while editing that file*. Reserve a central gotchas doc for what no single usage site
73	owns: a trap you could hit *without* reading the relevant file (a mistake of omission), or a
74	cross-cutting invariant. The test: next time the agent takes the good path first, not after a
75	detour.
76	
77	### 3. Long wall-time waits — and measure them
78	
79	The signs: independent operations run **serially** that had no dependency and could have been
80	parallel; a padded fixed `sleep` that over- or under-shot; a process **waited out after its
81	result was already in hand**; blind long polling; re-running a check (e.g. CI) that was already
82	green.
83	
84	**Measure, don't hand-wave.** Quantify the wait with real wall-clock numbers and separate the
85	kinds of time — compute (a compile, a test/render run) vs. genuine **idle waiting** (sleeps,
86	polling, a finished process not yet killed). A captured log carries per-entry timestamps and
87	per-message token usage, so the numbers are computable, not estimated. A fix is only credible
88	against numbers; name the **single highest-leverage change** that shortens it, or conclude it
89	was already optimal.
90	
91	**Convert to:** a batch/parallelize rule, a poll-with-rolling-backoff instead of a blind sleep,
92	kill-the-process-once-its-output-is-in-hand, or merge-on-an-already-green-check. (These are the
93	subject of the standing **efficiency analysis** in
94	[the unattended-agents skill](../../skills/unattended-agents/SKILL.md) — fold a wall-time
95	finding into that frame rather than re-deriving it.) The test: same result, less wall clock, no
96	loss of quality.
97	
98	## Encode the questions the user keeps asking
99	
100	The single highest-leverage move for **less human feedback**: the questions a user asks
101	*repeatedly* are a checklist the agent should run **itself.** If the user keeps asking "how
102	many processes ran — could it be fewer?", "what took longest?", "did you kill it the moment its
103	work was done?", stop waiting to be asked — fold those exact questions into a **standing
104	self-check the agent answers proactively** at the end of the relevant work, and close with a
105	terse verdict (a concrete change, or an explicit "already optimal"). Each recurring question
106	converted this way is one the user never has to ask again — and it's where a *measured*
107	retrospective comes from: the user's recurring efficiency questions, answered pre-emptively,
108	with numbers.
109	
110	## From signal to lesson — the bar
111	
112	A friction signal is only worth writing down when the fix is **durable, reusable, and
113	generalizable beyond this one session.** Before adding anything:
114	
115	- **Dedupe ruthlessly** against the existing packs — a lesson already covered (even worded
116	  differently, even in another pack, even enforced by a check) is not a new lesson.
117	- **Route to the local pack that owns it, and prefer the strongest mechanism** — the **local
118	  promotion ladder**, at the project's own level. A project's capture surface is its **local
119	  packs** (`.claudinite/local_packs/<pack>/`): pick the pack whose territory the lesson belongs
120	  to, then a deterministic rule becomes a **check** in that pack's `rules` (its failure message
121	  *is* the lesson; ship it with a red-first fixture), an activity-scoped procedure becomes a
122	  **pack skill**, and only what neither can carry lands as terse **prose** in the pack's
123	  `RULES.md`. Write more checks and less prose — a check relieves every session's context
124	  completely where prose only relocates it. A gotcha tied to one call site still goes as a
125	  comment at that site (above), not into any pack. When no pack's scope fits and the lesson
126	  warrants segregation, a new local pack is a self-contained directory drop.
127	- **Promote what's portable.** A lesson true for projects beyond this one belongs in shared,
128	  cross-project canon, not stuck in one repo's local packs — the growth lifecycle's promote
129	  stage lifts it there centrally; capture it well locally and let promote generalize whatever
130	  travels.
131	- **One-offs don't qualify.** A situational detail, a restatement of a generic truism, or
132	  something already implied by an existing rule is below the bar.
133	
134	## "No new lessons" is a valid — and common — result
135	
136	Most sessions yield nothing durable, and that's the expected outcome. Say so and write nothing
137	rather than padding the docs to look productive: a spurious or duplicative "lesson" pollutes
138	the canon and costs every future reader. **Default to no edit when unsure** — the bar is a
139	genuinely new, reusable insight, not a diary of what happened.
140
