content.content:
1	---
2	name: git-github-advanced
3	description: Git/GitHub procedures beyond the baseline lifecycle. Use for commit layering, recovering a branch after a squash-merge, CI-trigger rules, GitHub Actions gotchas, or merge-relocation traps.
4	---
5	
6	# Portable git & GitHub procedures
7	
8	The project-agnostic half of how we drive GitHub: the branch/commit-history rules for PR work, the CI-trigger rules, and how we keep merge-conflict churn cheap across parallel branches. (The issue → branch → PR task lifecycle that every task follows lives in [RULES.md](../../packs/basics/RULES.md), the `basics` baseline every project declares and every session loads — it's not task-gated, so it doesn't live here.) Project-specific GitHub procedures (the merge-to-main command, when to open a PR early, the merge-cheaply poll loop tuned to the local environment, and the generated-file merge rules) live in the consuming repo's own GitHub-procedures doc.
9	
10	## Updating an issue's status: comment, don't overwrite
11	
12	To post a **status update** on an issue (the lifecycle's "update the issue's status" step), use `add_issue_comment`. **Don't** reach for `issue_write` with `method: update` — that edits the issue itself and **replaces the whole body**, silently wiping the original description. Reserve `issue_write`/`update` for genuinely editing the issue (retitling, rewriting the body on purpose).
13	
14	## Branch and commit history
15	
16	### Commit often, in layers
17	
18	While working a branch, commit frequently rather than landing one big commit at the end — small, ordered commits let the owner follow the work as it develops. Use commits to *layer* the work in the order you'd want it reviewed:
19	
20	- Write the failing test(s) first, commit them, **then** implement the feature — so the history shows the contract before the code that satisfies it (and you've seen the test fail before trusting it, per [the engineering-practices skill](../engineering-practices/SKILL.md)).
21	- Keep any documentation update as its own commit *after* the feature, not folded into it.
22	
23	There's no cost to a branch carrying many commits when the project uses a **squash** merge to `main` (one commit per PR): the squash collapses them into a single commit on `main`, so `main`'s one-commit-per-PR history is unaffected no matter how granularly the branch is committed.
24	
25	- **One caveat, at merge time: before rebasing an *unmerged* multi-commit branch onto a *moved* base, collapse commits that rewrite the same lines into one.** When your own commits churn the same lines (build X → refactor → remove X, common in an iterative design session) and the base has advanced, rebasing the stack replays *every* intermediate conflict against the new base. The squash-merge discards those commits anyway, so collapse them first — `git reset --soft $(git merge-base HEAD origin/main)` then one fresh commit, and rebase that single commit: one conflict pass, not N. Lossless (unlike `reset --hard` + redo) and simpler than `--onto` when you want a single commit anyway.
26	
27	- Don't rewrite published/shared history to satisfy a tooling or authorship check (e.g. a hook flagging "unverified" commits): only amend your own un-pushed branch commits. Commits already on a shared branch — including ones merged in from `main` — belong to that history; reset-authoring or rebasing them forks your branch away from it.
28	- After your commit is **squash-merged** to `main`, a *reused* feature branch still carries that original commit (the squash created a *new* commit on `main`, so the branch's own is unreachable from it) — and the next PR off the branch re-includes it in the diff, because the three-dot merge-base predates the squash. Sync the branch to `origin/main` before opening the next PR (`git rebase origin/main`, which drops the commit as an already-applied cherry-pick, or a hard reset): it's your own un-merged branch, so this is the amend-your-own-commits case above, not rewriting shared history.
29	  - **`git rebase origin/main` only drops the old commit cleanly when the branch carried a *single* squash-merged commit.** When it carried *several* commits that `main` squashed into *one* (then kept developing), git can't match them to the squash as already-applied, so it replays them and conflicts mid-rebase. Replant only the genuinely-new commits instead: `git rebase --onto origin/main <last-squash-merged-commit>` (then `git push --force-with-lease`). If the new work is small, a `git reset --hard origin/main` + redo beats fighting the replay.
30	  - **If the merge auto-deleted the remote branch, start follow-up work on a new branch off `origin/main`** rather than reusing the old name — it has no stale tracking ref and no rebase dance. If you do reuse the old name: `--force-with-lease` actively *fails* — `git push --force-with-lease` rejects with `stale info` then `couldn't find remote ref <branch>` because the lease expects a remote branch that no longer exists. There's nothing to overwrite, so `git fetch --prune` (drop the stale tracking ref) then a plain `git push -u origin <branch>` just recreates it.
31	
32	## An automated commit-nag is not authorization to commit drift
33	
34	An automated prompt to commit the working tree (a stop-hook, a CI nag) tells you the tree is dirty — not that the changes are yours or intended. Before obeying, inspect what actually changed (`git status` / `git diff`): if it's environment/setup drift — a submodule pointer moved by `git submodule update` at clone time, a lockfile a setup script regenerated, generated artifacts — revert it rather than committing it onto your branch. Committing drift slips an unintended dependency or generated-file bump into an unrelated change.
35	
36	## Open a PR early when the reviewable artifact only exists on CI
37	
38	The default is to hold a PR until asked. Reverse that when a change's only reviewable output is produced by CI — an e2e/heavy-browser run, or a rendered artifact (a UI-snapshot pixel diff, a generated gallery) that can't be exercised in the local sandbox. Opening the PR is how the change is seen working and how failures surface, so doing it up front — rather than iterating locally first, which proves nothing for these classes — is the faster path to a working, reviewable result. Each CI iteration costs a full round-trip, so get the first one running as early as possible.
39	
40	## Sync early to keep merge conflicts small
41	
42	Conflict size scales with how long a branch lives and how far it drifts from the default branch. Sync early rather than at the end: when starting work on a branch — and periodically while it's open — bring the latest default branch in first, so the branch carries current sources instead of discovering the gap at merge time. A one-commit-per-PR squash history already keeps each branch a single reviewable unit, so shorter-lived, freshly-synced branches are the norm.
43	
44	**In a repo that forbids merge commits on the branch — the `squash-merge-history` check, or squash-only merging generally — sync by *rebasing*, never `git merge` the base in.** A `git merge origin/main` (even just to resolve conflicts) leaves a **merge commit** the check blocks, forcing a redo; `git pull --rebase` / `git rebase origin/main` replays your work with no merge commit. GitHub's **"Update branch"** button — and the `update_pull_request_branch` API behind it — is this same base-merge in disguise: it adds exactly the merge commit the check blocks, so refresh with a local rebase + `git push --force-with-lease` instead. The merge-conflict-resolution gotchas below apply to a rebase's conflicts exactly the same way.
45	
46	## After a remote-side merge, fetch before branching off origin/main
47	
48	A GitHub API/UI (or any remote-side) merge does **not** advance your local `origin/main` — it stays at the pre-merge commit until you `git fetch`. Branching off `origin/main` immediately after a remote merge forks the pre-merge state, silently missing the just-merged work; symptoms surface later as a missing file or a failed `git mv` on the new branch. Fix: `git fetch origin main` before creating the branch.
49	
50	## `git pull` failing "refusing to merge unrelated histories" usually means upstream re-rooted
51	
52	When syncing a long-lived local clone's default branch fails with `refusing to merge unrelated histories` (often alongside `ahead N, behind M`), the usual cause is that upstream **re-rooted** the branch — a history rewrite replaced the root commit — not repository corruption. Do **not** reach for `--allow-unrelated-histories`: that "fix" welds the two histories together into a merge nobody wants. Instead inspect what the local side uniquely holds (`git log origin/<branch>..<branch>` — typically just the old root), keep anything real by rebasing it onto the new history, then `git reset --hard origin/<branch>`. A work branch created from *fetched* refs is unaffected; only stale local refs from before the rewrite hit this.
53	
54	## A conformance finding on history you didn't write may be a stale diff base — refresh before fixing
55	
56	A Stop-hook or CI conformance check diffs your branch against `origin/main`. In a fresh cloud sandbox that ref can be **stale** — behind real `main` by whole merged PRs — so the check reads the wrong base and can flag findings on commits or code you never touched. Before acting on a finding you don't recognize, `git fetch origin <default-branch>` and re-run: a stale-base phantom disappears, and whatever survives against the current base is real — fix that. (The same stale ref bases new work on outdated product code, so fetch before building against `main`, too.)
57	
58	The same reflex applies when a **real** (not phantom) red check blocks *your* PR and you've correctly pinned it as pre-existing and unrelated to your diff: that "not mine" diagnosis is a **sync-the-base** signal, not a fix-it-yourself-or-ask-the-user one — `main` has often *already fixed* it since you branched, so merge/rebase the latest base into your branch and re-check before proposing a fix or escalating.
59	
60	## In a squash-merge repo, "commits ahead of main" does not mean "unmerged"
61	
62	A squash-merge creates a new commit on `main` that the branch's own commits are unreachable from, so a branch whose work has already landed still shows ahead by N. **"Ahead by N" never alone means unmerged.** Determine real status by content, not raw count:
63	
64	1. **PR state** — a merged PR means the work is in `main`; safe to delete however many commits show ahead.
65	2. **Content diff** — `git diff --stat main..branch`: if everything the branch adds is already in `main`, the work landed (catches a squash with no surviving PR).
66	3. **Superseded elsewhere** — the branch's work may have landed in `main` under a *different path or form* (a doc distilled into another, a feature re-implemented), so a file/line diff still shows its additions as branch-side and it reads as unmerged. Before concluding so, check whether its *intent* already exists in `main` — grep for the concept, not just the exact path.
67	4. **No merge-base** — `git merge-base` fails when a force-push rewrote `main` and orphaned the branch; can't be proven in or out mechanically; needs human review, never an automatic delete.
68	5. Otherwise — genuine unmerged work.
69	
70	## A push or PR made with the Actions `GITHUB_TOKEN` does not start another workflow
71	
72	GitHub suppresses workflow runs triggered by the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` to prevent recursion, so a workflow's own `git push` or `gh pr create` won't fire another workflow (e.g. a `test` or cache-refresh workflow). The one exception is `workflow_dispatch` / `repository_dispatch` — which is why an automation pipeline that needs the downstream checks to run must dispatch them explicitly. A run dispatched against a branch executes on its head commit, so its checks still attach to the PR. The same suppression covers a Claude session's own app-scoped pushes: creating a PR through the API does fire its `pull_request` run, but a follow-up push to that PR gets **no** `synchronize` run — so don't wait for checks that will never start on the new head; gate by running the CI commands locally, and rely on the post-merge run (merging through the merge API does fire the `push` workflow on the default branch).
73	
74	## A workflow definition only takes effect once it's on the default branch
75	
76	A `workflow_dispatch` or scheduled workflow runs from the copy on the **default branch**, and a `pull_request` run uses the workflow files from the PR's **base** branch — so a workflow you only added on a feature branch isn't dispatchable, and a build/CI change doesn't apply, until it's merged. Merge workflow and build-script changes *before* the release or run that depends on them, or that run executes the *old* definition (shipping a stale build, or silently skipping a check you thought you'd added). This is distinct from the `GITHUB_TOKEN` recursion rule above: even a human-triggered run reads the definition from the default/base branch, not your feature branch.
77	
78	## A CI OIDC `sub` claim is matched case-sensitively by the cloud trust policy
79	
80	GitHub's OIDC `sub` uses the repository's **canonical casing** (`repo:Owner/Repo:ref:…`), and a cloud trust policy compares it exactly — AWS IAM `StringEquals` is case-sensitive — so a trust policy written with the wrong case fails with a bare `Not authorized to perform sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` and **no** hint that casing is the cause. Match the exact canonical `owner/repo` (or list both casings in the condition). The same exact-match caution applies to any OIDC-federated cloud, not just AWS.
81	
82	## Gate an optional CI job on a repo variable, not a secret
83	
84	`secrets.*` are not available in a job-level `if:`, so a job gated on a secret can't evaluate its condition and **fails (red)** instead of skipping. Put a non-sensitive flag (a deploy-role ARN, a feature toggle) in a repository **variable** and gate with `if: ${{ vars.X != '' }}` so the job is **skipped (neutral)** until it's configured — keeping the default branch green for anyone who hasn't set the integration up. Reserve secrets for the values consumed *inside* the job's steps.
85	
86	## An automated job needs a unique branch per run
87	
88	An automated or scheduled job that derives its branch name from a non-unique key (e.g. the date) collides with itself on a repeat run for that key — `git checkout -b` fails when the branch already exists, and a push to the diverged remote branch is rejected non-fast-forward (so the run can't even open its PR). Give every run its own branch: append a per-run-unique suffix (`$RANDOM` / a short token) to the readable prefix.
89	
90	## A workflow that adds a brand-new label must create it first
91	
92	`gh issue edit --add-label "<name>"` fails when the label doesn't exist yet — unlike applying an already-defined label, GitHub won't create it on demand, so a workflow that introduces a new label breaks the first time it runs. Create it idempotently before the edit (`gh label create "<name>" --color … 2>/dev/null || true`), then `--add-label`.
93	
94	## A GitHub Actions `run:` step's default shell has no `pipefail`
95	
96	GitHub's implicit default run-shell is `bash -e {0}` — **without** `pipefail` — so a step piping through another command (e.g. `cmd 2>&1 | tee log`) reports the *last* command's exit code, not the piped command's: a failing command still shows the step green. Set `defaults.run.shell: bash` (job- or step-level), which GitHub runs as `bash --noprofile --norc -eo pipefail {0}`, so the step fails when any command in the pipe fails.
97	
98	## An unattended workflow must escalate its own failure to a human-visible state
99	
100	A workflow with no human watching the run — scheduled, triggered by a push/merge, or a fire-and-forget manual dispatch, with no other path that reaches a person — must converge a failure to something a human will see (e.g. open a `workflow-failure` issue for it) rather than merely exiting red in the Actions list, where nobody looks. Skip this only when the failure is already loud: a `pull_request`/push CI run that blocks merge with a red required check the author is watching, or a workflow that already flags the triggering issue itself on failure. The canon's [report-failure](../../.github/actions/report-failure/action.yml) action does exactly this — a **fresh** issue per failure, with earlier open failure issues for the same workflow closed as duplicates of the newest — so the current failure is always the single open bug to triage; a standing per-workflow issue appended to on each failure is an equally valid shape, the invariant is only that the red run reaches a person.
101	
102	When the escalation is itself a separate reusable workflow invoked via `workflow_call`, permissions don't propagate implicitly through the chain: the job that calls it needs the permission explicitly granted (e.g. `issues: write`), and if that reusable workflow in turn calls another one, the middle workflow must forward the same grant to its own call — a caller two levels up granting it once is not enough.
103	
104	## A CI job that reads submodule files must fetch submodules in its checkout
105	
106	`actions/checkout` does **not** fetch submodules by default — the submodule directory is an empty folder in CI unless you pass `submodules: true` (or `recurse-submodules: true`). Without it, any gate that reads submodule content passes vacuously: the check is a no-op, not a signal. Add the flag to every CI job whose tests read submodule content.
107	
108	## A CI job that inspects commit shape must check out the PR head, not the merge ref
109	
110	On `pull_request`, `actions/checkout` checks out the synthetic `refs/pull/N/merge` commit by default — an ephemeral merge of the PR head into the base — so `HEAD` is itself a merge commit, not the branch tip. Any job that reasons about commit *shape* or history (merge commits, commit count, `--first-parent`, commit messages) reads that synthetic merge and misfires: a "no merge commits" check false-positives even on a linear branch, since `HEAD` is a merge. Check out the head SHA (`ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha || github.sha }}`) so CI evaluates the same branch tip a local run does.
111	
112	## To read a PR's CI result, look at its check runs — `get_status` misses Actions
113	
114	GitHub **Actions** reports results as **check runs**, not the legacy **commit statuses**, so `pull_request_read` `method: get_status` returns `state: pending`, `total_count: 0` for a PR whose Actions CI has already **passed** — reading falsely as "no CI / not started," which can skip a real gate or trigger an endless wait. Gate on CI by reading the **check runs for the PR head SHA** (`get_check_run`, or the workflow run for that SHA) instead. Target the head SHA directly rather than `actions_list`-ing the repo's runs — that returns every run, its output is huge, and a specific check-run/SHA query is both correct and cheap.
115	
116	## To confirm a non-PR run (push / dispatch), read its job logs — it has no PR check runs
117	
118	A `push` or `workflow_dispatch` run isn't attached to a PR, so the PR-scoped check-run query above doesn't apply to it. Confirm such a run through the GitHub API/MCP tools: `get_job_logs(run_id, failed_only: true)` — "0 failed jobs" means green — or, for a release build, `get_release_by_tag`. Don't `curl` the run's status instead: in a sandboxed session `api.github.com` is proxy-blocked and returns an error body that never matches a success pattern, so a `curl`/`Monitor` poll silently reports "still running" until it times out.
119	
120	## Mark large committed fixtures `linguist-vendored` to fix language stats
121	
122	Large committed fixture files (full-page HTML, generated data dumps) can dwarf actual source by byte count and cause GitHub to mislabel the repo's primary language. Add a `.gitattributes` entry for each such path (e.g. `test/fixtures/*.html linguist-vendored`) to tell Linguist to ignore it; apply the same annotation whenever you add another large generated or fixture file.
123	
124	## Renaming a directory that houses a submodule
125	
126	`git mv` on a directory containing a submodule rewrites `.gitmodules` and the index correctly but leaves `.git/config` stale — its old `[submodule "<old/path>"]` entry lingers, so any operation that consults `.git/config` (submodule status, checkout) sees the old path until you fix it. Run `git submodule sync && git submodule update --init` after the move to propagate the new path and re-register the submodule.
127	
128	## GitHub Markdown inside a `<td>` requires surrounding blank lines
129	
130	In a GitHub-rendered Markdown file, cmark-gfm re-enters Markdown mode inside a raw `<td>` only when blank lines surround the cell's content — without them the cell is treated as a raw HTML block and its content is shown verbatim (no `![img]()`, no `**bold**`, no links). GitHub's sanitizer strips `style` / CSS, so a flexbox two-column layout won't render; use a plain `<table>` with `align` / `valign` / `width` instead. GFM pipe-table cells can't hold multi-line prose — use the raw-`<table>` form when a cell needs it. A leading inline `<!-- … -->` comment also opens an HTML block, so keep any such marker as the *last* token on the line, leaving the line to *start* as Markdown.
131	
132	## `commit.gpgsign = true` does not cover merge commits
133	
134	`commit.gpgsign = true` signs ordinary commits but does not apply to `git merge` — each merge produces an unsigned commit even with signing fully configured. In a repo that enforces commit verification, this requires a fix-up after the fact. Cover merges proactively: either pass `-S` to every `git merge` call, or set `merge.gpgsign = true` globally so merges are signed the same way commits are.
135	
136	## When access is scoped to an explicit repo list, query per-repo — never an org/user-wide search
137	
138	A broad call (e.g. `search_repositories` with `org:X`, or any list/search tool that takes no repo argument) returns every repo the token can see, not just an allowed subset — filtering the result afterward doesn't undo the fact that disallowed repos' data was already pulled into the call. When operating under a repo allowlist, scope every call explicitly instead: pass the specific `owner`/`repo` params, or anchor the query to `repo:owner/name`, one call per repo in the allowlist rather than one broad call filtered after the fact.
139	
140	## Merging gotchas
141	
142	These conflict/merge traps are independent of any one project's file layout.
143	
144	### Merging across a file relocation
145	
146	git's rename detection re-applies your content edits onto the moved files, but it does *not* fix *references* to the moved paths — an npm script, a `.gitattributes` glob, or a doc link naming the old location keeps pointing there and breaks with no conflict (a green local test on the old layout won't catch it either). After such a merge, grep the files your branch touched for the old paths.
147	
148	### Merging in content that predates a branch-wide invariant
149	
150	When your branch establishes a cross-cutting invariant — a renamed term, a "mentioned only in X" containment rule — a branch you merge in that *predates* it can silently reintroduce violations: its independent additions (a new file, a new section) auto-merge clean, no conflict, while still using the old form. Resolving the marked conflicts isn't enough. Re-run the check that *defines* the invariant (the grep) over the whole tree after the merge, not just the conflicted files.
151	
152	### Porting old work forward across a changed invariant
153	
154	The converse direction: when you re-implement or cherry-pick an *older* change onto current `main`, check each part against the invariants `main` has added since — not just whether it still applies. A part that violates a now-enforced guarantee becomes a **silent no-op** if taken verbatim (e.g. a guard that reverts any write outside an allowed set leaves the patch landed but doing nothing), so drop or redesign it rather than copy it.
155

1	# Adopting Claudinite
2	
3	How a consuming repo adopts these shared guidelines, under the **vendored mount**
4	([mount/DESIGN.md](mount/DESIGN.md)): adoption is the **one network moment** — fetch the canon
5	once, vendor what this repo needs into **tracked files** under `.claudinite/shared/`, wire the
6	hooks — and every session after runs **offline** from the committed snapshot. The nightly
7	maintenance is the only regular updater. Idempotent: re-running refreshes the snapshot exactly
8	like a nightly would (fetch → converge → stamp) and never clobbers your own config — the
9	`@`-import line and your other `settings.json` entries are only added where missing.
10	
11	> **Members adopted before the vendored mount** (the tracked sync hook, gitignored corpus,
12	> per-session fetch): do **not** re-run this document to convert — conversion is the gated flip
13	> note the nightly applies. Until a member is flipped, the nightly maintains its legacy shape
14	> per the [transition appendix](#appendix--pre-flip-members-transition-window-retiring).
15	
16	## Part 1 — fetch the canon (the network moment)
17	
18	```sh
19	scratch="$(mktemp -d)"
20	curl -fsSL https://codeload.github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/tar.gz/main \
21	  | tar -xz --strip-components=1 -C "$scratch"
22	ref="$(git ls-remote https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite.git main 2>/dev/null | cut -f1)"
23	```
24	
25	Adoption (and an on-demand refresh) is the only time a consumer fetches — sessions never do, so
26	the environment's network policy no longer needs `codeload.github.com` for day-to-day work.
27	
28	## Part 2 — write the pack declaration (and run the adoption interview)
29	
30	```sh
31	node "$scratch/checks/run.mjs" --init
32	```
33	
34	`--init` seeds `.claudinite-checks.json`: the baseline, the technology packs the repo's
35	fingerprint suspects, the default-on maintenance packs, each declared pack's `requires` closure,
36	and `"maintenance": { "delivery": "push" }`. A fingerprint only *suspects* a pack — from here on
37	the declaration is authoritative and adding/dropping packs is the project's call. Settings
38	**validity** is enforced at load: an unknown pack name, an unknown property, or malformed JSON is
39	a blocking `config` error.
40	
41	If `--init` prints **pending adoption questions**, run the adoption interview now — this is the
42	one strict point (the owner is present by construction; outside bootstrap the same gap only ever
43	surfaces as a mild SessionStart note — [packs/README.md](packs/README.md#adoption-interview-questions)):
44	ask the owner each question via `AskUserQuestion`, record each answer **verbatim** on that pack's
45	entry as `answers: { "<question-id>": "<answer>" }` ("n/a — none wanted" is a valid answer), and
46	derive the entry's `config` where the question's distill note says how.
47	
48	## Part 3 — vendor the snapshot
49	
50	```sh
51	node "$scratch/mount/apply-vendor.mjs" --target . ${ref:+--ref "$ref"}
52	```
53	
54	This materializes the repo's vendor set — the engine, the mount, the declared packs with their
55	skills, the corpus index — under `.claudinite/shared/` at canon-relative paths, and stamps the
56	declaration (`"claudinite": { "updated": "YYYY-MM-DD", "ref": "<sha>" }`). Whole-set convergence:
57	re-running it (or declaring a new pack and re-running) rebuilds the tree; errors abort before any
58	write. The `shared/` root is a **submodule emulation** — a future `git submodule add … .claudinite/shared`
59	lands a superset at the same path with no wiring change (see the design doc).
60	
61	## Part 4 — track it
62	
63	```sh
64	for rule in '/.claudinite/*' '!/.claudinite/shared/' '!/.claudinite/local_packs/' '/.claudinite-hooks.log' '/.claudinite-hooks.log.tmp'; do
65	  grep -qxF "$rule" .gitignore 2>/dev/null || echo "$rule" >> .gitignore
66	done
67	git add .gitignore .claudinite-checks.json .claudinite/shared
68	```
69	
70	`.claudinite/*` stays ignored **wholesale with re-includes** — only `shared/` (the vendored
71	canon) and `local_packs/` (the repo's own packs) are tracked — so legacy flat leftovers or a
72	stale environment's stray sync stay invisible instead of appearing as untracked noise.
73	
74	## Part 5 — wire the hooks
75	
76	Claude Code runs `SessionStart` entries **in parallel, in non-deterministic order**, so anything
77	sequenced must live inside **one** entry. Register exactly one — the orchestrator, which runs
78	the context steps in sequence in a single process (preferences → active-pack prose → skill
79	mounts → env check → interview check) and forwards their stdout into the session context:
80	
81	```json
82	{ "hooks": { "SessionStart": [ { "hooks": [
83	  { "type": "command", "command": "bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/mount/session-start.sh" }
84	] } ] } }
85	```
86	
87	Register the Stop hook (runs the conformance sweep when the session changed something; blocks
88	the stop while blocking findings remain) and the PreToolUse guard (deterministically blocks
89	forbidden commands) alongside it:
90	
91	```json
92	{ "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "hooks": [
93	  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/checks/stop-hook.mjs" }
94	] } ],
95	  "PreToolUse": [ { "matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [
96	  { "type": "command", "command": "node $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/shared/checks/pretooluse-guard.mjs" }
97	] } ] } }
98	```
99	
100	Invoke scripts **through `bash`/`node`**, never as bare paths — a dropped exec bit would fail the
101	hook before line 1 and swallow its own message. Notes on how the steps behave:
102	
103	- **Preferences are fail-soft** — per-user content is never vendored;
104	  `shared/mount/inject-preferences.sh` reads a local copy where the tree has one and otherwise
105	  fetches the single `preferences/<email>.md`; any miss is a one-line note, and the session
106	  proceeds on defaults.
107	- **The halt-gate** — a SessionStart hook cannot block, but its stdout is injected into context,
108	  so a step that can't do its load-bearing job (`env.mjs check` — a missing toolchain) prints a
109	  plain-text directive telling the assistant to STOP and confirm via `AskUserQuestion` before any
110	  work. Plain text always — one hook's stdout must never mix JSON and prose.
111	- **The durable hook log** — every hook appends `start` / `done exit=N` lines to
112	  `.claudinite-hooks.log` at the repo root. No lines ⇒ the hook never triggered; `start` without
113	  `done` ⇒ it died executing. Reach for it first when a session says the harness didn't load.
114	- **Skill mounts are session-generated, never committed** — `shared/skills/mount-skills.mjs`
115	  (an orchestrator step) regenerates `.claude/skills/<name>` symlinks for the declared packs'
116	  union each session and maintains a self-ignoring `.gitignore` there; a committed link would
117	  dangle on every plain checkout.
118	
119	## Part 6 — import the corpus index
120	
121	```sh
122	grep -qxF '@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md' CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null \
123	  || printf '\n@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md\n' >> CLAUDE.md
124	grep -qF 'Claudinite self-check' CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null \
125	  || printf '\n> Claudinite self-check: if the `@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md` import above did not resolve (no `.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md` in this checkout), the Claudinite harness is **not active** this session — a broken or partial checkout. Treat it as not loaded and confirm with the user before substantive work.\n' >> CLAUDE.md
126	```
127	
128	The self-check lives in the consumer's own tracked `CLAUDE.md` — the one file always in context —
129	so the assistant has a tell that is independent of every hook.
130	
131	One standing rule the vendored tree does **not** change: committed consumer code must not
132	`import`/`require` canon helpers from `.claudinite/` — the canon is refreshed nightly and
133	refactored upstream, so code reaching into it inherits every rename as a breaking change. Inline
134	what you need. The `claudinite-isolation` check enforces this outside the wiring files.
135	
136	## Part 7 — the CI backstop
137	
138	Hooks fire only in Claude Code sessions; CI catches edits made anywhere else — same sweep, same
139	messages, from the **same committed snapshot** the sessions judge by:
140	
141	```sh
142	mkdir -p .github/workflows
143	cp .claudinite/shared/packs/basics/stubs/claudinite-checks-ci.yml .github/workflows/claudinite-checks-ci.yml
144	git add .github/workflows/claudinite-checks-ci.yml
145	```
146	
147	## Part 8 — request fleet enrollment (open one tracking issue)
148	
149	A consuming project schedules nothing: the growth lifecycle and nightly maintenance run
150	centrally, from the owner's home repo — but only over repos on the routine's access list. So, as
151	part of a **first** adoption, open a GitHub issue in this repo's tracker assigned to
152	`missingbulb`, titled exactly **`Enroll <PROJECT_NAME> in Claudinite fleet maintenance`**
153	(idempotent: search first, skip if one exists, open or closed). Under the vendored mount this
154	matters more, not less: an unenrolled repo's snapshot simply freezes until someone refreshes it.
155	When the fleet's sweep baselines a repo it already maintains, it closes any still-open enrollment
156	issue — being reached proves enrollment.
157	
158	## Part 9 — categorize the project (declare its class pack)
159	
160	**Only for a fresh / empty project** — one without its own established working style. The owner
161	runs recurring **classes** of project, each carried by a project-class pack:
162	
163	1. Ask the owner which class this project is, offering the project-class packs under
164	   [`packs/`](packs/) as the options.
165	2. A class pack fits → add its id to `"packs"` in `.claudinite-checks.json` and re-run Part 3
166	   (a declaration change triggers a whole-set refresh, so the new pack's content lands).
167	3. No class pack fits → run the project-instructions skill: it decomposes the project into pack
168	   facets and extracts its working instructions into new/refined canon packs (the primary
169	   deliverable) plus a thin project-specific overlay.
170	
171	## Part 10 — land the adoption green
172	
173	Run the sweep once and clear what it surfaces:
174	
175	```sh
176	node .claudinite/shared/checks/run.mjs
177	```
178	
179	On a repo with existing code, **expect a backlog** — enforcement scope is whole-repo, and
180	findings in code you never touched would otherwise block every future session's Stop hook and
181	CI. Fix causes, or record a reasoned `accept` in `.claudinite-checks.json` for the deliberate
182	keeps. Don't reach for `--changed` to hide the backlog — it is a transitional aid, never the
183	enforcement default. Commit the adoption as one change (the vendored tree, the declaration, the
184	wiring) and push it through the normal PR flow.
185	
186	## Part 11 — cloud environment setup (Claude Code on the web)
187	
188	The web base image ships no toolchains; installs belong in the environment **image** (built
189	once, snapshotted), not a per-session hook. The corpus holds the one generic script —
190	[`mount/environment-setup.sh`](mount/environment-setup.sh), vendored into
191	`.claudinite/shared/mount/` — identical for every project: paste its full body into the
192	environment's **Setup script** field and rebuild. It runs each active pack's declared installs
193	(`env.mjs install`, driven by the declaration); the SessionStart `env.mjs check` then only
194	*probes* and halt-gates on a genuinely missing prerequisite. The network policy must reach what
195	the active packs install from (npm registry, `pub.dev`, …) — the corpus itself is already in the
196	checkout.
197	
198	## Bespoke merge policy (optional)
199	
200	The portable merge recipe ships as the merge skill and needs nothing from you (squash via PR,
201	gate on CI only when the repo has it). Only if your project genuinely diverges: put the policy in
202	its own file and **name it explicitly in your `CLAUDE.md`** — the recipe reads it only then.
203	
204	---
205	
206	## Appendix — pre-flip members (transition window, retiring)
207	
208	Maintenance shapes for members still on the **legacy fetch-at-session-start mount** (tracked
209	sync hook at `.claudinite/mount/sync-claudinite.sh`, gitignored synced corpus, flat
210	`.claudinite/` paths, `@.claudinite/CLAUDE.md` import). The nightly baselining applies **only
211	this appendix** to them — never the fresh path above; conversion to the vendored mount is the
212	gated flip note's job ([mount/DESIGN.md](mount/DESIGN.md), phase 2). The whole appendix is
213	deleted in phase 3, once the fleet has flipped.
214	
215	- **Sync hook refresh** — the tracked hook is a generated artifact the canon owns: overwrite the
216	  member's copy with the canon's current [`mount/sync-claudinite.sh`](mount/sync-claudinite.sh);
217	  never hand-edit or inline a copy. Its `settings.json` registration stays the single
218	  `SessionStart` entry, invoked through `bash`
219	  (`bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claudinite/mount/sync-claudinite.sh`); fix in place an entry
220	  pointing at a legacy path (`.claude/hooks/…`, pre-mount `.claudinite/sync-claudinite.sh`) or
221	  invoking a bare path, and delete redundant standalone entries for the orchestrator's steps.
222	- **Legacy hook paths** — Stop `node …/.claudinite/checks/stop-hook.mjs`, PreToolUse
223	  `node …/.claudinite/checks/pretooluse-guard.mjs`, import `@.claudinite/CLAUDE.md` (flat, no
224	  `shared/`).
225	- **Legacy gitignore set** — `/.claudinite/*`, `!/.claudinite/mount/`, `/.claudinite/mount/*`,
226	  `!/.claudinite/mount/sync-claudinite.sh`, `!/.claudinite/local_packs/`, `/.claudinite.new/`,
227	  plus the hooks-log ignores; drop a bare `.claudinite/` wholesale-ignore (it blocks the
228	  negations) and the retired `.gitkeep` / pre-mount-hook negations.
229	- **Legacy file cleanup** (idempotent): `git rm` a hook at `.claude/hooks/sync-claudinite.sh`,
230	  a `.claudinite/.gitkeep` marker, and any committed `.claude/skills/*` symlink pointing into
231	  `.claudinite/skills/`.
232	- **Declaration backfills** (idempotent; `--init` covers fresh files): materialize a missing
233	  explicit `"basics"` entry; seed the default-on packs (`tidy-repo`, `grow_with_claudinite`)
234	  **only while each one's seed migration file is still present** in the canon (never re-add
235	  after the seed retires); re-run `resolveDeclaredPacks` so `requires` closures and `via` stay
236	  accurate; fold a legacy top-level `packConfig` into pack-entry `config`; materialize a missing
237	  `"maintenance": { "delivery": "push" }`.
238	- **Environment prerequisite** — the legacy mount fetches at every session start, so these
239	  members still need `codeload.github.com` allowlisted in their environment's network policy.
240

1	---
2	name: adopt-claudinite
3	description: Bootstrap Claudinite into a consuming repo — mount, hooks, checks, skills. Use when asked to bootstrap, adopt, or set up Claudinite, or to baseline a repo to pick up updates.
4	---
5	
6	Follow [bootstrap.md](../../bootstrap.md) — canonical there, and idempotent by design. Under the
7	vendored mount it is: fetch the canon once (the one network moment), `--init` the pack
8	declaration + run the adoption interview, vendor the snapshot into tracked
9	`.claudinite/shared/` (`mount/apply-vendor.mjs` — whole-set + stamp), track it, register the
10	single SessionStart orchestrator plus the Stop/PreToolUse hooks at their `shared/` paths, add
11	the `@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md` import + self-check, copy the CI backstop stub, open the
12	maintenance-enrollment issue, categorize the project, and land the sweep green.
13	
14	**Refreshing** an already-vendored repo on demand is the same flow minus the wiring: fetch a
15	fresh canon to scratch, run its `mount/apply-vendor.mjs` against the repo, commit the one
16	resulting change. **Never** hand-convert a legacy (fetch-at-session-start) member to the
17	vendored mount — that conversion is the gated flip note the nightly applies
18	([mount/DESIGN.md](../../mount/DESIGN.md), phase 2); until then legacy members are maintained
19	per bootstrap.md's transition appendix.

1	# Making changes to Claudinite without hurting consumers
2	
3	> **Status: provisional — beliefs, not law.** Every entry here says what we're *currently* doing
4	> and *why we think* it helps. When an entry blocks a better change, challenge the entry rather
5	> than contorting the change; being cited in this guide is not, by itself, a reason something
6	> must stay. We'll solidify entries once they've earned it.
7	
8	Claudinite is mounted by every consuming repo, so a change here lands in many places, on those
9	repos' schedules, usually with no human watching. This guide collects what we currently believe
10	about making such changes safely. It is a maintainer document for sessions working *in* this
11	repo; it is not part of the mounted corpus.
12	
13	## First, know which channel the change travels
14	
15	We think the most useful first question is *how* a change reaches consumers, because each
16	channel has a different blast radius and a different rollback story:
17	
18	- **Canon reusable workflows and composite actions** (`.github/workflows/`, `.github/actions/`)
19	  — consumers reference them `@main`, so a merge here reaches every consumer's **next run
20	  instantly**, unattended, with no pin to hide behind. This is the highest-blast-radius channel.
21	- **Checks and pack prose** (`packs/`) — executed/loaded from the mount; tarball-sync consumers
22	  pick them up at their next session start, submodule consumers at their next pointer bump. A
23	  wrong *blocking* check fails consumers' Stop hook and CI — visible and recoverable, but
24	  fleet-wide noise, so treat check changes as production changes.
25	- **Copied artifacts** (pack `stubs/`, seeded configs) — these do **not** propagate at all. Each
26	  consumer holds its own copy, which drifts silently unless a check enforces its shape. Changing
27	  the source file alone only affects repos bootstrapped *after* the change.
28	- **Bootstrap wiring** (the committed artifacts [bootstrap.md](bootstrap.md) seeds into each
29	  consumer: the tracked sync hook, `settings.json` hook registrations, gitignore rules, the
30	  pack declaration) — propagates through the **nightly baselining**, which
31	  re-runs the idempotent bootstrap on every member and commits drift **directly to its default
32	  branch**, no PR. Fleet-wide but lagging one nightly; the rollback story is the same channel
33	  (fix the canon, the next nightly re-heals). The discipline: a change here is written as
34	  bootstrap steps that **converge from every layout in the wild** — fresh, current, legacy,
35	  half-migrated — not just from the latest.
36	- **Skills** (`skills/`) — surfaced on demand from the mount; same propagation as prose, but
37	  only sessions doing the matching activity ever read them.
38	
39	## Migrating a copied artifact (stubs and the like)
40	
41	Because copies won't move on their own, we currently migrate them through the conformance
42	checks, in one Claudinite-only change:
43	
44	1. Change the source artifact in the pack (the stub under `packs/<pack>/stubs/`).
45	2. Make sure the pack's conformance check enforces the properties that changed, with a `fix`
46	   that says how to align (usually "re-copy the stub"). A consumer's own next session or CI run
47	   then raises a blocking finding and performs the migration in that repo — no per-repo PR from
48	   here.
49	3. Keep the pack's `detect` fingerprint **tolerant of the legacy shape** while copies migrate
50	   (e.g. a `LEGACY_*` list beside the current constant), so a fresh `--init` still recognizes a
51	   not-yet-migrated repo and seeds the pack. (The fingerprint only *suspects* a pack — it no
52	   longer drives any drop/keep finding — so a stale shape can't contradict the conformance
53	   check's *fix-the-stub* remedy; the tolerance is just to keep detection working during the
54	   migration window.)
55	
56	Fleet-wide "everyone has migrated" telemetry now exists — declare the rename as a
57	[baseline migration](migrations/README.md), **in the same change that introduces the tolerance**: a
58	tolerance that lands without its record has no signal that will ever retire it, and the gap only
59	resurfaces as a review question later. And not only for renames — any legacy shape a tolerance keeps
60	readable (an old path, a stub's old form, a legacy key *inside* a consumer's config file) is trackable
61	the same way, `legacyPresent` reading whatever expresses the old shape. The fleet-coverage census probes every repo for the legacy
62	shape and **auto-retires** the migration (deletes its record) once none remain, so dropping a fully-applied
63	tolerance is no longer a manual judgment call. While a migration's tolerance still lives inline
64	(readers not yet consulting its `resolvePath`), keep it `retire:'manual'` and drop it deliberately;
65	once every reader is resolver-driven, `retire:'auto'` lets the census drop the record — and with it the
66	tolerance — automatically.
67	
68	## Relocating wiring that consumers reference by path
69	
70	What we learned moving the Method B sync hook from `.claude/hooks/` into `.claudinite/`
71	(#211/#213): the file is only half the change — every *reference* to its old path is itself a
72	consumer-held copy that won't move on its own, and each needs a channel.
73	
74	- **Enumerate the reference holders before touching the file** — `settings.json` hook commands,
75	  other canon scripts, the fleet routine's discovery probe, docs — then grep for the old path
76	  afterward (the text-sweep skill owns the mechanics). Include state that lives
77	  **outside the repo entirely**: a web environment's pasted Setup script is snapshotted at
78	  environment build, where no check, sync, or baselining can ever reach it.
79	- **Never break the channel the migration itself travels through.** Fleet discovery finds
80	  members by the tracked `.claudinite/` signal; when the signal file itself relocated, the
81	  discovery probe had to accept both shapes. A probe that recognizes only the new shape doesn't
82	  make noise like a wrong check — it **silently and permanently orphans** every unmigrated repo,
83	  since discovery is what reaches them to migrate them. Same for the settings registration: a
84	  session between canon merge and nightly migration still runs the old wiring, so the old layout
85	  must keep working until the baselining lands.
86	- **References nothing can reach get tolerance in the artifact instead** — the relocated script
87	  tries the new layout first and falls back to the legacy path, and the existing SessionStart
88	  probe/halt-gate asks for the one human action (re-paste) when a stale out-of-repo copy finally
89	  misfires. Prefer a probe that *detects* staleness over hoping the stale copy keeps working.
90	- **A canon-owned file tracked in consumer git updates on the commit channel, not the sync
91	  channel.** The session sync preserves the consumer's tracked copy over the tarball's newer
92	  one — otherwise every canon edit dirties every consumer's working tree until the nightly
93	  commits it. Clean trees each session; baselining's direct commit is the update path.
94	- **Verify by replaying the migration on a simulated legacy consumer** before merging: build the
95	  old layout in a scratch git repo, run the documented steps verbatim, and assert the tree is
96	  clean both right after the migration and after a subsequent real session sync. This is the
97	  wiring-channel analog of "verify against a real consumer before the nightly does".
98	
99	## A vendored file executes in the consumer's context
100	
101	Canon files that ship into consumers — the whole vendor set (`mount/`, `checks/`, pack machinery
102	and stubs) — run at their *landed* path inside the consumer's repo, not in the canon checkout they
103	were written and tested in. Before changing one, audit every environment assumption against each
104	context it ships to: **git discovers `.git` by walking upward**, so introspection from a vendored
105	copy answers with the *consumer's* repo unless constrained to the expected toplevel (the
106	`--show-toplevel` guard in `mount/apply-vendor.mjs` — an unconstrained `rev-parse HEAD` nearly
107	stamped a consumer sha as canon provenance, #340); relative paths resolve against the vendored
108	location; network access and credentials present where the file was written may be absent where it
109	lands. The co-located header comment at the usage site carries each specific trap; this entry is
110	the class.
111	
112	## Normalize requirements to canonical form at the door
113	
114	When a requirement arrives expressed in someone's local context — a schedule in a local
115	timezone, a path from one repo's layout, a phrasing tied to one project's vocabulary — we
116	currently translate it to its canonical form **once, on the way in**, and record only the
117	result. A "nightly at 3:30 my time" request lands in the canon as a fixed UTC cron, stated in
118	UTC and nothing else. The reasoning: the person who asked can carry the original framing;
119	consumers shouldn't inherit it, and the canon shouldn't have to re-explain a conversion (or its
120	edge cases, like DST) forever. If the origin matters for revisiting the decision, it belongs in
121	the issue/PR that made the change, not in the shipped artifact.
122	
123	## Changing the canon workflows themselves
124	
125	Since `@main` references make these instant and fleet-wide, we currently lean on:
126	
127	- **Additive, backward-compatible edits** — a new input gets a default; an output nobody
128	  consumes yet is free; renames and removals get the two-phase treatment above.
129	- **Verify against a real consumer before the nightly does** — a manual `workflow_dispatch` on
130	  one consuming repo is cheap; the scheduled run exercising a bad merge in every repo at once is
131	  not. The failure reporter turning red runs into issues is the safety net, not the plan.
132

1	# Claudinite
2	
3	Portable Claude instructions/rules shared across projects — the **project-agnostic** maintainer guidelines, maintained **once** here and mounted into each consuming repo so they don't have to be copy-pasted per project. Project-*specific* rules stay in the consuming repo's own docs.
4	
5	**Just adding Claudinite?** Tell the agent on the consuming repo:
6	
7	> Read https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/blob/main/README.md and bootstrap it.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## For the reading agent: how to traverse this corpus
12	
13	**The agent-facing index lives in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md), not here.** It is the map of the corpus — two homes selected by *when* a rule is active: **`packs/<name>/`** (prose + checks, active once per session by the project's `.claudinite-checks.json` declaration — no pack is active undeclared, the baseline included; the active packs' prose is injected by a SessionStart hook) and **`skills/<name>/`** (activity-scoped procedures the harness surfaces on demand). Consumers mount it as `@.claudinite/CLAUDE.md`; an agent working in this repo loads it as the repo's own `CLAUDE.md`. Start there.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## How consuming repos join
18	
19	Consumers hold a **vendored, tracked** snapshot of the corpus at `.claudinite/shared/` — their
20	declaration-derived subset, committed as ordinary files, refreshed by the nightly maintenance as
21	one transactional commit, and imported with `@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md` from the consumer's
22	`CLAUDE.md` (SessionStart hooks inject the active packs' prose on top, all offline). Adoption is
23	the one network moment. The model, its trade-offs, and the fleet transition from the earlier
24	fetch-at-session-start mount → [mount/DESIGN.md](mount/DESIGN.md); **setup steps →
25	[bootstrap.md](bootstrap.md)** (members on the legacy mount are converted by the gated flip
26	note, not by hand — bootstrap's transition appendix maintains them meanwhile).
27	
28	## Repository operations
29	
30	Beyond the portable corpus above, two folders hold the machinery that keeps it fed and tidy — Claudinite-internal orchestration, **not** part of the mounted corpus.
31	
32	Before changing the canon itself, read **[consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md)** (provisional) — which propagation channel a change travels, how to migrate copied artifacts like stubs through the conformance checks, and the other practices we currently believe keep a canon change from hurting consuming repos. And before *adding* a feature, read **[extending.md](extending.md)** — the core/pack boundary (what's engine vs. pack-contributed content) and where each kind of new feature goes.
33	
34	The **growth lifecycle** — how a lesson is learned in a consuming project, lifted into the canon when it's portable, and pruned back out once the canon owns it — is **fully pack-based**, three independent stages split across two packs by who declares them (no folder-access graph, no bespoke orchestrator step):
35	
36	- The growth pack — the **member-side** stages, on every repo declaring the pack: **extract** (captures the last 24h of bugs/PRs/commits into the project's **own** docs, committing directly to its `main`), **dedup** (prunes local items the canon covers, keeping items the canon states too generally; opens a PR), and the weekly **pack discovery** pipeline.
37	- The curation pack — the **home-only** pack, declared solely by the canon home repo, so its tasks run central-once by declaration cardinality: **promote** (reads the changed members' local docs, **generalizes** the portable lessons, routes each to the right canon home, and opens a PR against Claudinite's `main` — the sole judgment gate before shared canon; it replaced the old cross-repo handoff, Action + PAT + labelled issue, which is gone) with its shared **item-routing** method, and the weekly **prose-mining sweep** task.
38	
39	The mounted corpus itself is **`packs/`** (each `packs/<name>/` bundling a pack's prose `RULES.md` and its check modules, discovered structurally by [packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs) and activated by declaration) and **`skills/`** (activity-scoped procedures — catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md)). `checks/` holds only the **engine** that runs the packs' checks — the dependency-free runner, its lib, the Stop hook and PreToolUse guard, and their tests. Usage and configuration → [checks/README.md](checks/README.md); design → [checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md); the per-rule audit → [docs/conversion-inventory.md](docs/conversion-inventory.md).
40	
41	`migrations/` holds the **baseline migrations** mechanism — declared, self-retiring path relocations, one record per in-flight canon rename (a renamed or relocated artifact consumers hold their own copy of) that supplies the read-side resolver, the write-side rename, and the fleet telemetry that auto-retires it once every consumer has moved. Named for when it runs: baselining applies and retires each. See [migrations/README.md](migrations/README.md).
42	
43	`routines/` holds the scheduled jobs:
44	
45	- [routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md](routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md) — **the single scheduled entry point**, run daily from the fleet-**enforcer** repo (one that declares the enforcer pack). It runs the core planner over the repos it can reach (the home repo included, planned last), reads the emitted units, and runs each covered repo's due `run_daily` units at their `smarts` tier — no repo or unit can stop the others. Schedule **this**, nothing else.
46	- [routines/fleet/plan.mjs](routines/fleet/plan.mjs) — the **planner**: pack-agnostic core code that goes over the repos it can reach and, for each covered member, assembles its declared packs' `run_daily` tasks, runs each gate in code, and emits the day's work plan. It depends on no single pack — an enforcer pack's coverage census is a separate, isolated concern, never the source of the plan.
47	- `check-fleet-coverage.mjs` — the enforcer pack's **census** (the cross-repo reach the pack adds): with an account-spanning PAT it enumerates the owner's repos, classifies coverage, and converges one adoption issue per uncovered repo. It is **coverage, not planning** — it does not build the work plan, and it carries **no migration logic** (baseline-migration application and retirement are the migrations flow's own standalone passes, `migrations/fleet-apply.mjs` + `migrations/fleet-retire.mjs`). Dispatched via that repo's `workflow_dispatch`-only coverage workflow (materialized from the pack's stub); which repos to cover and the exclude list come from its pack entry's `config`.
48	- [routines/fleet/](routines/fleet/DESIGN.md) — the **run_daily engine** (the code that decides each repo's due tasks from its active packs) and the scheduling contract. **Baselining** is now the baseline pack's daily task; **growth** rides the member-side pack and the home-side pack; the PR/branch/issue sweep is the repo-tidy pack.
49	
50	**Where things land:** **extract** commits directly to each project's `main` (it writes only local docs), while **promote** (the canon gate) and **dedup** each open a PR for the owner to approve. Every stage reads only what's already merged: a lesson extracted tonight is promoted the next night (the extract commit trips the next night's signals), and promote's PR means promote → dedup waits an approval cycle — the dominant latency either way. An owner-requested, in-session retrospective still delivers a PR.
51	
52	## The submodule future (for consumers)
53	
54	The vendored `shared/` root deliberately mirrors this repo's layout so that mounting Claudinite
55	as a **git submodule at `.claudinite/shared/`** — once sessions run where a cross-repo git
56	credential exists — is a drop-in upgrade that changes no wiring. Details in
57	[mount/DESIGN.md](mount/DESIGN.md).
58

1	// grow_with_claudinite run_daily task: conversation-extract — the conversation-side
2	// sibling of growth-extract. Mines the repo's captured conversation logs (the orphan
3	// conversation-logs branch, written by merge-to-main's capture step) for durable
4	// lessons, posts the dialogue behind each extracted rule on the issue it was worked
5	// under, and prunes logs past the entry's retention_days. Worker: the co-located
6	// conversation-extract.md.
7	//
8	// An ordinary independent planner unit with the SAME access model as growth-extract:
9	// the conversation-logs branch is IN this repo, so reading it, committing lessons to
10	// local packs, and pruning aged logs are all plain local git in the checkout; only
11	// posting the dialogue on the worked issue uses the GitHub MCP tools (the issue API
12	// isn't a git operation). (Only CAPTURE needs the live session transcript, so capture
13	// stays in-session at merge; extraction consumes what capture already pushed, so it is
14	// a regular run_daily task, not a per-repo schedule.)
15	
16	export default {
17	  id: 'conversation-extract',
18	  worker: 'packs/grow_with_claudinite/conversation-extract.md',
19	  full_sweep_supported: true,
20	  smarts: 'high', // deciding what clears the lesson bar is the heaviest judgment
21	
22	  async gate(repo, signals) {
23	    // Fire the day after a real merge — that's when a fresh capture sits on the
24	    // conversation-logs branch (a merge is a substantiveChange; a [skip ci] capture
25	    // commit is not, so substantiveChange tracks the merge, not the capture). The
26	    // worker no-ops when the branch holds nothing fresh, exactly like growth-extract
27	    // when the window yields no lesson.
28	    if (signals.substantiveChange) {
29	      return { run: true, targets: {}, reason: 'recent merge — extract any freshly captured conversation logs' };
30	    }
31	    // The weekly full sweep re-processes (dedup absorbs the overlap) and, crucially,
32	    // runs the retention prune even on a repo gone quiet — a log ages out on wall
33	    // time, not on the repo changing.
34	    if (signals.fullSweep) {
35	      return { run: true, targets: {}, reason: 'weekly full conversation extract + retention prune' };
36	    }
37	    return { run: false };
38	  },
39	};
40

content.content:
1	// grow_with_claudinite run_daily task: growth-extract-new-instructions (the growth
2	// lifecycle's capture stage). Captures the last 24h of bugs/PRs/commits into the
3	// project's own docs. Worker: the co-located extract.md. An ordinary independent
4	// unit — no barrier; the central home-only promote stage picks up whatever is
5	// merged here by its next run.
6	//
7	// Trigger: the project changed in the window (commits / merged PRs). No distinct full
8	// mode — extract's window is a fixed lookback, and a quiet project has nothing to
9	// extract even on a full-sweep night — so full_sweep_supported is false (fullSweep is
10	// a no-op for it: the engine masks it, so the gate never sees fullSweep true here).
11	
12	export default {
13	  id: 'growth-extract-new-instructions',
14	  worker: 'packs/grow_with_claudinite/extract.md',
15	  full_sweep_supported: false,
16	  smarts: 'high', // generalizing/curating lessons is the heaviest judgment
17	
18	  async gate(repo, signals) {
19	    // substantiveChange, not projectChanged: a bot bump / [skip ci] / nightly
20	    // baselining commit advancing main is not a lesson to extract (see signals.mjs).
21	    if (signals.substantiveChange) {
22	      return { run: true, targets: {}, reason: 'project changed substantively in the window' };
23	    }
24	    return { run: false };
25	  },
26	};

1	// grow_with_claudinite run_daily task: growth-discover-packs — the pack-discovery
2	// pipeline, a regular member-scoped unit (the central promote stage is the
3	// home-only one). The planner picks it up per member on that member's weekly full
4	// sweep; the orchestrator dispatches it like any unit. The worker runs centrally
5	// (home session, fleet token): for the member it's handed it manifests the stack,
6	// suggests a pack for each unhomed technology, populates it from real usage, and
7	// opens one canon PR per pack. Worker: the co-located discover-packs.md.
8	//
9	// Weekly only — a repo's stack is slow-moving, so full_sweep_supported is true and the
10	// gate fires solely on fullSweep (the engine masks fullSweep for tasks that don't declare
11	// it, so the gate only ever sees it true here).
12	
13	export default {
14	  id: 'growth-discover-packs',
15	  worker: 'packs/grow_with_claudinite/discover-packs.md',
16	  full_sweep_supported: true,
17	  smarts: 'high', // authoring a pack — distilling rules, writing checks + fixtures — is heavy judgment
18	
19	  async gate(repo, signals) {
20	    if (signals.fullSweep) {
21	      return { run: true, targets: {}, reason: 'weekly pack discovery' };
22	    }
23	    return { run: false };
24	  },
25	};

which kills the false alarm. (Example: `Authorization` is only wrong inside a CloudFront policy's
*own* header list, not elsewhere in the template.) Parse only as much as you need, and hold the
check to the same fixture bar.

When even a scoped parser can't make detection confident, **leave the prose and log the
candidate** to a tagged conversion-backlog issue rather than shipping a shaky check.

## Bounds

- **One PR, bounded surface** — the new rule module, its `pack.mjs` line, its fixture, and the
  trimmed prose. Don't "improve" unrelated rules while you're in there.
- **Never delete a rule you didn't convert** — trimming prose is only for a rule a *landed* check
  now enforces.
- Run the suite and the sweep green before opening the PR; open it for the owner's approval,
  never a direct push to `main`.

content.content:
1	# Growth — extract lessons (per member)
2	
3	The growth lifecycle's capture stage, this pack's daily task: review a project's recent activity and fold any durable, reusable lesson into the project's own **local packs** (`.claudinite/local_packs/` — the normalized capture surface) — at the project's own level, without straining to generalize it. It **commits directly to the project's default branch** (no per-run PR); finding nothing to add on a given run is a perfectly good outcome.
4	
5	> This is the **unattended daily** routine. **Unlike** an owner-requested, in-session retrospective (which delivers a PR for review), this routine commits straight to `main`: it writes only the project's *own* local packs, and the owner has opted out of a per-run review gate here to keep the fleet's daily lesson-capture from piling up PRs. The shared canon stays gated — lifting anything up into it is the central promote stage's job, and that *does* open a PR.
6	
7	## Capture at the project's own level
8	
9	Write each lesson at whatever level reads naturally for this project — refer to its files, services, or mechanics wherever that's what makes the lesson clear, but don't force either extreme: don't contort a lesson to be hyper-specific, and don't polish it into a general, portable rule. Making a lesson portable is the central promote stage's job, done centrally later (it picks up whatever is merged here by its next run); here, just capture it usefully and let promotion lift whatever turns out to travel.
10	
11	## Conventions used in this doc
12	
13	- **Default branch.** `main` stands for **your repository's default branch** — substitute whatever your repo uses.
14	- **GitHub access is MCP-native.** Reading issue/PR activity and updating the tracking issue go through the session's **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`). The fleet run has no shell GitHub access — the shell reaches only a git-over-HTTPS proxy scoped to one repo, with no REST credential — so never reach for `gh`/`curl` or a cross-repo clone.
15	- **The project's local packs.** The set identified in [this pack's README](README.md#identifying-a-projects-capture-surface-its-local-packs) — everything under `.claudinite/local_packs/`, the project's own packs; never the read-only mounted canon elsewhere under `.claudinite/`.
16	
17	## How it finds lessons (scoped to the last 24h)
18	
19	1. **Activity gate, first.** Count commits + updated issues/PRs in the window. None → stop; a quiet day has nothing to learn from.
20	2. **Read the window.** The last-24h **commits** (`git log --since`, full bodies, diffs where a fix is non-obvious) and **issue/PR activity** (`updated:>=<since>`, then the changed comments).
21	3. **Extract only durable, reusable lessons** — gotchas, engineering practices, test discipline, architecture rules, project mechanics — and **dedupe** each against what the project already documents. When in doubt, leave it out.
22	4. **Route each lesson to the owning local pack, and prefer the strongest mechanism.** Pick the pack whose territory the lesson belongs to (most projects have one general pack; some segregate a domain pack), then run the **local promotion ladder**: a deterministic rule becomes a **check** in that pack's `rules` (author the `.mjs`, list it on `pack.mjs`, add a red-first fixture — its failure message *is* the lesson), an activity-scoped procedure becomes a **pack skill**, and only what neither can carry lands as terse **prose** in the pack's `RULES.md`. A gotcha tied to one call site still goes as a comment right at that site (the file-local rule — [extracting-lessons.md](extracting-lessons.md) owns the usage-site-vs-central call). Write more checks and less prose; keep each addition terse and in the project's own voice.
23	
24	If an edit touches something a test reads (a doc constant, a code path), run the project's offline test suite and keep it green before committing.
25	
26	## Output: commit to `main`
27	
28	If it found at least one genuinely new lesson, it **commits the edits directly to `main`** — one commit for the whole run, not one per lesson. This writes only the project's *own* local packs (not the shared canon), and the owner has opted for a direct commit here rather than a per-run PR, to keep the fleet's daily lesson-capture from flooding review requests. Keep the commit terse and reference the tracking issue. A run that finds nothing and commits nothing is fine — and common. (A new check must ship green — see it fail on a violating fixture, pass on a clean one — before the commit; a check that can't be made confident lands its lesson as prose instead, never a broken check.)
29	
30	## Tracking: log each run under the routine's own issue
31	
32	The routine's standing log is the issue titled exactly, in this member repo:
33	
34	> **Claudinite tracker: Growth Extract**
35	
36	Find it **by that exact title, never a fuzzy match or a hard-coded number** (a bare number can dangle, and it differs per repo). This tracker never had a fixed title before now, so there is no reliable prior title to migrate from — a run that finds no issue under the exact new title just creates one (closed); it does not need to search for an old name. **Never open, close, or reopen it** afterward — its state carries no meaning, only the log does. When a run adds a lesson, log it as a **dated comment** — not a sub-issue — so the issue accumulates a scrollable history, each entry naming **what was added and where**.
37	
38	## Run on a capable model
39	
40	Deciding whether a lesson is genuinely new and durable — and deduping it against what's already documented — is a **judgment call**, not mechanical extraction. A downgraded model adds noise or restates what's there, and there's **no per-run PR gate here to catch it** — the commit lands straight in the project's packs — so the capable-model requirement matters all the more. Run this routine on a capable model.
41	
42	## What this routine must never do
43	
44	- **Never touch the shared canon** — this routine writes only the project's *own* local packs under `.claudinite/local_packs/`; everything else under `.claudinite/` is the read-only mount, and lifting a lesson up into the canon is the central promote stage's job.
45	- **Don't add noise** — a duplicate or hallucinated "lesson" is worse than adding nothing, the more so when it lands directly in the project's packs with no review gate.
46

# Growth — extract lessons (per member)

The growth lifecycle's capture stage, this pack's daily task: review a project's recent activity and fold any durable, reusable lesson into the project's own **local packs** (`.claudinite/local_packs/` — the normalized capture surface) — at the project's own level, without straining to generalize it. It **commits directly to the project's default branch** (no per-run PR); finding nothing to add on a given run is a perfectly good outcome.

> This is the **unattended daily** routine. **Unlike** an owner-requested, in-session retrospective (which delivers a PR for review), this routine commits straight to `main`: it writes only the project's *own* local packs, and the owner has opted out of a per-run review gate here to keep the fleet's daily lesson-capture from piling up PRs. The shared canon stays gated — lifting anything up into it is the central promote stage's job, and that *does* open a PR.

## Capture at the project's own level

Write each lesson at whatever level reads naturally for this project — refer to its files, services, or mechanics wherever that's what makes the lesson clear, but don't force either extreme: don't contort a lesson to be hyper-specific, and don't polish it into a general, portable rule. Making a lesson portable is the central promote stage's job, done centrally later (it picks up whatever is merged here by its next run); here, just capture it usefully and let promotion lift whatever turns out to travel.

## Conventions used in this doc

- **Default branch.** `main` stands for **your repository's default branch** — substitute whatever your repo uses.
- **GitHub access is MCP-native.** Reading issue/PR activity and updating the tracking issue go through the session's **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`). The fleet run has no shell GitHub access — the shell reaches only a git-over-HTTPS proxy scoped to one repo, with no REST credential — so never reach for `gh`/`curl` or a cross-repo clone.
- **The project's local packs.** The set identified in [this pack's README](README.md#identifying-a-projects-capture-surface-its-local-packs) — everything under `.claudinite/local_packs/`, the project's own packs; never the read-only mounted canon elsewhere under `.claudinite/`.

## How it finds lessons (scoped to the last 24h)

1. **Activity gate, first.** Count commits + updated issues/PRs in the window. None → stop; a quiet day has nothing to learn from.
2. **Read the window.** The last-24h **commits** (`git log --since`, full bodies, diffs where a fix is non-obvious) and **issue/PR activity** (`updated:>=<since>`, then the changed comments).
3. **Extract only durable, reusable lessons** — gotchas, engineering practices, test discipline, architecture rules, project mechanics — and **dedupe** each against what the project already documents. When in doubt, leave it out.
4. **Route each lesson to the owning local pack, and prefer the strongest mechanism.** Pick the pack whose territory the lesson belongs to (most projects have one general pack; some segregate a domain pack), then run the **local promotion ladder**: a deterministic rule becomes a **check** in that pack's `rules` (author the `.mjs`, list it on `pack.mjs`, add a red-first fixture — its failure message *is* the lesson), an activity-scoped procedure becomes a **pack skill**, and only what neither can carry lands as terse **prose** in the pack's `RULES.md`. A gotcha tied to one call site still goes as a comment right at that site (the file-local rule — [extracting-lessons.md](extracting-lessons.md) owns the usage-site-vs-central call). Write more checks and less prose; keep each addition terse and in the project's own voice.

If an edit touches something a test reads (a doc constant, a code path), run the project's offline test suite and keep it green before committing.

## Output: commit to `main`

If it found at least one genuinely new lesson, it **commits the edits directly to `main`** — one commit for the whole run, not one per lesson. This writes only the project's *own* local packs (not the shared canon), and the owner has opted for a direct commit here rather than a per-run PR, to keep the fleet's daily lesson-capture from flooding review requests. Keep the commit terse and reference the tracking issue. A run that finds nothing and commits nothing is fine — and common. (A new check must ship green — see it fail on a violating fixture, pass on a clean one — before the commit; a check that can't be made confident lands its lesson as prose instead, never a broken check.)

## Tracking: log each run under the routine's own issue

The routine's standing log is the issue titled exactly, in this member repo:

> **Claudinite tracker: Growth Extract**

Find it **by that exact title, never a fuzzy match or a hard-coded number** (a bare number can dangle, and it differs per repo). This tracker never had a fixed title before now, so there is no reliable prior title to migrate from — a run that finds no issue under the exact new title just creates one (closed); it does not need to search for an old name. **Never open, close, or reopen it** afterward — its state carries no meaning, only the log does. When a run adds a lesson, log it as a **dated comment** — not a sub-issue — so the issue accumulates a scrollable history, each entry naming **what was added and where**.

## Run on a capable model

Deciding whether a lesson is genuinely new and durable — and deduping it against what's already documented — is a **judgment call**, not mechanical extraction. A downgraded model adds noise or restates what's there, and there's **no per-run PR gate here to catch it** — the commit lands straight in the project's packs — so the capable-model requirement matters all the more. Run this routine on a capable model.

## What this routine must never do

- **Never touch the shared canon** — this routine writes only the project's *own* local packs under `.claudinite/local_packs/`; everything else under `.claudinite/` is the read-only mount, and lifting a lesson up into the canon is the central promote stage's job.
- **Don't add noise** — a duplicate or hallucinated "lesson" is worse than adding nothing, the more so when it lands directly in the project's packs with no review gate.
