74	a file the set dropped, or a note's delete) — MCP has no multi-file+delete, so they follow immediately
75	as their own `delete_file` commits, **after** the stamped content commit (#329). Trailing deletes are
76	safe to interrupt: the next night's unconditional convergence re-deletes any straggler, whereas note
77	ops are stamp-gated and so must always land with the stamp.
78	
79	Never touch the home (sheepdog) repo; never adopt without an open adoption issue; never flip a
80	pre-flip member; never let alignment edit beyond a failing check's own remedy; never merge a
81	delivery PR or guess a delivery preference. `smarts: medium` — the bootstrap and alignment merge
82	into existing `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` without clobbering, which is judgment.
83

1	# wiki-growth worker
2	
3	One compile-once/refine-in-place research pass over the repo's product wikis (the
4	Karpathy LLM-wiki pattern): read what the wiki already knows, research only what
5	its own backlog flags, write it back cited. **Most runs find little or nothing —
6	no new citable material means no branch, no PR, and that is the documented good
7	outcome.** A padded or fabricated update is worse than none.
8	
9	Works entirely over the session's **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`) — never
10	`gh`/`curl`/a clone; the fleet run has no consumer shell or npm. "Default branch"
11	below means the repo's actual default branch. The wiki set is derived
12	**structurally**: every `README.md` at depth ≥ 2 under `product-wiki/`, excluding the
13	`product-wiki/product-requirements/` and `product-wiki/sample-data/` subtrees.
14	
15	## Preflight
16	
17	Search the repo for an open PR whose head branch matches `claude/product-wiki-growth/*`.
18	If one is open, do **not** stack a second round of unreviewed research on it —
19	add one dated nudge comment on that PR and stop.
20	
21	## Method
22	
23	1. **Read every wiki page end to end** (`get_file_contents`), the `## Open
24	   questions` and `## Growth log` sections first-class. Never re-derive a claim
25	   that is already cited and current.
26	2. **Pick the one or two open questions** across the set most worth this run;
27	   also spot-check one or two existing citations for staleness (a dead link, a
28	   superseded stat).
29	3. **Research.** *Web mode* (WebSearch/WebFetch available): the open web.
30	   *Repo-derived mode* (web tools absent — do not fail, do not fake): citable
31	   repo-native signal only — new feature-request/extractor-request issues,
32	   merged PRs, issue/PR discussion carrying user-side signal — each citable to
33	   its own GitHub URL.
34	4. **Write into the relevant wiki page(s) only**, per the pack's RULES.md: cite
35	   every claim in that page's `## Sources`; correct a wrong or superseded claim
36	   with a note of why, never a silent deletion; touch `product-wiki/sample-data/`
37	   only when a new claim needs an illustrative example; **never write
38	   `product-wiki/product-requirements/`** — a finding that should move a requirement
39	   gets a growth-log note (and a repo issue) and waits for a human.
40	5. **One dated `## Growth log` entry per touched page** (`- **YYYY-MM-DD** —
41	   what changed`).
42	6. **Update `## Open questions` both directions** — remove what this run
43	   answered, add what its research surfaced.
44	
45	## Stop condition
46	
47	Neither mode yields citable material → stop. No commit, no log entry, no PR.
48	
49	## Delivery
50	
51	This worker's own delivery policy (deliberately stricter than the member's
52	`maintenance.delivery`, and documented here as that sanctioned deviation):
53	branch `claude/product-wiki-growth/<date>` (unique branch per run), commits
54	touching only `product-wiki/**` minus `product-requirements/`, one **PR — never
55	merged, never pushed to the default branch directly**. Web-researched claims
56	entering a committed knowledge base need the review gate; mechanical
57	drift-correction doesn't, but this isn't that. PR body: the question(s)
58	researched, what changed where, the citations added, and the open questions
59	left for the next run.
60	
61	## Tracking
62	
63	Standing issue found by exact title **"Claudinite tracker: Product Wiki
64	Growth"** in the member repo — found by title, never by number, never
65	opened/closed as state (open it if missing). One dated comment when a pass
66	**grew** (`grew (PR #N): <one line>`) or was **blocked** (`blocked: <why>` —
67	the human-visible convergence for a failed unattended run). Clean no-ops stay
68	silent — the pack's freshness advisory is the prolonged-silence observer.
69	
70	Run on a capable model (`smarts: high`): open-web research plus curation is
71	judgment-heavy, and the PR review gate is the last catch for fabrication.
72	
73	## What this worker must never do
74	
75	Edit `product-wiki/product-requirements/`; edit anything outside `product-wiki/`; create
76	new wiki folders (automation refines existing pages — a human creates a wiki
77	deliberately); write an uncited claim; delete a claim without a superseded
78	note; pad a growth log or a wiki page to look productive; merge or approve its
79	own PR; schedule anything (no cron, no `schedule:` workflow); touch the shared
80	canon.
81

1	# Fleet daily maintenance routine (the single scheduled entry point)
2	
3	A portable, **project-agnostic** spec for the **one** daily routine that maintains **every repo the owner has opted into Claudinite** — scheduled once, from the canon repo, and reaching all the others. It carries no maintenance logic of its own. It is a thin **orchestrator** that does two things: **(1)** run the **planner** logic — a pack-agnostic walk over the repos it was handed that decides *what work each repo needs* in code and emits the plan — then **(2)** dispatch each planned unit as an isolated worker **at the tier its `smarts` names**. Every unit is independent — they run concurrently, nothing waits on anything else. The point is twofold: the owner registers **a single schedule** for **all** their repos, and every unit is **guaranteed to run** because each is its own isolated subagent (or code action) — one failing, stalling, or exiting early cannot stop the others. The "should I run" decision is **code** (the planner's gates), never this routine's judgment.
4	
5	Two deterministic **migration passes** bracket that pack work: **before** any pack runs, the migration **apply pass** brings every covered member to the current canonical shape; **after** every unit settles, the migration **retire pass** finalizes (retires) any migration the fleet has fully applied and left quiet for a cycle. Both are pure decision logic (`smarts: none`) the orchestrator runs directly: the orchestrator **passes them this session's scoped repos** and the MCP-backed `gh` reader/writer, and they act only on those repos. The migrations flow owns them ([../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs](../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs), [../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs](../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs)); the routine only sequences them around the plan, adding no reasoning of its own. Retirement targets the **canon repo the migrations live in** — the same place they are loaded from, not a separately-discovered "home".
6	
7	This routine **replaces** scheduling anything individually **and** any per-repo maintenance schedule: schedule *this* one, in the canon repo, and nothing else. Do **not** also schedule the planner, a pack's task, or a per-repo routine. The planner and each step's spec stay exactly as written — this routine only dispatches the plan the planner emits, concurrently, in isolation.
8	
9	## Conventions used in this doc
10	
11	- **Canon repo.** This routine is scheduled from, keeps its tracking issue in, and ships its migrations from a single fixed **canon repo** — the repo where you vendor and schedule this doc (for the reference deployment, that's Claudinite itself). "Canon repo" below means that repo; every *other* repo it maintains is a **member repo**. It is a known constant the orchestrator names — nothing is discovered from a CI env var.
12	- **Default branch.** Each member repo's own default branch is whatever *that* repo uses; the plan and workers substitute it per repo, so this routine never assumes `main` for a member repo.
13	- **GitHub access is MCP-native — no REST client, no token.** This routine, its `smarts: none` code steps, and its workers all reach GitHub **only** through the session's **GitHub MCP tools** (`mcp__github__*`). The orchestrator knows the repos it manages from **this session's granted scope** — it does not enumerate GitHub — and backs the code steps' injected I/O with those MCP tools. There is no `GITHUB_TOKEN`, no `gh`/`curl`, and no raw `fetch` to `api.github.com` anywhere in this process: in a scheduled web session the shell has no GitHub REST credential, and cross-repo `git` checkout is impossible, so MCP is the whole surface. Two injected shapes, both the orchestrator's to fulfill over MCP:
14	  - The **planner** reads through a read-only `gh(path) -> { status, json }` — each path a `list_*` / `get_file_contents` call.
15	  - The **migration passes** take a semantic **`io`** object whose methods map **1:1 to MCP write/read tools**, so no Git-Data-API shapes leak into the code: `io.getDefaultBranch` (get repo), `io.read` (`get_file_contents`), `io.ensureBranch` (`create_branch`), `io.commit` (`push_files`), `io.remove` (`delete_file`), `io.hasOpenPr` (`list_pull_requests`), `io.openPr` (`create_pull_request`), `io.openIssue` (`issue_write`), and the optional `io.updateBranchFromBase` (`update_pull_request_branch` on the branch's open PR; throws on conflict — the pr delivery lane uses it to refresh the maintenance branch from base each night, #332). Writes land as one `push_files` commit; a rare file-move's delete follows via `delete_file` (MCP has no atomic multi-file+delete — no active migration produces a delete).
16	
17	## What the planner decides, what this routine dispatches
18	
19	The **planner** ([fleet/DESIGN.md](fleet/DESIGN.md), [fleet/plan.mjs](fleet/plan.mjs)) is **pack-agnostic decision logic**: it goes over the repos it was handed — every covered member, plus the **canon repo itself, planned last** with the fleet aggregate its home-only packs' gates need — and for each assembles the maintenance actions its declared packs contribute (their `run_daily` tasks), runs each action's **"should I run" gate** (pure code), and emits the day's **work plan** — a flat list of `(repo, task, worker, targets, reason, order, smarts)` **units**, one per `(repo, task)` a gate marked live. A repo with nothing to do yields no units, at an MCP-read's cost. **The planner never dispatches or depends on any one pack's workflow** — every unit stands alone, so an un-set-up or failing pack surfaces as one isolated unit (a baselining fix), never a reason the plan can't be built. This routine runs the planner over the repos it was handed, reads its units, and dispatches them; it decides *nothing* itself.
20	
21	The units span every kind of maintenance, each deferring wholly to its own doc:
22	
23	- **Growth** (the growth pack owns the lifecycle narrative) — the member-side tasks (`growth-extract-new-instructions`, `growth-dedup-local-instructions`, `growth-discover-packs`, `conversation-extract`) on every repo declaring the growth pack, and the central `growth-promote-to-claudinite` on the canon repo via its home-only curation pack. **All ordinary, independent planned units** — no phases, no barriers, no bespoke central step: each stage reads only what's already merged, so extract→promote→dedup propagates across successive nightly runs (and promote's PR approval was always the dominant latency). `conversation-extract` is the conversation-side sibling of `growth-extract-new-instructions` — same access model (local git on the repo's working tree, MCP only for the issue API), mining the repo's captured `conversation-logs` branch instead of its issues/PRs/commits.
24	- **The repo-tidying pack** — the single `repo-tidy` task (assess branches/PRs read-only, act on issues, reconcile the standing tracker, in one pass) on every repo declaring the pack, and against the canon repo.
25	- **`baselining`** (the baseline pack's task — the member re-run of the idempotent bootstrap + check-alignment); a handed repo that isn't covered yet gets its **first baseline** (adoption) from the same worker.
26	- **Any pack task** — e.g. `chrome-store-release`. New maintenance is a new pack `(gate, worker)` pair; the plan picks it up automatically — **no edit here**.
27	
28	## Step 0 — apply migrations across the fleet (before any pack work)
29	
30	Before the planner runs, bring every managed repo to the current canonical shape: run the migration **apply pass** ([../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs](../migrations/fleet-apply.mjs))'s `applyToRepos(io, repos, migrations)` over this session's scoped repos, with the MCP-backed `io` object and the migration set (loaded via `migrations/registry.mjs`). It computes each member's pending migration writes — the declared file renames, template materializations, and ref rewrites — and lands them as **one commit** through `io.commit` (`push_files`), honoring the member's `push`/`pr` delivery. It is **pure decision logic (`smarts: none`** — no subagent) and idempotent: a member already on the canonical shape gets no commit. Running migrations *first* means the pack tasks (baselining's check-alignment included) evaluate the already-migrated shape. It **returns the ids it applied this cycle** — hold that set in hand and pass it to **Step 3** as its quiescence evidence (no intermediate file).
31	
32	## Step 1 — run the planner over the handed repos
33	
34	The orchestrator already knows the repos it manages — **this session's granted scope** — so run the **planner** ([fleet/plan.mjs](fleet/plan.mjs))'s `planGivenRepos(gh, canonRepo, repos)` over them with the MCP-backed `gh`, and read the returned plan. For each repo it assembles the declared packs' `run_daily` tasks ([fleet/registry.mjs](fleet/registry.mjs)) and runs each task's **gate** ([fleet/gates.mjs](fleet/gates.mjs) — pure "should I run" code) over the repo's signals ([fleet/signals.mjs](fleet/signals.mjs)). The result is the unit list — `buildWorkPlan` *is* exactly this loop over the repos you hand it. **The worklist is decided by code, not by this routine's reasoning.**
35	
36	**Local-pack tasks are planned by default.** A member's canon-pack tasks are on disk in the canon checkout; its **own local packs** (`.claudinite/local_packs/`) contribute `run_daily` tasks the same way, read from the member repo by `readLocalTasks(gh, fullName)` ([fleet/local-tasks.mjs](fleet/local-tasks.mjs)) — the planner's **default** `localTasksFor`, so `planGivenRepos(gh, canonRepo, repos)` plans them with no extra wiring. A local task is gated by the member's own declaration exactly like a canon task, and the canon home's curation tasks ride this path nightly. The reader's header states the single-owner-trust contract it runs under; a task shadowing a canon pack id is never planned (canon wins).
37	
38	If the planner logic itself **fails** (the gate code errors across the board), or its `errors` show probes that couldn't cover repos you *can* reach, you **cannot guarantee coverage** — log it (see Tracking) and dispatch whatever plan you have. But a **broken or un-set-up pack is not that failure**: it surfaces as an isolated adoption/baselining unit for that one repo (which its own worker fixes), never as a missing plan. Only a genuine planner-code failure is a no-op day, logged.
39	
40	## Step 2 — dispatch the units, at their tier
41	
42	Run each unit's **worker** at the tier its `smarts` names: a subagent on the matching capability tier for `high` / `medium` / `low`, or, for **`none`**, a **direct code / tool execution** with no subagent. Dispatch subagents with the **Agent/Task tool, never the multi-agent-orchestration Workflow tool** — its mandatory interactive opt-in stalls an unattended run on step one, waiting for an "Allow" no one is there to click. Each worker runs **exactly per its own doc**, handed the unit's `targets`; this routine adds **no** behavior. Read the worker doc from the right repo: a canon-pack unit's `worker` is a canon-relative path read from the canon checkout (`workerRepo` null); a **local-pack** unit carries `workerRepo` — the member repo — and a member-relative `worker` path, read from the member over MCP.
43	
44	**Pass the plan's gathered enumeration through — don't make the worker re-fetch it.** The planner already enumerated each repo's touched branches / PRs / issues to build the unit (they're in the unit's `targets`, and the worker docs already say "the plan hands you the list — you don't enumerate"). So hand the worker that enumeration and let it start from there; **do not re-run `list_branches` / `list_pull_requests` / `list_issues` in the dispatch, and do not instruct the worker to re-enumerate** what the plan already holds — a worker fetches only the per-item detail it needs to verify (e.g. a branch's landed status against the default branch). Re-gathering the same lists the planner just fetched is pure duplicated cost and adds nothing; the plan is the single source of the worklist.
45	
46	Concurrency — every unit is independent:
47	
48	- **All units run concurrently**, capped to a sane batch. There is **no ordering and no barrier of any kind** — not per-repo, not fleet-wide. The repo tidy-up is a single `repo-tidy` unit whose worker does assess-branches → assess-PRs → triage-issues → reconcile-the-tracker in one pass, so that sequencing lives inside the one worker, never in the plan; nothing else waits on anything.
49	- **Await async downstream** — a unit that triggers a dispatch-only Action (a pack task's) is done only when that Action **completes**, not at the trigger; poll it (report at completion, not the trigger).
50	
51	The subagent boundary delivers the guarantees the owner cares about: **failure isolation** (a unit that errors, stalls, or exits early fails *its own* subagent only), **context isolation**, and **behavior unchanged**. Also run the tidy tasks against the **canon repo** (it doesn't declare the tidy pack — its declaration carries only its home-only packs and the checks it runs on itself — but its PRs/branches/issues still need tending). Cap concurrency if the fleet is large, but **every** unit must be launched **and** waited on — a launched-but-unwaited unit is not a guaranteed unit. Wait for **everything** to settle before finishing.
52	
53	## Step 3 — finalize migrations (retire)
54	
55	Only after **every** unit has settled, run the migration **retire pass** ([../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs](../migrations/fleet-retire.mjs))'s `runRetirement(io, canonRepo, migrations, repos, unknownCount, today, appliedThisCycle)` over the **same** scoped repo list — it must be the COMPLETE managed set, since retirement fires only when the whole set is clean, so a missing repo could hide drift. Pass it Step 0's returned `appliedThisCycle` set directly (in memory) and the canon repo — the same repo the migrations were loaded from, where the deletes land. It probes each migration's fleet-wide completion via `io.read`, and for a migration the whole fleet has applied **and** that Step 0 touched on **no** repo this cycle (the **quiescence guard**: if that evidence is absent it retires **nothing**), it **stages the deletions** — the record and any files it relocated into the consumers — onto a stable retire branch and opens **one PR** (`io.ensureBranch` → `io.remove` on that branch → `io.openPr`, idempotent via `io.hasOpenPr`). Retirement is **irreversible**, so it is **never pushed to the canon's default branch**: the PR routes it through the canon's own CI (`barrier`, `reference-integrity`, the test suite), which validates that the deletion strands no inline reference before it can land — a clean retirement is a green, mergeable PR (never auto-merged); a stranding one is a red PR held for cleanup, so `main` never breaks. It runs **last**, on a fleet proven both converged and quiet, and it writes only to the canon repo. It is **pure decision logic (`smarts: none`)**.
56	
57	## What the orchestrator itself must not do
58	
59	The orchestrator is a sequencer, not a maintainer. It **never** decides the worklist by its own reasoning — the "should I run" gate is code (the planner); the orchestrator *runs* that code and reads its units, it doesn't reason its way to a plan. And it **never** runs a *worker's* logic, merges, commits docs by its own judgment, edits the canon, or writes to a step's tracking issue — every such action belongs to the worker (or the deterministic pass) that owns it. Running the plan's **code steps** — the planner and the migration apply/retire passes (all `smarts: none`, performing their reads/writes to members and the canon repo through the MCP `gh`) — is not the orchestrator reasoning; its *only* judgment-driven write is the failure log on its **own** tracking issue in the canon repo (next section). Keep it that thin.
60	
61	## Tracking: log only failures, on the canon repo's own issue
62	
63	This routine keeps its **own** standing tracking issue **in the canon repo**, separate from every step's — found **by title**, never a hard-coded number. Open it if it doesn't exist; reopen it if it was closed while a run still needs logging.
64	
65	It logs **failures only** — the job is to guarantee every planned unit *ran* where it should, so the one thing worth surfacing is a run that **didn't** complete:
66	
67	- **The planner run failed / the plan couldn't cover the fleet**, or **any worker failed, stalled, or exited without completing** (name the repo and task) → post a **dated comment** naming what was affected and the symptom.
68	- **Every planned unit completed where it should** (whether it changed anything or correctly did nothing) → **log nothing.** Workers already log their own *changes* to their own issues; a fleet-wide "all green" roll-up here would just be noise.
69	
70	So on a normal day this routine writes nothing at all, and the only entries that accumulate are the failures you actually need to see.
71	
72	## The launcher (Claude Code routine)
73	
74	Keep the routine's config a **thin pointer** to this doc, not an inlined copy. Vendor this file (and the [fleet/](fleet/DESIGN.md) specs) in your canon repo, then schedule **this one routine** daily, pasting a prompt like the following and substituting the path where you vendored these docs:
75	
76	> Run the fleet daily maintenance routine exactly as specified in `<path/to/routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md>`. Work **entirely through the GitHub MCP tools** — there is no `GITHUB_TOKEN` and no REST client. Back the code steps' injected I/O with those MCP tools: a read-only `gh(path)` for the planner (each path a `list_*`/`get_file_contents` call), and a semantic `io` object for the migration passes whose methods map 1:1 to the MCP tools (`io.read`→`get_file_contents`, `io.commit`→`push_files`, `io.remove`→`delete_file`, `io.ensureBranch`→`create_branch`, `io.hasOpenPr`→`list_pull_requests`, `io.openPr`→`create_pull_request`, `io.openIssue`→`issue_write`, `io.getDefaultBranch`→get repo, `io.updateBranchFromBase`→`update_pull_request_branch` on the branch's open PR). **The repos you manage are this session's granted scope** — you already know them, so do not enumerate GitHub. Name the **canon repo** (a known constant — the repo this doc and the migrations ship from). **Apply migrations**: run the apply pass's `applyToRepos(io, repos, migrations)` (migrations loaded via `migrations/registry.mjs`) to bring every managed repo to the canonical shape before any pack work — it lands each repo's pending writes as one commit honoring `push`/`pr` delivery, and **returns the ids it applied this cycle**; keep that set in hand for the retire step. **Run the planner**: run `planGivenRepos(gh, canonRepo, repos)` — it decides every unit in code (pack-agnostically; a broken pack is one isolated unit, not a missing plan); you do not decide the worklist by your own reasoning. Local-pack daily tasks are planned by default — the planner reads each member's own `.claudinite/local_packs/` descriptors over `gh` (Step 1) — so pass nothing extra. **Then dispatch each unit** as an isolated **plain subagent (never the Workflow orchestration tool, whose interactive opt-in an unattended routine can't answer)** at the tier its `smarts` names — or, for `smarts: none`, as a direct code/tool action, no subagent — running its `worker` doc (canon-relative, from the canon checkout) against its repo with the unit's `targets`. Every unit is independent: run them concurrently (capped to a sane batch) — there is no ordering and no barrier of any kind (the repo tidy-up is a single `repo-tidy` unit that sequences its own pass internally). Run the tidy task against the canon repo too. Await any dispatched Action to completion, not to its trigger. Once every unit has settled, **finalize migrations**: run `runRetirement(io, canonRepo, migrations, repos, unknownCount, today, appliedThisCycle)` with the same repo list and the applied-ids set from the apply step, to retire any migration the fleet has fully applied AND left untouched this cycle (it retires nothing if that evidence is absent). Retirement is **never pushed to the canon's default branch** — the pass stages its irreversible deletes onto a retire branch and opens one CI-gated PR (never auto-merged), so the canon's own checks validate reference-safety before it lands. Wait for everything to settle. You are a sequencer only: never decide the worklist by your own reasoning, never run a worker's logic, commit docs, edit the canon, or write to any step's tracking issue. Log **only failures** — the planner code failing, a plan that couldn't cover the reachable fleet, or a unit that didn't complete — to this routine's own standing tracking issue in the canon repo (found **by title**), naming the repo and task affected; on a clean day, log nothing.
77	
78	Schedule it daily as a **Claude Code routine** (the Routines UI, or any scheduler that launches a Claude Code session — the steps need the session's MCP GitHub tools), from the canon repo. Every pack-task Action stays `workflow_dispatch`-only; this routine is the one schedule ([fleet/scheduling.md](fleet/scheduling.md)).
79	
80	## Run on a capable model
81	
82	The workers make **judgment calls** — each unit's `smarts` names the tier its worker needs. This routine's own step (run the planner, read its units, fan out at each unit's tier) is mechanical, but it drives those judgment-heavy workers, so run this routine — and therefore its subagents — on a capable model.
83	
84	## What this routine must never do
85	
86	- **Never decide the worklist by the orchestrator's own reasoning** — run the planner (the gate code) over the repos it was handed and read its units; the "should I run" gate is code, not a judgment call.
87	- **Never run a worker's logic itself** — it dispatches units into subagents (or runs the `none`-tier code action); it does not merge, commit, edit docs, or open any worker's output except its own failure log.
88	- **Never fan out through the Workflow tool** — dispatch runs as plain subagents (Agent/Task). Its mandatory interactive opt-in has no one to answer it in an unattended routine, so it stalls the whole run on step one.
89	- **Never let one unit's failure block another** — each is its own isolated subagent (or code action), and every unit is launched and waited on regardless of how the others fare.
90	- **Never artificially sequence units** — every unit is independent and runs concurrently; there is no ordering or barrier to impose.
91	- **Never also schedule the planner, a pack's task, or a per-repo maintenance routine** — this routine is their single schedule across the whole fleet.
92	- **Never log on a clean day** — it logs only failures to its own canon-repo tracking issue.
93	- **Never inline this spec, a worker's spec, or the plan into the launcher** — the launcher stays a thin pointer here.
94

content.text: Let me read the README, consumer-safe-changes, prose-to-checks skill, item-routing, and extracting-lessons.

content.content:
30	Beyond the portable corpus above, two folders hold the machinery that keeps it fed and tidy — Claudinite-internal orchestration, **not** part of the mounted corpus.
31	
32	Before changing the canon itself, read **[consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md)** (provisional) — which propagation channel a change travels, how to migrate copied artifacts like stubs through the conformance checks, and the other practices we currently believe keep a canon change from hurting consuming repos. And before *adding* a feature, read **[extending.md](extending.md)** — the core/pack boundary (what's engine vs. pack-contributed content) and where each kind of new feature goes.
33	
34	The **growth lifecycle** — how a lesson is learned in a consuming project, lifted into the canon when it's portable, and pruned back out once the canon owns it — is **fully pack-based**, three independent stages split across two packs by who declares them (no folder-access graph, no bespoke orchestrator step):
35	
36	- The growth pack — the **member-side** stages, on every repo declaring the pack: **extract** (captures the last 24h of bugs/PRs/commits into the project's **own** docs, committing directly to its `main`), **dedup** (prunes local items the canon covers, keeping items the canon states too generally; opens a PR), and the weekly **pack discovery** pipeline.
37	- The curation pack — the **home-only** pack, declared solely by the canon home repo, so its tasks run central-once by declaration cardinality: **promote** (reads the changed members' local docs, **generalizes** the portable lessons, routes each to the right canon home, and opens a PR against Claudinite's `main` — the sole judgment gate before shared canon; it replaced the old cross-repo handoff, Action + PAT + labelled issue, which is gone) with its shared **item-routing** method, and the weekly **prose-mining sweep** task.
38	
39	The mounted corpus itself is **`packs/`** (each `packs/<name>/` bundling a pack's prose `RULES.md` and its check modules, discovered structurally by [packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs) and activated by declaration) and **`skills/`** (activity-scoped procedures — catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md)). `checks/` holds only the **engine** that runs the packs' checks — the dependency-free runner, its lib, the Stop hook and PreToolUse guard, and their tests. Usage and configuration → [checks/README.md](checks/README.md); design → [checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md); the per-rule audit → [docs/conversion-inventory.md](docs/conversion-inventory.md).
40	
41	`migrations/` holds the **baseline migrations** mechanism — declared, self-retiring path relocations, one record per in-flight canon rename (a renamed or relocated artifact consumers hold their own copy of) that supplies the read-side resolver, the write-side rename, and the fleet telemetry that auto-retires it once every consumer has moved. Named for when it runs: baselining applies and retires each. See [migrations/README.md](migrations/README.md).
42	
43	`routines/` holds the scheduled jobs:
44	
45	- [routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md](routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md) — **the single scheduled entry point**, run daily from the fleet-**enforcer** repo (one that declares the enforcer pack). It runs the core planner over the repos it can reach (the home repo included, planned last), reads the emitted units, and runs each covered repo's due `run_daily` units at their `smarts` tier — no repo or unit can stop the others. Schedule **this**, nothing else.
46	- [routines/fleet/plan.mjs](routines/fleet/plan.mjs) — the **planner**: pack-agnostic core code that goes over the repos it can reach and, for each covered member, assembles its declared packs' `run_daily` tasks, runs each gate in code, and emits the day's work plan. It depends on no single pack — an enforcer pack's coverage census is a separate, isolated concern, never the source of the plan.
47	- `check-fleet-coverage.mjs` — the enforcer pack's **census** (the cross-repo reach the pack adds): with an account-spanning PAT it enumerates the owner's repos, classifies coverage, and converges one adoption issue per uncovered repo. It is **coverage, not planning** — it does not build the work plan, and it carries **no migration logic** (baseline-migration application and retirement are the migrations flow's own standalone passes, `migrations/fleet-apply.mjs` + `migrations/fleet-retire.mjs`). Dispatched via that repo's `workflow_dispatch`-only coverage workflow (materialized from the pack's stub); which repos to cover and the exclude list come from its pack entry's `config`.
48	- [routines/fleet/](routines/fleet/DESIGN.md) — the **run_daily engine** (the code that decides each repo's due tasks from its active packs) and the scheduling contract. **Baselining** is now the baseline pack's daily task; **growth** rides the member-side pack and the home-side pack; the PR/branch/issue sweep is the repo-tidy pack.
49	
50	**Where things land:** **extract** commits directly to each project's `main` (it writes only local docs), while **promote** (the canon gate) and **dedup** each open a PR for the owner to approve. Every stage reads only what's already merged: a lesson extracted tonight is promoted the next night (the extract commit trips the next night's signals), and promote's PR means promote → dedup waits an approval cycle — the dominant latency either way. An owner-requested, in-session retrospective still delivers a PR.
51	
52	## The submodule future (for consumers)
53	
54	The vendored `shared/` root deliberately mirrors this repo's layout so that mounting Claudinite
55	as a **git submodule at `.claudinite/shared/`** — once sessions run where a cross-repo git
56	credential exists — is a drop-in upgrade that changes no wiring. Details in
57	[mount/DESIGN.md](mount/DESIGN.md).
58

1	# Making changes to Claudinite without hurting consumers
2	
3	> **Status: provisional — beliefs, not law.** Every entry here says what we're *currently* doing
4	> and *why we think* it helps. When an entry blocks a better change, challenge the entry rather
5	> than contorting the change; being cited in this guide is not, by itself, a reason something
6	> must stay. We'll solidify entries once they've earned it.
7	
8	Claudinite is mounted by every consuming repo, so a change here lands in many places, on those
9	repos' schedules, usually with no human watching. This guide collects what we currently believe
10	about making such changes safely. It is a maintainer document for sessions working *in* this
11	repo; it is not part of the mounted corpus.
12	
13	## First, know which channel the change travels
14	
15	We think the most useful first question is *how* a change reaches consumers, because each
16	channel has a different blast radius and a different rollback story:
17	
18	- **Canon reusable workflows and composite actions** (`.github/workflows/`, `.github/actions/`)
19	  — consumers reference them `@main`, so a merge here reaches every consumer's **next run
20	  instantly**, unattended, with no pin to hide behind. This is the highest-blast-radius channel.
21	- **Checks and pack prose** (`packs/`) — executed/loaded from the mount; tarball-sync consumers
22	  pick them up at their next session start, submodule consumers at their next pointer bump. A
23	  wrong *blocking* check fails consumers' Stop hook and CI — visible and recoverable, but
24	  fleet-wide noise, so treat check changes as production changes.
25	- **Copied artifacts** (pack `stubs/`, seeded configs) — these do **not** propagate at all. Each
26	  consumer holds its own copy, which drifts silently unless a check enforces its shape. Changing
27	  the source file alone only affects repos bootstrapped *after* the change.
28	- **Bootstrap wiring** (the committed artifacts [bootstrap.md](bootstrap.md) seeds into each
29	  consumer: the tracked sync hook, `settings.json` hook registrations, gitignore rules, the
30	  pack declaration) — propagates through the **nightly baselining**, which
31	  re-runs the idempotent bootstrap on every member and commits drift **directly to its default
32	  branch**, no PR. Fleet-wide but lagging one nightly; the rollback story is the same channel
33	  (fix the canon, the next nightly re-heals). The discipline: a change here is written as
34	  bootstrap steps that **converge from every layout in the wild** — fresh, current, legacy,
35	  half-migrated — not just from the latest.
36	- **Skills** (`skills/`) — surfaced on demand from the mount; same propagation as prose, but
37	  only sessions doing the matching activity ever read them.
38	
39	## Migrating a copied artifact (stubs and the like)
40	
41	Because copies won't move on their own, we currently migrate them through the conformance
42	checks, in one Claudinite-only change:
43	
44	1. Change the source artifact in the pack (the stub under `packs/<pack>/stubs/`).
45	2. Make sure the pack's conformance check enforces the properties that changed, with a `fix`
46	   that says how to align (usually "re-copy the stub"). A consumer's own next session or CI run
47	   then raises a blocking finding and performs the migration in that repo — no per-repo PR from
48	   here.
49	3. Keep the pack's `detect` fingerprint **tolerant of the legacy shape** while copies migrate
50	   (e.g. a `LEGACY_*` list beside the current constant), so a fresh `--init` still recognizes a
51	   not-yet-migrated repo and seeds the pack. (The fingerprint only *suspects* a pack — it no
52	   longer drives any drop/keep finding — so a stale shape can't contradict the conformance
53	   check's *fix-the-stub* remedy; the tolerance is just to keep detection working during the
54	   migration window.)
55	
56	Fleet-wide "everyone has migrated" telemetry now exists — declare the rename as a
57	[baseline migration](migrations/README.md), **in the same change that introduces the tolerance**: a
58	tolerance that lands without its record has no signal that will ever retire it, and the gap only
59	resurfaces as a review question later. And not only for renames — any legacy shape a tolerance keeps
60	readable (an old path, a stub's old form, a legacy key *inside* a consumer's config file) is trackable
61	the same way, `legacyPresent` reading whatever expresses the old shape. The fleet-coverage census probes every repo for the legacy
62	shape and **auto-retires** the migration (deletes its record) once none remain, so dropping a fully-applied
63	tolerance is no longer a manual judgment call. While a migration's tolerance still lives inline
64	(readers not yet consulting its `resolvePath`), keep it `retire:'manual'` and drop it deliberately;
65	once every reader is resolver-driven, `retire:'auto'` lets the census drop the record — and with it the
66	tolerance — automatically.
67	
68	## Relocating wiring that consumers reference by path
69	
70	What we learned moving the Method B sync hook from `.claude/hooks/` into `.claudinite/`
71	(#211/#213): the file is only half the change — every *reference* to its old path is itself a
72	consumer-held copy that won't move on its own, and each needs a channel.
73	
74	- **Enumerate the reference holders before touching the file** — `settings.json` hook commands,
75	  other canon scripts, the fleet routine's discovery probe, docs — then grep for the old path
76	  afterward (the text-sweep skill owns the mechanics). Include state that lives
77	  **outside the repo entirely**: a web environment's pasted Setup script is snapshotted at
78	  environment build, where no check, sync, or baselining can ever reach it.
79	- **Never break the channel the migration itself travels through.** Fleet discovery finds
80	  members by the tracked `.claudinite/` signal; when the signal file itself relocated, the
81	  discovery probe had to accept both shapes. A probe that recognizes only the new shape doesn't
82	  make noise like a wrong check — it **silently and permanently orphans** every unmigrated repo,
83	  since discovery is what reaches them to migrate them. Same for the settings registration: a
84	  session between canon merge and nightly migration still runs the old wiring, so the old layout
85	  must keep working until the baselining lands.
86	- **References nothing can reach get tolerance in the artifact instead** — the relocated script
87	  tries the new layout first and falls back to the legacy path, and the existing SessionStart
88	  probe/halt-gate asks for the one human action (re-paste) when a stale out-of-repo copy finally
89	  misfires. Prefer a probe that *detects* staleness over hoping the stale copy keeps working.
90	- **A canon-owned file tracked in consumer git updates on the commit channel, not the sync
91	  channel.** The session sync preserves the consumer's tracked copy over the tarball's newer
92	  one — otherwise every canon edit dirties every consumer's working tree until the nightly
93	  commits it. Clean trees each session; baselining's direct commit is the update path.
94	- **Verify by replaying the migration on a simulated legacy consumer** before merging: build the
95	  old layout in a scratch git repo, run the documented steps verbatim, and assert the tree is
96	  clean both right after the migration and after a subsequent real session sync. This is the
97	  wiring-channel analog of "verify against a real consumer before the nightly does".
98	
99	## A vendored file executes in the consumer's context
100	
101	Canon files that ship into consumers — the whole vendor set (`mount/`, `checks/`, pack machinery
102	and stubs) — run at their *landed* path inside the consumer's repo, not in the canon checkout they
103	were written and tested in. Before changing one, audit every environment assumption against each
104	context it ships to: **git discovers `.git` by walking upward**, so introspection from a vendored
105	copy answers with the *consumer's* repo unless constrained to the expected toplevel (the
106	`--show-toplevel` guard in `mount/apply-vendor.mjs` — an unconstrained `rev-parse HEAD` nearly
107	stamped a consumer sha as canon provenance, #340); relative paths resolve against the vendored
108	location; network access and credentials present where the file was written may be absent where it
109	lands. The co-located header comment at the usage site carries each specific trap; this entry is
110	the class.
111	
112	## Normalize requirements to canonical form at the door
113	
114	When a requirement arrives expressed in someone's local context — a schedule in a local
115	timezone, a path from one repo's layout, a phrasing tied to one project's vocabulary — we
116	currently translate it to its canonical form **once, on the way in**, and record only the
117	result. A "nightly at 3:30 my time" request lands in the canon as a fixed UTC cron, stated in
118	UTC and nothing else. The reasoning: the person who asked can carry the original framing;
119	consumers shouldn't inherit it, and the canon shouldn't have to re-explain a conversion (or its
120	edge cases, like DST) forever. If the origin matters for revisiting the decision, it belongs in
121	the issue/PR that made the change, not in the shipped artifact.
122	
123	## Changing the canon workflows themselves
124	
125	Since `@main` references make these instant and fleet-wide, we currently lean on:
126	
127	- **Additive, backward-compatible edits** — a new input gets a default; an output nobody
128	  consumes yet is free; renames and removals get the two-phase treatment above.
129	- **Verify against a real consumer before the nightly does** — a manual `workflow_dispatch` on
130	  one consuming repo is cheap; the scheduled run exercising a bad merge in every repo at once is
131	  not. The failure reporter turning red runs into issues is the safety net, not the plan.
132

1	---
2	name: prose-to-checks
3	description: Mine the corpus's existing prose (pack RULES.md, skill SKILL.md) for always-testable rules that were never converted to checks, and convert the strongest ones. Use when auditing the corpus for convertible rules, or when the nightly growth routine runs its prose-to-checks sweep.
4	---
5	
6	# Convert existing prose to checks
7	
8	A completeness-critic over Claudinite itself. The growth *promote* stage converts each **new**
9	lesson down the promotion ladder; this pass sweeps the **existing** prose backlog for rules that
10	are always-testable but still live only as prose — and converts them, so the corpus keeps
11	shedding context over time instead of only at the moment a rule is first learned. It runs as the
12	`canon-curation` pack's weekly `prose-to-checks-sweep` task, and on demand.
13	
14	## What to look for — the check-the-world test
15	
16	Read the prose of the packs and skills (`packs/<name>/RULES.md`, `packs/<name>/RELEASE.md`,
17	`skills/<name>/SKILL.md`). For each rule, ask the one question from
18	[checks/DESIGN.md](../../checks/DESIGN.md): **does it constrain a *static signature in the repo
19	artifact* — something a post-hoc scan could observe?**
20	
21	- **Yes → a conversion candidate.** A dangling-reference rule, a filename convention, a workflow
22	  or manifest shape, a "these two files must agree" invariant, a forbidden pattern in code.
23	- **No → leave it.** In-flight process (leaves no artifact — "see the test fail first"),
24	  judgment ("name by scope"), or knowledge whose failure is only visible at runtime (jsdom
25	  diverging from Chrome). These are why the rule is prose; don't force them.
26	
27	The check-the-world rule from DESIGN holds: if a rule is always-testable, it was never really
28	part of the on-demand skill — it belongs in a pack as a check.
29	
30	## How to convert one
31	
32	Follow the promote stage's check-authoring discipline
33	([promote.md](../../.claudinite/local_packs/canon-curation/promote.md) and
34	[item-routing.md](../../.claudinite/local_packs/canon-curation/item-routing.md) own the ladder). For each candidate:
35	
36	1. **Author the check** in the owning pack (`packs/<pack>/<rule>.mjs`, listed in `pack.mjs`) —
37	   the failure message *is* the rule (what / why / fix / `doc:` pointer back to the prose).
38	2. **Write the fixture first and see it fail** — a violating fixture must find, a clean one must
39	   not (the test lives in the owning pack's `packs/<pack>/pack.test.mjs`). A conversion with
40	   no proving fixture doesn't ship.
41	3. **Ship at real severity, fail-fast** — blocking for a defect, advisory only when the rule is
42	   directional by kind.
43	4. **Trim the prose** to rationale — the check owns enforcement now; leaving both pays twice and
44	   springs the drift trap.
45	
46	**Before writing a rule off as un-checkable, try parsing the file's structure instead of grepping
47	its text.** Grep finds the pattern anywhere; parsing finds it in the one spot the rule means —
48	which kills the false alarm. (Example: `Authorization` is only wrong inside a CloudFront policy's
49	*own* header list, not elsewhere in the template.) Parse only as much as you need, and hold the
50	check to the same fixture bar.
51	
52	When even a scoped parser can't make detection confident, **leave the prose and log the
53	candidate** to a tagged conversion-backlog issue rather than shipping a shaky check.
54	
55	## Bounds
56	
57	- **One PR, bounded surface** — the new rule module, its `pack.mjs` line, its fixture, and the
58	  trimmed prose. Don't "improve" unrelated rules while you're in there.
59	- **Never delete a rule you didn't convert** — trimming prose is only for a rule a *landed* check
60	  now enforces.
61	- Run the suite and the sweep green before opening the PR; open it for the owner's approval,
62	  never a direct push to `main`.
63

content.text: Let me read the remaining relevant files: item-routing, extracting-lessons, grow README, and the run_daily scripts.

content.content:
1	# Evaluating and routing a new item
2	
3	The shared method for three decisions every proposed addition to this corpus must pass through: **is it worthy** of inclusion at all, **which mechanism** should carry it, and — only for prose — **where does it go**. Any routine or person that adds an item — the [growth promote stage](promote.md), an owner-requested retrospective pass, a hand edit — applies this same protocol, so the decision-making lives here once instead of being re-derived per caller.
4	
5	This is a Claudinite-internal doc used by the growth lifecycle (see [the canon-curation README](README.md)): it is **not** part of the mounted corpus and consuming repos do not `@import` it.
6	
7	An "item" is one **distilled, portable rule** — not a transcript, not a narration of what happened. A candidate still phrased as "here's what we did" has not been distilled yet and is below the bar on form alone, before any of the tests below. This doc picks up once a distilled rule exists and decides its fate.
8	
9	## Worthiness — does it earn a place at all
10	
11	The corpus is read read-only by every consuming repo, so a **duplicative** item (already covered) or an **undistilled** one (a story or a truism, not a rule) pollutes shared canon and costs every future reader. The bar guards against *those* — it is **not** a bar on *scope*: an item useful to only one project today can still earn a place (see Portable). Admit an item only when **all** hold:
12	
13	- **Distilled.** It is a single tight rule in the imperative, not a story, a log, or a restatement of a generic truism.
14	- **Portable — would a *future* project benefit?** Not "useful to every project today." An item can help only **one** project right now and still belong here, so long as another project — **especially one with a similar tech stack or process** — would benefit when it arrives. Being specific to a technology or a process is **not** disqualifying: generalize it and route it to that technology's or practice's home. What disqualifies is leaning on *this* project's non-transferable particulars — a product requirement, or a one-off incident in how it happened to call some API. The test, a genuinely hard one: *if another project with a similar tech stack came along, would it benefit from this?* Yes → portable; only-ever-this-product → local.
15	- **Durable and reusable.** It will still apply on a future, unseen task — not a one-off, not a situational detail, not something already implied by an existing rule.
16	- **Not already covered.** The insight does not already exist **anywhere** in the corpus, even worded differently or owned by a different file. This is checked against the *entire* corpus, not just the file it would land in — see [Dedupe against the whole corpus](#dedupe-against-the-whole-corpus).
17	
18	Reject on any miss — but the misses that matter are *undistilled*, *already-covered*, or *genuinely one-off*, not merely *narrow*. When you're torn over a real, non-duplicate rule, lean toward **keeping** it — the goal is a broad library. When you're torn over whether something is a duplicate, or a rule at all, leave it out. The failure to avoid is padding the canon with restatements of what's already there; admitting a narrow-but-reusable rule is not that failure.
19	
20	### Dedupe against the whole corpus
21	
22	Before admitting anything, read across the corpus, not only the file you expect to own the item. The same insight is frequently already present under a different heading or in an adjacent group. If it sharpens an **existing** rule, fold it in with a minimal edit rather than adding a second bullet that says nearly the same thing — and never weaken or restate what is already there in the process.
23	
24	## Mechanism triage — prose is the fallback, not the default
25	
26	A worthy item next descends the **promotion ladder** ([../../../checks/DESIGN.md](../../../checks/DESIGN.md) owns it): platform setting (always paired with its verifying check) → PreToolUse hook → conformance check → skill → prose. The *first* rung that can carry the item wins — a rule about repo state becomes a check whose failure message *is* the lesson; a bad action to prevent becomes a guard; a procedure or knowledge with a nameable trigger becomes (or joins) a skill. Only an item none of those can carry — judgment, in-flight behavior, signature-less knowledge — proceeds to the file routing below, and its landing **names that reason** in the promote log so conversion rates stay auditable.
27	
28	**"No universal signature" alone never routes an item to prose.** A rule converts to a check when a *narrow, file-scoped* signature covers the **grounded cases** — the concrete shapes the rule was distilled from — even though no signature covers every conceivable violation. Gate it relevance-first so it stays inert off-target, and document the genuinely signature-less residue in the rule's own comments as out of scope. The prose rung is only for an item whose grounded cases *themselves* have no reliable signature (pure judgment, runtime/in-flight behavior).
29	
30	## Routing — picking the right file (rung-5 items only)
31	
32	Routing is robust when it keys on **stable file groups**, not on the current roster of individual files (which grows over time). The corpus is organized into a few groups, each with its own selection rule:
33	
34	1. **The practice group** — durable, project-agnostic practices, each file a self-contained, reusable unit of know-how the agent applies whenever its subject comes up (engineering, agentic, git/GitHub, testing, investigation, discipline, architecture), partitioned **by kind of practice** with each file owning one recurring subject cluster. This group spans **two folders that differ only by *when* the rule loads**: `always/` for a rule that applies to essentially every task (the index `@`-imports it, so it's force-loaded every session), and `tasks/` for a subject-gated rule read on demand when its trigger fires. Route in two steps: first match the item's *kind* to the file whose cluster already owns that subject; then keep it in `always/` only if it genuinely isn't tied to one kind of task (the rare case — `always/` stays small), otherwise it belongs in `tasks/`. Read the group's own index (the root `CLAUDE.md`) to find the current owner for a kind — never from memory, since the set of clusters can grow.
35	2. **The per-user group** — interaction preferences and trigger phrases, **one file per person**, named for that person's identity. Route an item of this kind to the file for the user it belongs to; it never goes in the practice group.
36	3. **The per-technology group** — practices specific to one technology, **one file per technology**. Route a technology-specific item to that technology's file. When **no pack owns that technology** (not even a stub), the item doesn't land in a loose doc — it **mints a fingerprinted stub pack** (a `packs/<tech>/`, delivered in its own PR), so the next project on that stack self-declares it; [promote](promote.md)'s stub-minting floor and [the generate-project-instructions skill](../../../skills/generate-project-instructions/SKILL.md) own the mechanics.
37	
38	Pick **exactly one** owner. If an item plausibly fits two files, choose the single best one — never split one item across files, and never duplicate it into both. The group decides the *axis* (kind, person, or technology); the file within the group is then the one whose existing scope the item falls squarely inside.
39	
40	### When nothing fits
41	
42	An item that matches **no** existing file is **not** a reject signal by itself — a genuinely new category of lesson is meant to be learnable, so a worthy item that no file owns earns its own file. Resolve it in this order:
43	
44	- **Route into an existing owner** when one genuinely fits — the first choice. If the item belongs to a file's cluster and merely sits slightly off-center, a small stretch toward that owner beats spinning up a new file. A new file is for when no existing file's scope genuinely fits, **not** a way to dodge folding into an existing owner.
45	- **Open a new file** when the item clears the [Worthiness](#worthiness--does-it-earn-a-place-at-all) bar in full — distilled, portable, durable and reusable, and not already covered anywhere in the corpus — **and** no existing file's scope genuinely fits it. That is the whole bar: nothing more is required. Do **not** also demand that you expect the cluster to recur, that forcing the item into the closest file would *distort* it, or default to rejecting when the new-file call is a close one — those extra hurdles are dropped. Creating a file obliges registering it where the corpus is indexed and in any routing table, in the **same** change, so it never lands orphaned (see [Keep the write surface bounded](#keep-the-write-surface-bounded)).
46	
47	The loosening above is **only** to the new-cluster hurdle, never to the per-item bar. An item that misses [Worthiness](#worthiness--does-it-earn-a-place-at-all) — a one-off, non-portable or project-specific, a restatement of a generic truism, or already covered anywhere in the corpus — is rejected whether or not a file already exists for it; for the per-item admission decision, the default-to-reject-when-unsure tiebreak still stands.
48	
49	## Keep the write surface bounded
50	
51	Once worthiness and routing are settled, the edit touches **only** the single owning file: add or sharpen one terse rule in that file's existing voice and format, and change nothing else. Do not "improve" unrelated rules while you are in there. The sole exception is the new-file path above, whose surface is itself bounded to the new file plus its index and routing-table registration.
52

1	# Extracting lessons from a working session
2	
3	> **Scope: a single agent ↔ user conversation.** This doc is the **method** for mining one
4	> working session's *dialogue and the actions taken in it* for improvements. It is **not** the
5	> growth extract ([extract.md](extract.md)) that mines **issues, PRs, and commits** over repo
6	> history — that routine never sees a conversation. Its two callers: the
7	> [conversation-extract daily task](conversation-extract.md), which applies it to **captured**
8	> conversation logs — where the friction signals below (a clarifying round-trip, a backtrack, a
9	> wait you sat through) survive with their timestamps — and an owner's occasional plain-words
10	> ask for a retrospective over the live session.
11	
12	Portable guidance for mining an agent ↔ user session — a Claude Code web session, a CLI run, a
13	captured conversation log — for durable improvements: read the conversation and convert its
14	friction into changes that make the **next** run faster, clearer, and less dependent on human
15	feedback.
16	
17	Run the reflection on a **capable model.** Extraction is mechanical, but deciding *what clears
18	the bar* is a judgment call a weaker model fails silently — it ships a plausible-but-wrong
19	"lesson" where a capable model correctly bails (see
20	[the unattended-agents skill](../../skills/unattended-agents/SKILL.md), "Match the agent model
21	to the judgment it must make"). The terse rules that *govern* the pass — run it, plus the
22	separate efficiency analysis — also live there; this doc is the how-to.
23	
24	## The goal
25	
26	Every pass optimizes for one outcome: **the next run needs less.** Less clarification, less
27	rework, less waiting, less human steering. Read the session not as "what did we accomplish" but
28	as "where did this cost more than it should have, and what durable change removes that cost
29	next time." If a pass can't name a concrete next-time saving, it found nothing — and that's
30	fine (see [No new lessons](#no-new-lessons-is-a-valid--and-common--result)).
31	
32	## When it runs
33	
34	Routinely, in the [conversation-extract daily task](conversation-extract.md) over each captured
35	log (a fresh pass within a day of capture, a final hindsight pass before retention deletes the
36	log) — the standard path. If an owner asks in plain words for a retrospective over the live
37	session, apply the same method and deliver the capture as a branch + PR for review, never
38	straight to `main` (the nightly commits direct per its own doc; a live pass doesn't). **Never
39	reflect-and-edit unprompted in the middle of a task** — it interrupts the work; the owner
40	decides when a session is done enough to mine.
41	
42	## What to look for — the friction signals
43	
44	Read the conversation end to end and hunt for these signatures. Each marks a place the loop
45	cost more than necessary, and each converts to a specific durable fix.
46	
47	### 1. Misunderstandings between agent and user
48	
49	The signs: a clarifying-question round-trip; the user correcting an assumption ("no, I
50	meant…"); the agent building the wrong thing and reworking it; the user restating the same
51	request; an `AskUserQuestion` whose answer was, in hindsight, the obvious default.
52	
53	**Convert to:** encode the resolved understanding so the next agent never has to ask. Every
54	question the user answered is a candidate **default**; every correction is a candidate
55	**convention or glossary entry**; a recurring wrong assumption is a candidate **confirm-first
56	checkpoint placed earlier**, or a clearer doc the agent reads cold and gets right the first
57	time. The test of a good fix: next time the agent proceeds correctly **without the round-trip**.
58	
59	### 2. Suboptimal agent actions
60	
61	The signs: the agent took an approach then backtracked; did redundant or repeated work; re-ran
62	a command that had already failed for a knowable reason; reached for a heavy tool where a light
63	one fit; reinvented something the codebase already provided; re-read a file it had just written
64	to "verify"; missed an existing convention and had it corrected in review.
65	
66	**Convert to:** a rule pointing at the right approach or tool, a pointer to the existing
67	helper/convention so it's found first, or a **footgun note placed where it'll be read.** Place
68	it by where the trap is hit, not in a central bucket: a gotcha from **misusing a specific API,
69	class, or library goes as a comment right at the usage site** — next to the call itself, where
70	the next editor of that line sees it — never collected into a centralized gotchas list.
71	Co-locate it in a file's own header comment when the trap spans the whole file and you'd only
72	hit it *while editing that file*. Reserve a central gotchas doc for what no single usage site
73	owns: a trap you could hit *without* reading the relevant file (a mistake of omission), or a
74	cross-cutting invariant. The test: next time the agent takes the good path first, not after a
75	detour.
76	
77	### 3. Long wall-time waits — and measure them
78	
79	The signs: independent operations run **serially** that had no dependency and could have been
80	parallel; a padded fixed `sleep` that over- or under-shot; a process **waited out after its
81	result was already in hand**; blind long polling; re-running a check (e.g. CI) that was already
82	green.
83	
84	**Measure, don't hand-wave.** Quantify the wait with real wall-clock numbers and separate the
85	kinds of time — compute (a compile, a test/render run) vs. genuine **idle waiting** (sleeps,
86	polling, a finished process not yet killed). A captured log carries per-entry timestamps and
87	per-message token usage, so the numbers are computable, not estimated. A fix is only credible
88	against numbers; name the **single highest-leverage change** that shortens it, or conclude it
89	was already optimal.
90	
91	**Convert to:** a batch/parallelize rule, a poll-with-rolling-backoff instead of a blind sleep,
92	kill-the-process-once-its-output-is-in-hand, or merge-on-an-already-green-check. (These are the
93	subject of the standing **efficiency analysis** in
94	[the unattended-agents skill](../../skills/unattended-agents/SKILL.md) — fold a wall-time
95	finding into that frame rather than re-deriving it.) The test: same result, less wall clock, no
96	loss of quality.
97	
98	## Encode the questions the user keeps asking
99	
100	The single highest-leverage move for **less human feedback**: the questions a user asks
101	*repeatedly* are a checklist the agent should run **itself.** If the user keeps asking "how
102	many processes ran — could it be fewer?", "what took longest?", "did you kill it the moment its
103	work was done?", stop waiting to be asked — fold those exact questions into a **standing
104	self-check the agent answers proactively** at the end of the relevant work, and close with a
105	terse verdict (a concrete change, or an explicit "already optimal"). Each recurring question
106	converted this way is one the user never has to ask again — and it's where a *measured*
107	retrospective comes from: the user's recurring efficiency questions, answered pre-emptively,
108	with numbers.
109	
110	## From signal to lesson — the bar
111	
112	A friction signal is only worth writing down when the fix is **durable, reusable, and
113	generalizable beyond this one session.** Before adding anything:
114	
115	- **Dedupe ruthlessly** against the existing packs — a lesson already covered (even worded
116	  differently, even in another pack, even enforced by a check) is not a new lesson.
117	- **Route to the local pack that owns it, and prefer the strongest mechanism** — the **local
118	  promotion ladder**, at the project's own level. A project's capture surface is its **local
119	  packs** (`.claudinite/local_packs/<pack>/`): pick the pack whose territory the lesson belongs
120	  to, then a deterministic rule becomes a **check** in that pack's `rules` (its failure message
121	  *is* the lesson; ship it with a red-first fixture), an activity-scoped procedure becomes a
122	  **pack skill**, and only what neither can carry lands as terse **prose** in the pack's
123	  `RULES.md`. Write more checks and less prose — a check relieves every session's context
124	  completely where prose only relocates it. A gotcha tied to one call site still goes as a
125	  comment at that site (above), not into any pack. When no pack's scope fits and the lesson
126	  warrants segregation, a new local pack is a self-contained directory drop.
127	- **Promote what's portable.** A lesson true for projects beyond this one belongs in shared,
128	  cross-project canon, not stuck in one repo's local packs — the growth lifecycle's promote
129	  stage lifts it there centrally; capture it well locally and let promote generalize whatever
130	  travels.
131	- **One-offs don't qualify.** A situational detail, a restatement of a generic truism, or
132	  something already implied by an existing rule is below the bar.
133	
134	## "No new lessons" is a valid — and common — result
135	
136	Most sessions yield nothing durable, and that's the expected outcome. Say so and write nothing
137	rather than padding the docs to look productive: a spurious or duplicative "lesson" pollutes
138	the canon and costs every future reader. **Default to no edit when unsure** — the bar is a
139	genuinely new, reusable insight, not a diary of what happened.
140

1	# grow_with_claudinite
2	
3	Opt into the **growth lifecycle** — declaring this pack enrolls a repo in contributing its hard-won
4	lessons up to the shared Claudinite canon, and in pruning its local packs once the canon owns them.
5	Seeded by default (`--init` + the one-time `grow-with-claudinite-seed` baseline migration for the
6	existing fleet), and **opt-out by removal**: baselining never re-adds it.
7	
8	This pack carries the **member-side** stages of the growth lifecycle: capturing a repo's own
9	lessons into its local packs, and pruning them once the shared canon covers them. The central
10	**promote** stage — which lifts portable lessons up into the shared canon — is a home-only duty
11	that runs centrally, not a member-side task, so it lives outside this pack.
12	
13	Its scheduled work is three `run_daily` tasks, all ordinary, independent planner units:
14	
15	| Task | Runs when | Where it lands |
16	|---|---|---|
17	| `growth-extract-new-instructions` ([extract.md](extract.md)) | the project changed in the window | the project's own local packs, on `main` (directly) |
18	| `growth-dedup-local-instructions` ([dedup.md](dedup.md)) | canon changed, or the project's local packs changed (or weekly) | a PR against the project's `main` |
19	| `growth-discover-packs` ([discover-packs.md](discover-packs.md)) | the member's weekly full sweep | one PR per authored pack, against Claudinite's canon |
20	
21	A member that wants the local stages without contributing lessons upstream **opts out of
22	promotion** on its own entry — `{ "id": "grow_with_claudinite", "config": { "promote": false } }`
23	— and the central promote stage skips it (absent or `true` = participate).
24	
25	## The conversation lifecycle — capture at merge, extract in a daily task, retention
26	
27	The pack also owns **extraction from working sessions** — the conversation-side sibling of the
28	issues/PRs/commits extract above, replacing the old in-session post-merge lessons pass. The two
29	halves split by what each needs: **capture** needs the live session transcript, so it runs
30	in-session at merge; **extraction** only reads the already-pushed logs, so it is an ordinary
31	fleet `run_daily` task with `growth-extract`'s access model — the logs branch is *in the repo*,
32	so reading it, committing lessons to local packs, and pruning are plain local git on the working
33	tree; only posting the dialogue on the issue uses the GitHub MCP tools.
34	
35	1. **Capture — a step in the merge-to-main skill** (in-session, where the transcript lives).
36	   Right after a merge lands:
37	   `node .claudinite/shared/packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>`
38	   (in the canon repo itself: `node packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>`).
39	   Deterministic, seconds; it bundles the session transcript (sidechains inline, timestamp
40	   order), **scrubs enumeration-first** (every value the environment holds — `process.env`
41	   minus a short named allowlist of structural values, plus known credential stores — is
42	   redacted wherever it appears, with credential-shape patterns as the backstop; a secret the
43	   session itself transformed is beyond any static scrub, and push protection is the last
44	   net), and pushes one file per merge onto the orphan **`conversation-logs`** branch:
45	   `<stamp>--issue-<n>--<session>.jsonl`, commits marked `[skip ci]`. **Delta-aware:** a
46	   session that merges twice pushes a second, disjoint file for the second merge's issue.
47	   The branch is a **work queue, not an archive** — never merged; tips are cheap in shallow
48	   session clones and retention keeps them bounded.
49	2. **Fresh pass — the [conversation-extract](conversation-extract.md) run_daily task** (gate: the
50	   day after a real merge — `substantiveChange` — plus the weekly full sweep; local git on the
51	   repo's working tree, MCP only for the issue comment). It applies
52	   [extracting-lessons.md](extracting-lessons.md) (the method — friction signals and the
53	   measured efficiency analysis, computable from the log's timestamps and token usage), routes
54	   keepers into the member's local packs, and posts the rendered dialogue
55	   ([render-dialogue.mjs](render-dialogue.mjs)) on the worked issue for each rule that landed —
56	   **extraction is the only path to permanence**: a log that yields no rule gets no comment,
57	   and its conversation is gone once retention deletes it (a deliberate owner call).
58	3. **Final pass and deletion** — once a log ages past `config.retention_days`, the same task
59	   re-reads it with ~a week of hindsight (the rethink window), then deletes it (the weekly full
60	   sweep guarantees the prune runs even on a repo gone quiet). Every log gets exactly two
61	   judgment passes. **Unset retention = the prune deletes nothing** (capture-only, fail-safe).
62	
63	Adoption asks only for the `retention_days` value (10 recommended) — nothing to schedule, since
64	extraction rides the fleet's one daily run like the other growth tasks.
65	
66	## Rules
67	
68	| Rule | Kind | What |
69	|---|---|---|
70	| `growth-config` | hardcoded ([config-check.mjs](config-check.mjs)) | entry config shape valid |
71	
72	**Pack discovery** ([discover-packs.md](discover-packs.md)) is an ordinary `run_daily` task — the
73	planner picks it up per member on its weekly full sweep. For the member it's handed it runs the
74	whole pipeline: manifest the stack, suggest a pack for each technology no pack yet owns (on first
75	sight), populate it with rules and checks distilled from that member's real usage, and open one
76	canon PR per pack. Like every worker it **executes centrally** (home session, fleet token) — that's
77	how it writes the canon — but it's scheduled the regular way; over a week the staggered full sweep
78	covers the fleet, and the shelf + open-PR check keeps first sight from double-authoring.
79	
80	## Identifying a project's capture surface: its local packs (the same way in every stage)
81	
82	Every growth stage operates on a project's **local packs** — the tracked packs a repo keeps under
83	`.claudinite/local_packs/<pack>/` (prose in `RULES.md`, checks in the pack's `rules`, activity
84	procedures as the pack's skills, `run_daily` tasks). That subtree **is** the project's own content;
85	the rest of `.claudinite/` is the **read-only mounted canon** and is never a capture, prune, or
86	promote target. So "a project's local packs" means precisely *everything under
87	`.claudinite/local_packs/`, and nothing else under `.claudinite/`*. This is the normalized capture
88	surface — a structural set the stages read the same way, not a `CLAUDE.md`-graph walk over stray
89	Markdown (a repo with no local packs yet simply has nothing to extract, dedup, or promote here; a
90	project adopts the structure via the `generate-project-instructions` skill).
91	
92	Prefer the strongest mechanism the lesson allows — the **local promotion ladder**, applied at the
93	project's own level: a deterministic rule becomes a **check** in the owning pack's `rules` (its
94	failure message carries the lesson), an activity-scoped procedure becomes a **pack skill**, and only
95	what neither can carry lands as **prose** in a pack's `RULES.md`. A check relieves every session's
96	context completely where prose only relocates it, so capture writes *more checks and less prose*.
97	
98	The stages differ only in *how they read that set*, never in *which set it is*: extract and dedup
99	run against the member repo and read the local packs from the working tree; promote runs centrally
100	and reads the same subtree over the GitHub API (get-file-contents under `.claudinite/local_packs/`).
101	Extract writes into it, promote reads from it, dedup prunes within it — all against the identical,
102	`.claudinite/local_packs/`-rooted set.
103
